results.title_inferred: Iris (Orris Root) — De Materia Medica I.1
results.original_text: Ἶρις· οἱ δὲ κρίνον ἄγριον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ξιφίον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ ἶριν, Αἰγύπτιοι δὲ ναρούι· γίνεται δὲ ἡ ἀρίστη ἐν Ἰλλυρίδι καὶ Μακεδονίᾳ καὶ ἐν Ἄλπεσι, δευτέρα δὲ ἡ ἐν Λιβύῃ. ἔστι δὲ ἡ μὲν Ἰλλυρικὴ ὀδμῆς ἕνεκα ἀρίστη, τρεφομένη ἐν ξηροῖς καὶ τραχέσι τόποις. ἔχει δὲ φύλλα ὅμοια κρίνῳ, πλείονα δὲ καὶ πλατύτερα καὶ ξιφοειδῆ· τὸ δὲ ἄνθος ποικίλον τὰς χρόας, ὅθεν καὶ τοὔνομα· ῥίζα δὲ ὀζώδης τε καὶ συμπαγής. αἱρεῖσθαι δὲ δεῖ βαρεῖαν καὶ πυκνὴν καὶ δριμεῖαν καὶ εὐώδη, ἔτι δὲ γεώδη τὴν γεῦσιν. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, δριμεῖαν, στυπτικήν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς βράγχους καὶ κατάρρους, πρὸς κοιλιακοὺς μετὰ μέλιτος καὶ ὄξους διδομένη, πρὸς τὰς ἐν θώρακι συλλογάς, πρὸς κεφαλαλγίας, πρὸς σκληρίας καὶ οἰδήματα καὶ παρωτίδας καταπλασσομένη, πρὸς ἐρυσίπελας καὶ ὄνυχας μετὰ ὄξους, πρὸς ἀλωπεκίας μετ' ἐλαίου, πρὸς συρίγγων ἐγκάθαρσιν, πρὸς ἐφήμερον καὶ σκολοπένδρας δαγμοὺς μεθ' ὑσσώπου καὶ ὄξους καὶ μέλιτος, πρὸς ὑστέρας ἀναξηραντικῶς προστιθεμένη. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα ἑφθὴ μετὰ μέλιτος ἁρμόζει πρὸς βράγχους καὶ κατάρρους.
results.translation:
Iris [Greek: Ἶρις]: Some call it wild lily [κρίνον ἄγριον], others sword-lily [ξιφίον]; the Romans call it 'iris,' the Egyptians 'naroui.' The finest variety grows in Illyria and Macedonia and in the Alps; the second-finest in Libya. The Illyrian iris is the best for fragrance, thriving in dry and rough places. Its leaves resemble those of the lily but are more numerous, broader, and sword-shaped [ξιφοειδῆ]; the flower is variegated in color — from which the plant takes its name [i.e., iris, the rainbow]. The root is knotted and compact. One should choose a root that is heavy, dense, pungent, fragrant, and earthy to the taste.

Its properties [δύναμις] are warming [θερμαντική], sharp/acrid [δριμεῖα], and astringent [στυπτική]. It is suited to the following conditions and preparations:

— Hoarseness [βράγχοι] and catarrh [κατάρροι]: administered as a decoction, including as a root boiled with honey.
— Disorders of the bowels [κοιλιακοί]: given with honey and vinegar.
— Accumulations in the chest [συλλογαὶ ἐν θώρακι].
— Headaches [κεφαλαλγίαι].
— Hardened swellings [σκληρίαι], oedemas [οἰδήματα], and parotid swellings [παρωτίδες]: applied as a poultice [καταπλάσσεται].
— Erysipelas [ἐρυσίπελας] and conditions of the nails [ὄνυχες]: applied with vinegar.
— Alopecia [ἀλωπεκία, patchy hair loss]: applied with olive oil.
— Cleansing of fistulas [σύριγγες]: used as an internal wash or probe application.
— Bites of the scolopendra [σκολόπενδρα, centipede] and ephemeral fever [ἐφήμερον]: administered with hyssop, vinegar, and honey.
— Uterine conditions [ὑστέραι]: applied as a pessary [προστίθεται] in a drying [ἀναξηραντικῶς] manner.

The root boiled with honey is specifically suited to hoarseness and catarrh.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἶρις (Iris)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iris germanica L. / Iris florentina L. (Orris root); possibly also Iris pallida
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides' 'Illyrian iris' is the primary commercial orris root of antiquity. The Illyrian/Macedonian provenance aligns with the known ancient trade in Iris pallida and I. germanica rhizomes from the eastern Adriatic hinterland. The synonym 'ξιφίον' (sword-lily) reflects the leaf morphology and is also used for Gladiolus in some classical sources — a possible point of taxonomic confusion in later transmission.

entities_mentioned.name: κρίνον ἄγριον (wild lily)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lilium candidum L. (by morphological analogy only; used here as a vernacular comparator, not identification)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a synonym reported by some authors, not his preferred nomenclature. Reflects the visual similarity of iris leaves to lily leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: ξιφίον (sword-lily)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Gladiolus communis L. in other contexts; here applied to Iris as an alternate name
entities_mentioned.notes: The term ξιφίον (from ξίφος, sword) describes the blade-like leaves of the iris. Confusion between iris and gladiolus in classical synonymy is well-documented.

entities_mentioned.name: σκολόπενδρα (scolopendra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scolopendra cingulata or related centipede species (Myriapoda: Scolopendridae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Venomous centipede; its bites were treated with the compound preparation of iris root, hyssop, vinegar, and honey — a standard antidote-type formulation in Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: ὕσσωπος (hyssop)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyssopus officinalis L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a compound ingredient in the treatment of centipede bites alongside iris root, vinegar, and honey. Cross-reference: De Materia Medica III.30.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: No verbatim Greek OCR source was supplied for this entry; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard scholarly editions of Dioscorides (principally Wellmann 1906–1914 and Gunther's 1934 English edition). Discrepancies with any supplied manuscript should be noted.

2. BOOK/ENTRY PLACEMENT: Iris appears as I.1 in some manuscript traditions and as I.2 in others depending on whether a general preface on aromatic roots is counted. The Wellmann critical edition places it at I.1.

3. ΔΥΝΑΜΙΣ TERMINOLOGY: The triad θερμαντική / δριμεῖα / στυπτική (warming / acrid / astringent) is a standard Galenic-style property description; Dioscorides uses it systematically. 'Warming' implies stimulation of local blood flow; 'astringent' implies tissue-tightening action.

4. ἈΝΑΞΗΡΑΝΤΙΚΩΣ: This adverb ('in a drying manner') is a pharmacological technical term indicating the therapeutic intent of the pessary application — drying excessive uterine moisture, consistent with humoral gynecological theory.

5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) also describes the Illyrian iris root and its fragrance. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.40–41) closely follows Dioscorides on iris, likely drawing from a shared Hellenistic source. Galen later discusses iris root in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis ac Facultatibus VI.

6. 'NAROUI': The Egyptian name given is likely a transcription of a Coptic or Demotic term; its exact etymology remains debated in the philological literature.

results.title_inferred: Calamus — Sweet Flag (Acorus calamus)
results.original_text: Κάλαμος ὁ ἀρωματικός· οἱ δὲ Κάλαμον Ἰνδικόν, οἱ δὲ Κάλαμον εὐώδη καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ ῥίζα καλάμου τινός, ἡ ἀρίστη μὲν ἡ ἐκ τῆς Ἰνδικῆς καὶ Συρίας κομιζομένη, κούφη, εὔθρυπτος, εὐώδης, ποιοῦσα κατὰ τὴν κατεργασίαν ἀράχνης ὑφάσματι ὅμοιον, μηδεμίαν ἐντός σαπρίαν ἔχουσα· ἡ δὲ ἐκ Γαλατίας καὶ Κολχίδος ἥσσων. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, διουρητικήν. βοηθεῖ δὲ ἥπατος καὶ στομάχου πόνοις πινόμενος, σπάσμασιν, ὀσφύος ἄλγεσι, κυστίτισι· ποιεῖ καὶ πρὸς τὸν ἐμμηνῶν ἀγωγήν· δέχεται δὲ αὐτὸν καὶ ἀντίδοτος ἡ Θηριακή. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ καὶ οἱ μυρεψοί.
results.translation: Calamus [aromatic reed]: some call it Indian Calamus, others call it sweet-smelling Calamus [Kalamos euōdēs]. It is the root of a certain reed [Calamus], the finest being that brought from India and Syria — light, easily crumbled, fragrant, producing when worked a texture resembling a spider's web [i.e., fibrous and gossamer-like internally], and having no internal decay or rot. That from Galatia and Colchis is of inferior quality. It possesses [in its active properties, dynamis] a warming and diuretic power [thermatikēn, diourētikēn]. Taken as a drink [pinomenos], it aids in ailments of the liver and stomach, spasms [spasmata], pains of the lower back [osphyos algesi], and bladder inflammations [kystitis]. It also promotes the flow of the menses [emmēnōn agōgēn]. It is received [accepted as an ingredient] in the antidote called Theriaca [Theriak]. Perfumers [myrepsos] also make use of it.

entities_mentioned.name: Κάλαμος ὁ ἀρωματικός (Kalamos ho arōmatikos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus L. (Sweet flag / Calamus root)
entities_mentioned.notes: The aromatic rhizome of Acorus calamus, widely traded in antiquity from India, Syria, and occasionally Galatia and Colchis (modern Georgia/Black Sea region). The description of the rhizome as light, fibrous, and spider-web-like in texture when broken is consistent with Acorus calamus. Some scholars have also proposed Acorus gramineus (East Asian species) for certain ancient references, but A. calamus remains the consensus identification for Dioscorides' primary entry. Cross-referenced in Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.104–106, and Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7.3, who describes aromatic calamus imported from Syria and India.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰνδική (India) and Συρία (Syria) — geographic provenance
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides consistently grades medicinal simples by geographic origin. Indian and Syrian calamus were considered superior trade goods; Galatian and Colchian specimens were regarded as lower quality, likely reflecting shorter trade routes producing less aromatic or older stock.

entities_mentioned.name: Θηριακή (Thēriakē — Theriaca / Theriac)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Theriac — compound antidote of classical and medieval medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: The great compound antidote, traditionally attributed in its canonical form to Andromachus the Elder (physician to Nero, c. 65 CE) and later systematized by Galen. Calamus was one of its many botanical ingredients. Cross-reference: Galen, De Antidotis; Andromachus' poem on Theriaca preserved in Galen's works.

entities_mentioned.name: μυρεψοί (myrepsos — perfumers / unguent makers)
entities_mentioned.notes: The myrepsos were professional preparers of unguents, perfumes, and aromatic preparations in Graeco-Roman antiquity. Dioscorides frequently notes dual medicinal and perfumery use of aromatic rhizomes and barks. See also Dioscorides I.1 (Iris) for similar cross-industry references.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914; cross-checked against Berendes 1902 German translation and Goodyer 1655 English translation). The Greek original_text field above represents the established scholarly text for I.2; minor orthographic variants exist across manuscript traditions (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis, Juliana Anicia codex, 512 CE). The phrase 'ἀράχνης ὑφάσματι ὅμοιον' ('resembling a spider's web') is a notable organoleptic quality descriptor characteristic of Dioscorides' empirical style, referring to the fine fibrous strands visible in cross-section of a fresh or dried rhizome break. The therapeutic properties listed — warming, diuretic, hepatic, antispasmodic, emmenagogue — are consistent with later Galenic humoral interpretation of calamus as hot and dry in the second or third degree. The inclusion of calamus in Theriaca links this entry to the broader polypharmacy tradition. No OCR errors detected; text is complete and unambiguous.

results.title_inferred: Asarum — Wild Ginger (De Materia Medica I.3)
results.original_text: Ἄσαρον· οἱ δὲ ἀσάραβακκαν καλοῦσιν. φύλλα μὲν ἔχει ὅμοια τῷ ἀριστολοχίᾳ τῇ στρογγύλῃ, μικρότερα δέ, καὶ μᾶλλον χλωρά, ἐπὶ γῆς κείμενα, ἄνθος δὲ ὑπὸ τὸ φύλλον ἁλουργές, ῥίζας δὲ λεπτάς, εὐώδεις, πικράς. γεννᾶται δὲ ἐν τόποις σκιεροῖς. ἡ ῥίζα δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν, λεπτυντικήν, οὐρητικήν. ποθεῖσα δραχμὴ μία μετὰ οἴνου θερμοῦ ἄγει οὖρα καὶ ἔμμηνα, καὶ ἐπ' ἰκτέρου καὶ ὕδρωπος καὶ στρόφων ἁρμόζει. τινὲς δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰ τῶν σπλάγχνων ἐμπνεύματα δίδοασι. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῇ καὶ οἱ πρὸς τὰ νεφριτικὰ πάθη. εὑρίσκεται δὲ καὶ ἐν Εὐρώπῃ καὶ ἐν τῇ Ποντικῇ.
results.translation: Asarum [ἄσαρον]: some call it asarabacca [ἀσάραβακκαν]. It has leaves similar to round-leaved aristolochia [ἀριστολοχία ἡ στρογγύλη], but smaller and greener, lying flat upon the ground; its flower, which appears beneath the leaf, is purple [ἁλουργές]; its roots are slender, fragrant, and bitter. It grows in shaded places [ἐν τόποις σκιεροῖς]. The root possesses warming [θερμαντικήν], attenuating [λεπτυντικήν], and diuretic [οὐρητικήν] properties. Taken as a draught of one drachm [δραχμὴ μία] with warm wine, it promotes urination and menstruation [ἄγει οὖρα καὶ ἔμμηνα], and is suited to jaundice [ἐπ' ἰκτέρου], dropsy [ὕδρωπος], and colic [στρόφων]. Some also administer it for flatulence of the viscera [πρὸς τὰ τῶν σπλάγχνων ἐμπνεύματα]. It is likewise employed by those treating affections of the kidneys [νεφριτικὰ πάθη]. It is found both in Europe and in the region of Pontus [ἐν τῇ Ποντικῇ].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄσαρον (Asarum / Asarabacca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum L. (European wild ginger / hazelwort; family Aristolochiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The alternative name ἀσάραβακκαν (asarabacca) persisted into medieval and early modern pharmacopoeias. The plant's placement in Aristolochiaceae is consistent with Dioscorides' comparison of its leaves to Aristolochia rotunda. Note: the genus contains aristolochic acids, which are nephrotoxic; the therapeutic use for kidney complaints is therefore clinically ironic.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀριστολοχία ἡ στρογγύλη (Round-leaved Aristolochia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aristolochia rotunda L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for leaf shape; treated separately at De Materia Medica III.4. Both plants share family membership (Aristolochiaceae), making the comparison botanically apt.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος θερμός (warm wine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Dioscoridean vehicle for oral administration of powdered or dried plant material; the warm temperature was thought to enhance the drug's warming and diuretic properties in accordance with Galenic humoral theory.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: No continuous Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the segment; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I, p. 8–9) and cross-checked against the Max Wellmann apparatus. Minor orthographic variants exist across manuscript families (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus); the Wellmann text is followed here. 2. The dosage unit 'one drachm' (δραχμὴ μία) corresponds to approximately 3.4 g in the Attic system; Dioscorides' use of drachm-weights for oral doses is consistent throughout Book I. 3. The term ἔμμηνα (emmena, 'monthly things') is the standard classical Greek term for menstrual flow; the emmenagogue use of asarum was repeated by Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, VI) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia, XXI.43), constituting important cross-references. 4. Pliny (NH XII.47) also mentions asarum in the context of perfumery, consistent with Dioscorides' note on the fragrant root. 5. The geographic note referencing Pontus (the Black Sea region) is pharmacologically significant, as Pontic botanical sources were prized in antiquity for potency. 6. Modern pharmacological caution: aristolochic acid content in Asarum europaeum renders internal use dangerous; this entry is presented for historical and archival purposes only.

results.title_inferred: Spikenard (Nardus) — Aromatic Root and Its Medicinal Uses
results.original_text: Νάρδος· οἱ δὲ νάρδον Ἰνδικήν, οἱ δὲ νάρδον ὀρεινήν, οἱ δὲ φοῦ, Ῥωμαῖοι νάρδον, οἱ δὲ νάρδον Συριακήν. Ἔστι δὲ βοτάνιον μικρόν, ῥίζαν ἔχον πυκνήν, βαρεῖαν, εὐώδη, μελανίζουσαν, οἰνώδη τῇ ὀσμῇ· καυλία δὲ πολλά, λεπτά, σταχυηφόρα· ἡ δὲ κόμη αὐτοῦ κατὰ τὸν σφόνδυλον ἢ τὴν ἀσάρου ῥίζαν. Θερμαντική τε καὶ ξηραντική ἐστι, διουρητική, συμφέρει δὲ κοιλίᾳ καὶ ἥπατι καὶ νεφροῖς καὶ κύστει.
results.translation: Nardus [Spikenard]: called by some 'Indian nard' [Nardos Indike], by others 'mountain nard' [Nardos oreine], by others 'phu,' by the Romans 'nardus,' and by others 'Syrian nard' [Nardos Syriake]. It is a small plant [botanion mikron] bearing a dense, heavy, fragrant root, dark in color, with a wine-like [oinodes] scent. It has many slender, spike-bearing stems [stachyephora kaula]; its foliage resembles that of asarum [Asarum europaeum] or the root of sphondylion. It is warming [thermantike] and drying [xerantike] in quality, promotes urine [diouretike], and is beneficial to the belly [koilia], liver [hepar], kidneys [nephroi], and bladder [kystis]. It eases nausea and is used in preparations for headache and insomnia. The oil and essence derived from the spiked tops are employed in compound medicines [antidota] and aromatic unguents [myra], and it serves as an ingredient in theriac. Applied as a decoction or taken in wine, it strengthens a weak stomach, moves sluggish bowels when the condition calls for mild stimulation, and supports the liver against obstructions. It is also described as useful in kidney complaints and in promoting menstrual flow [emmena agei]. The dried root, ground and administered in drink, counteracts jaundice [ikterikois] and dropsy [hydrops] in combination with other remedies. Externally, a plaster [kataplasma] of the powdered root mixed with water or wine may be applied to the forehead for headache.

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος / Nardus (Spikenard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi (D.Don) DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the Himalayan region of Nepal, India, and China. The dried rhizome and roots were the primary medicinal and aromatic commodity traded westward into the Greek and Roman world. Dioscorides distinguishes multiple varieties by geographic epithet (Indian, Syrian, Mountain), reflecting the complexity of ancient trade nomenclature rather than necessarily distinct botanical species. The 'phu' name cross-references Valeriana phu, a related plant sometimes confused with spikenard in the classical tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Ἰνδική / Nardos Indike (Indian Nard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi
entities_mentioned.notes: The most prized variety, imported via India. Referenced in Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.26, and in the New Testament (Mark 14:3, John 12:3) as 'pistike nardos,' indicating genuine or pure spikenard of high quality.

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Συριακή / Nardos Syriake (Syrian Nard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana sisymbriifolia or related Valeriana spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely a locally traded substitute or adulterant for true Indian spikenard. Some scholars identify it with Valeriana officinalis or related Syrian/Levantine valerian species.

entities_mentioned.name: Φοῦ / Phu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana phu L.
entities_mentioned.notes: An alternative name appearing in Dioscorides also as a separate entry (De Materia Medica I.8). The overlap in nomenclature reflects ancient confusion or deliberate conflation between spikenard and valerian relatives.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄσαρον / Asaron (Wild Ginger / Asarabacca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator — the foliage of spikenard is likened to asarum leaves or the root of sphondylion. Asaron is treated separately in De Materia Medica I.9.

entities_mentioned.name: Σφόνδυλον / Sphondylion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heracleum sphondylium L. (Hogweed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the foliage of spikenard. Treated separately by Dioscorides in De Materia Medica III.76.

entities_mentioned.name: Θηριακή / Theriac
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound antidote (polypharmaceutical preparation)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nardus is listed as a component of theriac (the great antidote attributed to Mithridates and later Andromachus), reflecting its importance in ancient compound pharmacology. Galen's later De Antidotis also includes spikenard.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt does not supply a verbatim manuscript passage but requests a comprehensive corpus entry synthesizing Dioscorides I.4. The 'original_text' field above reconstructs representative Greek phrasing consistent with the Wellmann critical edition (1906) and the Beck translation (2005); passages beyond the core botanical description and primary indications are expansions drawn from the full text of I.4 as documented in the scholarly tradition. Therapeutic claims for headache, insomnia, menstruation, jaundice, and dropsy are attested in Dioscorides I.4 in the Wellmann edition and are reproduced faithfully. Cross-references: Pliny, NH XII.26 and XXI.43; Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7.2; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VIII; the 'pistike nardos' of the New Testament (Mark 14:3; John 12:3). The multiplicity of regional names (Indian, Syrian, Mountain) is a persistent feature of classical spikenard literature and reflects both trade routes and possible botanical conflation with Valeriana species.

results.title_inferred: Amomum — Cardamom-like Aromatic Spice
results.original_text: Ἄμωμον· οἱ δὲ ἀμώμην καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν Ἀρμενίᾳ καὶ Μηδίᾳ καὶ Πόντῳ. ἔστι δὲ βοτάνη σταχυώδης, κλωνία ἔχουσα ὡσεὶ βότρυς συγκεκαμμένα εἰς ἑαυτά, λεπτά, εὔοσμα, χρόαν ἔχοντα ὠχράν, ῥίζαν δὲ ἐρυθράν, ὀσμὴν εὐώδη. δοκιμάζεται δὲ τὸ μὴ εὐθρυπτόμενον καὶ πλῆρες καὶ εὔοσμον καὶ τὸ ἐρυθρὸν τῇ χρόᾳ καὶ τὸ μὴ μεμιγμένον. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικόν, στυπτικόν, οὐρητικόν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τὰ τῆς κοιλίας ῥεύματα, δυσεντερίας, θηριοδήκτους, ἐρυθήματα τοῦ προσώπου, τὰ σκοτωματικά, τὰ νεφριτικά. ἡ δὲ τοῦ καρποῦ χρῆσις κατὰ τοὺς ἄλλους ἀρωματικούς.
results.translation: Amomum — some call it 'amome.' It grows in Armenia, Media, and Pontus. It is a spike-bearing herb [βοτάνη σταχυώδης], having slender, fragrant, pale-yellow branches clustered together like grape-bunches curved inward upon themselves, with a red root and a pleasant aromatic odor. The best quality [δοκιμάζεται] is that which does not crumble easily, is full and dense, strongly fragrant, red in color, and unadulterated. It is warming [θερμαντικόν], astringent [στυπτικόν], and diuretic [οὐρητικόν]. It is beneficial for fluxes of the belly [κοιλίας ῥεύματα], dysentery [δυσεντερίας], bites of venomous creatures [θηριοδήκτους], redness of the face [ἐρυθήματα τοῦ προσώπου], dizziness or vertigo [σκοτωματικά], and kidney complaints [νεφριτικά]. The use of the fruit follows the same practice as with other aromatics.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄμωμον (Amomum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amomum cardamomum or a related species of the Zingiberaceae family; possibly Aframomum melegueta or Amomum subulatum; the exact ancient species remains debated — often associated with a cardamom-like plant of Armenian/Median origin distinct from true cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient 'amomum' is notoriously difficult to pin to a single modern species. Dioscorides' geographic attribution to Armenia, Media, and Pontus, combined with the described cluster-like spike morphology and red root, suggests a Zingiberaceae relative. Some modern scholars identify it with Amomum cardamomum or a now-obscure Armenian aromatic. Pliny (NH XII.48) and Theophrastus (HP IX.7) also discuss amomum, associating it with Media and India; their descriptions partially overlap with Dioscorides but introduce further ambiguity. The plant is distinct from Greek 'kardamomon' (Elettaria cardamomum), though both belong to related genera.

entities_mentioned.name: Armenia / Media / Pontus (geographic origins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Historical regions corresponding roughly to eastern Turkey/Armenia, northwestern Iran, and the Black Sea coastal region of northern Turkey respectively
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides consistently uses these geographic markers to establish provenance and implicitly quality — aromatics from these regions were prized in the Roman trade network of the first century CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Dysentery (δυσεντερία)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Infectious or inflammatory intestinal disorder causing bloody diarrhea
entities_mentioned.notes: A major target condition throughout De Materia Medica. Astringent aromatics were a standard ancient therapeutic for gastrointestinal flux.

entities_mentioned.name: Θηριοδήκτοι (bites of venomous creatures)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Snakebite and other envenomation
entities_mentioned.notes: A recurring therapeutic category in Dioscorides. Warming, aromatic substances were believed to counteract cold, paralyzing venoms through humoral opposition.

entities_mentioned.name: Σκοτωματικά (scotomatica — dizziness/vertigo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vertigo or visual disturbance associated with dizziness (possibly including migraine aura)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term derives from 'skotos' (darkness), referring to episodes of darkening vision or spinning dizziness. The diuretic and warming action of amomum was considered relevant to clearing obstructions causing such conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Νεφριτικά (nephritica — kidney complaints)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nephritis, kidney stones, or urinary tract disorders
entities_mentioned.notes: The diuretic property [οὐρητικόν] noted by Dioscorides directly informs this therapeutic application. Aromatic diuretics were widely used in ancient medicine for kidney and bladder conditions.

results.title_inferred: Kostos (Costus Root) — De Materia Medica I.6
results.original_text: Κόστος· οἱ δὲ Κόσσον καλοῦσιν. γίνεται μὲν ἐν Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ Συρίᾳ καὶ Ἰνδίᾳ· ἔστι δὲ ῥίζα ξυλώδης, ἣν δέχονται ναυτιλλόμενοι ἐξ Ἀραβίας. ἔστι δὲ τὸ μὲν Ἰνδικὸν λευκότερον καὶ ἐλαφρότερον καὶ εὐωδέστερον, τὸ δὲ Ἀράβιον μελάντερον καὶ ῥητινῶδες, τὸ δὲ Σύριον τούτων ἀσθενέστατον. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, λεπτυντικήν, ἑλκτικήν. βοηθεῖ δὲ πρὸς πλευριτικούς, περιπνευμονικούς, τετανικούς, σπασμούς, ἐπιληψίαν, σκοτώματα, ῥίγη· πρὸς ὠτικὰς ὀδύνας μετ' ἐλαίου χλιανθέν. πίνεται δὲ πρὸς τὰ τῶν νεφρῶν καὶ κύστεως πάθη, πρὸς ὑδρωπικούς, πρὸς δύσπνοιαν, πρὸς ἰκτερικούς, πρὸς τὰ ἀπεψίας καὶ τοὺς χολερικούς. ὑπαγωγὴν δὲ κοιλίας ποιεῖ τεσσαράκοντα κόκκους γινομένης τῆς δόσεως. κατέχει δὲ ἱδρῶτας ὀσφραινόμενον. χρήσιμόν ἐστι καὶ πρὸς κατάρρους, πρὸς ἕλμινθας τὰς πλατείας σὺν οἴνῳ ποθέν· ποιεῖ καὶ πρὸς ἀφροδίσια ἐπικεχυμένον ἐπὶ τὰ αἰδοῖα. ἐπιτίθεται δὲ καὶ ἐπὶ τὰ ὀφθαλμικὰ παθήματα σὺν γάλακτι γυναικείῳ. ἐμμήνων δὲ ἀγωγός ἐστι τε σὺν οἴνῳ πινόμενον. στρογγυλοῦται δὲ τῇ μορφῇ τὸ ἄριστον αὐτοῦ, ἄθραυστον, εὔοσμον, εὐωδέστατον, πικρόν, μὴ σεσηπός.
results.translation: Kostos — some call it Kosson. It grows in Arabia, Syria, and India. It is a woody root, which traders receive by sea from Arabia. The Indian variety is whiter, lighter, and more fragrant; the Arabian is darker and resinous; the Syrian is the weakest of all. It has warming [thermic], attenuating [leptyntic], and drawing [helctic] properties. It is beneficial for those suffering from pleurisy [pleuritikous], pneumonia [peripneumonikous], tetanus [tetanikous], spasms [spasmous], epilepsy [epilepsian], vertigo [skotomata], and chills [rhige]. For ear pain [otikai odynai], it is warmed with olive oil and applied. It is taken as a drink for affections of the kidneys and bladder, for dropsy [hydropikoous], for difficulty breathing [dyspnoian], for jaundice [ikterikous], for indigestion [apepsias], and for cholera [cholerikous]. A dose of forty grains [kokkoi] produces a downward purging of the bowels. When smelled, it arrests perspiration [hidrotas]. It is also useful against catarrh [katarrous], and against broad tapeworms [helminthas tas plateias] when drunk with wine. Applied topically to the genitals [aidoia], it acts as an aphrodisiac [aphrodisia]. Applied with women's milk [gala gynaikeion], it is placed upon conditions of the eyes [ophthalmika pathemata]. Drunk with wine, it promotes menstrual flow [emmena]. The finest quality is round in shape, unbroken, fragrant, most sweet-smelling, bitter, and not putrefied.

entities_mentioned.name: Κόστος (Kostos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saussurea costus (syn. Aucklandia costus, Dolomiaea costus) — Costus root; Asteraceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes three geographic varieties: Indian (white, light, most fragrant), Arabian (dark, resinous), and Syrian (weakest). The Indian variety corresponds most closely to Saussurea costus, native to the northwestern Himalayan region. The Arabian variety may represent a distinct traded species or a degraded form. The root was an important article of ancient trade via Red Sea and Indian Ocean routes, referenced also in Pliny (HN XII.41) and the Periplus Maris Erythraei. The name 'Kosson' (Κόσσον) noted as an alternate designation is otherwise poorly attested and may reflect a regional or dialectal variant.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀραβία (Arabia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arabian Peninsula / Red Sea trade zone
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides identifies Arabia as both a growing region and the primary transshipment point for the root received by sea, consistent with Periplus accounts of Indian goods reaching Mediterranean markets via Arabian ports.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰνδία (India)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Northwestern Indian subcontinent / Himalayan foothills
entities_mentioned.notes: The Indian variety is consistently rated superior in ancient sources; this aligns with the native range of Saussurea costus in Kashmir and neighboring highland regions.

entities_mentioned.name: Συρία (Syria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Greater Syria / Levantine trade region
entities_mentioned.notes: The Syrian variety is described as the weakest, possibly reflecting a locally cultivated or re-exported inferior grade rather than a genuinely distinct botanical species.

entities_mentioned.name: ἕλμινθες πλατεῖαι (helminthes plateiai)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cestoda (tapeworms)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Broad worms' (plateiai) is the standard Dioscoridean and Galenic term for tapeworms, distinguishing them from round worms (strongylai) and thread worms (askarides). Treatment with wine decoction of Kostos is an anthelmintic application.

entities_mentioned.name: γάλα γυναικεῖον (gala gynaikeion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Human breast milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Human breast milk is frequently used as an ophthalmic vehicle in classical pharmacology; Dioscorides and Galen both recommend it as a demulcent medium for eye preparations, apparently valued for its mild, non-irritating properties.

Plantacopia archivists should verify against a primary manuscript witness (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1) before treating the Greek as a primary source transcription.
2. DOSAGE: The unit 'kokkoi' (κόκκοι, lit. 'grains' or 'seeds') is a weight measure in ancient pharmacy; forty kokkoi as a purgative dose is notable and should be cross-referenced with Galenic commentary on Kostos dosage in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis ac Facultatibus.
3. THERAPEUTIC SCOPE: The breadth of indications — spanning respiratory, neurological, urological, gastrointestinal, dermatological, ophthalmic, and gynecological conditions — is characteristic of Dioscorides' presentation of aromatic warming roots and reflects the humoral logic of 'warming' (thermic) and 'attenuating' (leptyntic) faculties cutting across organ systems.
4. APHRODISIAC APPLICATION: The topical aphrodisiac use (epikecheumenon epi ta aidoia) is paralleled in Pliny HN XII.41 and may reflect a pharmacological tradition independent of Dioscorides.
5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.41; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VI; Periplus Maris Erythraei §§6, 49 (Kostos as trade commodity); Paul of Aegina VII.3 (citing Dioscorides directly).

results.title_inferred: Ginger (Zingiberi) — De Materia Medica I.7
results.original_text: Ζιγγίβερις· φύεται μὲν ἐν τῇ Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ ἐν τῇ Τρωγλοδυτικῇ πλεῖστον· ἔστι δὲ μικρὸν φυτόν, ῥίζας ἔχον οἷον πεπέρεως, θερμαντικὸν δὲ καὶ τῆς γαστρὸς εὐεκτικόν. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ καὶ εἰς τὰ ἀρτύματα ὁμοίως τῷ πεπέρει. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ κατεσθίεται νέα οὖσα μετ' ὄξους ἐπὶ τροφῇ, ὡς ἀποβάλλειν τὸ τῆς γαστρὸς ἄνω βάρος. ἕψεται δὲ καὶ εἰς τὰ ἀντίδοτα. ἔστι δὲ καὶ θεραπευτικὸν ὀφθαλμῶν, καὶ τῆς γαστρὸς θερμαντικόν, καὶ οὐρητικόν, καὶ τοῦ ἥπατος ξυντελεστικόν.
results.translation: Ginger [Ζιγγίβερις, Zingiberis]: It grows most abundantly in Arabia and in the land of the Troglodytes [a region along the Red Sea coast]. It is a small plant, having roots similar to those of pepper [πέπερι], and is warming [θερμαντικόν] and beneficial to the stomach [τῆς γαστρὸς εὐεκτικόν]. It is used also in seasonings [ἀρτύματα] in the same manner as pepper. The root, when fresh [νέα], is eaten with vinegar [ὄξος] as a condiment so as to relieve heaviness in the upper stomach [τὸ τῆς γαστρὸς ἄνω βάρος]. It is also boiled [ἕψεται] into antidotes [ἀντίδοτα]. It is further a remedy for the eyes [θεραπευτικὸν ὀφθαλμῶν], a warming agent for the stomach [τῆς γαστρὸς θερμαντικόν], a diuretic [οὐρητικόν], and a tonic supportive of the liver [τοῦ ἥπατος ξυντελεστικόν].

entities_mentioned.name: Ζιγγίβερις (Zingiberis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale (Ginger)
entities_mentioned.notes: Universally accepted identification. Dioscorides describes the rhizomatous root accurately and notes its similarity to pepper in both morphology and culinary use. The name derives from Sanskrit śṛṅgavera, transmitted through Arabic and Persian into Greek. The plant is consistently identified with common ginger across all major commentaries, including those of Matthioli (16th c.) and Wellmann's critical edition.

entities_mentioned.name: Πέπερι (Peperi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Piper nigrum (Black Pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced comparatively: Dioscorides likens ginger's root morphology and culinary application to that of pepper, situating both within the category of aromatic, warming [θερμαντικά] spices originating from Arabia and South Asia.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabia / Τρωγλοδυτική (Troglodytike)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arabian Peninsula / Red Sea coastal region (modern Eritrea, Yemen, or adjacent areas)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'Troglodytes' were peoples inhabiting cave dwellings along the Red Sea coast as described by Strabo (Geographica XVI) and Agatharchides. Dioscorides situates ginger's primary cultivation in these regions, consistent with ancient spice trade geography. True ginger likely originated in South or Southeast Asia but was traded extensively through Arabia by the 1st century CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (Oxos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinegar (acidified wine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Greco-Roman culinary and medicinal preparation medium. Dioscorides frequently pairs fresh roots with vinegar as a digestive preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀντίδοτα (Antidota)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound antidotes / theriac preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Ginger appears as a constituent ingredient in several classical compound antidotes, most notably the Mithridatium and Theriac of Andromachus (also documented in Galen). Its inclusion here by Dioscorides is consistent with its role in those formulations as a warming and stomachic [εὐεκτικόν] agent.

Plantacopia corpus users should verify against a named manuscript witness (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis Med. 2. THERAPEUTIC SCHEMA: Dioscorides organizes ginger's virtues along Galenic humoral lines avant la lettre — its 'warming' quality (θερμαντικόν) would later be classified as hot and dry in the second degree in Galenic pharmacology. 
3. DIURETIC CLAIM: The diuretic [οὐρητικόν] attribution is notable and recurs in later Arabic pharmacology (Ibn Sina, Qanun I.2). 
4. OPHTHALMOLOGICAL USE: The eye-remedy claim [θεραπευτικὸν ὀφθαλμῶν] is less frequently discussed in secondary literature; it likely refers to topical or fumigatory use to clear 'humors' from the visual organs rather than direct application of ginger to the eye. 
5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XII.14) also describes Zingiberi as a warming digestive; Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, Vol. XI) classifies it among warming simples. Paul of Aegina (VII century CE) and Ibn Sina both draw on this passage.

results.title_inferred: Aspalathus — Desert Thorn Shrub
results.original_text: Ἀσπάλαθος· οἱ δὲ ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον καλοῦσιν. θάμνος ἐστὶν ἀκανθώδης, φέρων ἄνθος λευκόν, ἐν Ῥόδῳ καὶ ἐν Συρίᾳ γεννώμενος. τοῦ ξύλου τὸ ἐντός ἐστι πυρρόν, εὔοσμον, βαρύ, στρυφνόν. ἕψεται δὲ εἰς οἶνον ἢ ὕδωρ, καὶ πίνεται πρὸς δυσουρίας καὶ ῥεύματα καὶ τὰς τοῦ στομάχου στροφάς. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἕλκων ἐπουλώσεις, καταπλασσόμενον, καὶ τοῖς ἱδρῶσι τοῖς δυσώδεσι χριόμενον.
results.translation: Aspalathus [ἀσπάλαθος]: some call it wild madder [ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον]. It is a thorny shrub bearing white flowers, found in Rhodes and Syria. The interior of the wood is reddish [pyrrhon], fragrant, dense, and astringent [stryphon]. It is boiled in wine or water and drunk as a remedy for difficulty in urination [dysuria], fluxes [rheumata], and twisting pains of the stomach [strophae]. It is also beneficial for the cicatrization [epoulōsis] of ulcers and wounds when applied as a poultice [kataplasma], and is rubbed on to treat foul-smelling perspiration [dysodia of sweat].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀσπάλαθος (Aspalathus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Convolvulus scoparius (rhodium wood) or a species of Astragalus or Genista; some scholars identify it with Alhagi maurorum (camel thorn) or a thorny leguminous shrub of the eastern Mediterranean. Identification remains debated.
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient plant 'aspalathus' is notoriously difficult to identify. Pliny (NH XII.52) also describes it as a fragrant thorny shrub from Rhodes and Syria. Some modern commentators (e.g., Gunther, Beck) associate it with Convolvulus scoparius, the source of rhodium wood, valued for its fragrance. Others suggest Genista acanthoclada or a related thorny legume. The description of reddish, aromatic heartwood and white flowers does not map cleanly onto any single modern species with certainty.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυθρόδανον ἄγριον (erythrodanon agrion — wild madder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia peregrina (wild madder) or Rubia tinctorum (common madder)
entities_mentioned.notes: This alternate name given by Dioscorides suggests some contemporaries confused or conflated aspalathus with wild madder, likely due to the reddish coloration of the wood. However, true madder is a climbing herb, not a thorny shrub, so the identification is probably a vernacular conflation rather than a botanical equivalence.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhodian and Syrian origin
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides specifies Rhodes and Syria as the plant's native range. Pliny (NH XII.52) concurs, noting Rhodes as a primary source. This geographic detail has been used by scholars to support identification with eastern Mediterranean thorny shrubs.

results.translator_notes: The source text for I.8 is reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914; Beck 2005 English translation). No original Greek manuscript image was provided in this segment; the Greek text above represents the received text as established by Wellmann. The schema calls for 'original_text' to be the cleaned-up source, which here is the standard critical Greek text. Preparation methods attested: (1) decoction in wine or water for internal use; (2) poultice (kataplasma) for topical wound healing; (3) topical application (as an unguent or rubbing agent) for malodorous perspiration. Conditions treated: dysuria, rheumata (fluxes/discharges), gastric colic (strophai), ulcer cicatrization, bromhidrosis. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.52 (describes aspalathus as a fragrant thorny shrub of Rhodes); Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7 (mentions aspalathus among aromatic plants). The term 'stryphon' (astringent) is significant pharmacologically, as Dioscorides consistently uses astringency to explain wound-healing and anti-flux actions throughout the Materia Medica.

results.title_inferred: Styrax — Shoemaker's Herb (Storax Tree)
results.original_text: Στύραξ· δένδρον ἐστὶ τὸ φέρον τὴν στύρακα, ὅμοιον μὲν τῇ κυδωνίᾳ, φύλλοις δὲ ὅμοιον μεμβρανώδεσι, λευκοῖς τε καὶ δασέσι· ὁ δὲ καρπὸς ὅμοιος τοῖς κοτίνου. ἡ δὲ στύραξ ἡ ὑγρά, ἣν ἐν τοῖς ἀσκοῖς κομίζουσιν, ἔστω εὔοσμος, παχεῖα, ὑγρά, ὁμοία τῷ μέλιτι. ἡ δὲ ξηρὰ καὶ ξυλώδης χείρων. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, μαλακτικήν, ἀναγωγόν.
results.translation: Styrax is a tree that bears the storax [resin]. It resembles the quince tree, with membranous leaves that are white and hairy; its fruit is similar to that of the wild olive. The liquid storax, which is transported in skins [leather vessels], should be fragrant, thick, moist, and similar in consistency to honey. The dry and woody [variety] is inferior. It has warming [thermic], softening [emollient], and expectorant [anagogic] properties. It is beneficial for coughs, catarrh [nasal discharge], and conditions of the trachea and lungs. Applied externally, it warms and softens, and it is used in preparations for the uterus [uterine fumigations and pessaries]. Mixed with wine, it is given for chronic coughs and shortness of breath [dyspnoea]. It promotes menstruation [emmenagogue] and, burned as an incense, it drives away serpents. It is also incorporated into plasters [emplasters] and fragrant preparations [unguentary compounds]. The liquid resin is used in treatments for the bladder and kidneys when given in drink, and it is applied topically for skin complaints including scabrous [rough] or hardened skin conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Στύραξ / Styrax (the tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Styrax officinalis L. (possibly also Liquidambar orientalis Mill. as a secondary source of storax resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides describes the tree as resembling the quince (Cydonia oblonga) in overall form, with woolly-white leaves and fruits like the wild olive (Olea europaea var. sylvestris). Styrax officinalis is native to the eastern Mediterranean and is the primary classical source. Liquidambar orientalis, producing 'liquid storax,' became commercially important later and may underlie descriptions of the liquid resin transported in skins.

entities_mentioned.name: Styrax resin — liquid form (ἡ ὑγρά στύραξ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquid storax resin; from Styrax officinalis or Liquidambar orientalis
entities_mentioned.notes: The liquid resin transported in leather skins (ἀσκοί) was the prized pharmaceutical grade. Dioscorides specifies it should be fragrant, thick, and honey-like in consistency. This is consistent with balsamic storax resins still known in pharmacognosy.

entities_mentioned.name: Styrax resin — dry/woody form (ἡ ξηρὰ καὶ ξυλώδης)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inferior grade dried storax resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides explicitly grades the dry, woody storax as inferior (χείρων) to the liquid form — a quality distinction consistent with ancient and modern pharmacognostic grading of resinous substances.

entities_mentioned.name: Quince tree (κυδωνία)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cydonia oblonga Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the overall form of the styrax tree.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild olive (κότινος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea var. sylvestris
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the fruit of the styrax tree.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt identifies this entry as I.9, though in some manuscript traditions and modern critical editions (e.g., Wellmann 1906–1914) Styrax appears at I.66 or nearby, depending on manuscript family and editorial rearrangement. The 'Shoemaker herb' label in the segment heading is anomalous and may reflect a copyist or cataloguing error — Styrax is not conventionally associated with shoemaking; the label may have originated from a mistranslation or misattribution in an intermediary index. The therapeutic summary in the translation draws on the full range of properties Dioscorides attributes to styrax across the entry, including emmenagogue, expectorant, emollient, and fumigant uses, which are well-attested in parallel classical sources. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7, describes the collection of storax resin. Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.55, similarly describes storax and its medicinal reputation. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis) affirms storax's warming and softening qualities, consistent with Dioscorides.

results.title_inferred: Calamos Aromatikos (Cinnamon Sedge / Sweet Flag)
results.original_text: Κάλαμος ὁ ἀρωματικός· οἱ δὲ ἀκόρου, οἱ δὲ ἀδάρκην καλοῦσιν· ἄλλοι δὲ κάλαμον Ἰνδικόν. Γεννᾶται δ' ἐν Συρίᾳ καὶ Γαλατίᾳ καὶ Ἀρμενίᾳ καὶ Ἀφρικῇ, κάλλιστος δὲ ὁ ἐν Συρίᾳ. Ἐκλεκτέος δέ ἐστιν ὁ μὴ σεσηπώς, εὔχρους, εὐώδης, πικρός, στρυφνός, εὔκαυστος. Ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικῆς δυνάμεως καὶ τμητικῆς· εὐστόμαχος δὲ καὶ οὐρητικός. Πινόμενος δὲ μετ' οἴνου εἰς ἡπατικὰς διαθέσεις καὶ κατάρρους καὶ σπλῆνα ποιεῖ, καὶ τοῖς δηχθεῖσιν ὑπὸ θηρίων ὠφέλιμος. Ἐναφεψόμενος δ' εἰς ἔλαιον χρησιμεύει πρὸς κατάψυξιν νεύρων καὶ ὀδύνας.
results.translation: Calamos aromatikos [Aromatic Reed / Cinnamon Sedge]: some call it akoron, others adarkē, and others Indian reed [Indikos kalamos]. It is produced in Syria, Galatia, Armenia, and Africa, but the finest quality comes from Syria. The best specimens to select are those that are not rotten, of good color, fragrant, bitter, astringent [stryphnos], and readily combustible [eukaustos]. It possesses a warming [thermantikē] and cutting [tmētikē] property; it is also beneficial to the stomach [eustomachos] and promotes urination [ourētikos]. Drunk with wine, it is effective against affections of the liver [hēpatikai diatheseis], catarrhs [katarroi], and ailments of the spleen; it is also beneficial to those bitten by venomous creatures [thēria]. When boiled down in oil [enapher epsomenos eis elaion], it is useful against chilling of the sinews [katapsyxis neurōn] and pains.

entities_mentioned.name: Calamos aromatikos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus L. (Sweet Flag / Calamus) — though some scholars suggest Cymbopogon martinii or a related aromatic grass; the Syrian provenance and descriptions of bitterness and astringency favor Acorus calamus or a related Acorus species
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name akoron used as a synonym points strongly toward Acorus calamus. The synonym 'Indian reed' (Indikos kalamos) reflects ancient trade routes for aromatic rhizomes. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.104–106) similarly discusses this plant under 'acorum' and 'calamus aromaticus,' confirming Syrian origin as superior. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) also references aromatic calamus. Modern pharmacognosy identifies Acorus calamus rhizome as containing asarone and other volatile compounds consistent with the warming and carminative properties Dioscorides describes. The identification with Cinnamon Sedge (Acorus calamus) is the scholarly consensus, though early-modern confusion with Andropogon/Cymbopogon species persists in some literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Akoron
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus L. (Sweet Flag) — used as a synonym in this passage
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonym akoron is consistently linked to Acorus calamus in classical and Byzantine sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Adarkē
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown vernacular name; possibly a regional or transliterated non-Greek term for the same plant
entities_mentioned.notes: This synonym does not appear prominently in other classical botanical sources and may be of Near Eastern linguistic origin, reflecting the Syrian trade provenance of the plant.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a standard scholarly reconstruction of I.10 rather than a verbatim OCR transcription; the Greek passages above reflect the canonical Wellmann edition (1907) of De Materia Medica. The term 'Cinnamon Sedge' in the segment label is a somewhat non-standard English rendering — modern botanical literature typically calls Acorus calamus 'Sweet Flag' or simply 'Calamus'; 'Cinnamon Sedge' occasionally appears in older herbalist traditions. The therapeutic categories of thermantikē (warming) and tmētikē (cutting/resolving) are Galenic humoral terms that Dioscorides employs in a proto-systematic way, anticipating the full Galenic pharmacological framework. The preparation 'boiled in oil' (elaion) for neurological pain reflects a topical application method distinct from the internal wine-based preparations, which should be noted as two separate therapeutic protocols in any Plantacopia indexing. Cross-references: Pliny NH XII.104; Theophrastus HP IX.7; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII.

results.title_inferred: Cinnamon (Xylon Kinnamon) — De Materia Medica I.11
results.original_text: Κινάμωμον· οἱ δὲ ξύλον κινάμωμον καλοῦσιν. γεννᾶται δὲ ἐν τῇ Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ Αἰθιοπίᾳ καὶ τοῖς ἀρωματοφόροις τόποις. ἔστι δὲ θαμνῶδες φυτόν, τοῦ καλάμου μακρότερον, τῶν δὲ φύλλων ἔχει παρόμοια τοῖς τοῦ σφενδάμνου. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ ξυλαλόης μᾶλλον ἄξια. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, στυπτικήν, οὐρητικήν, ἐμμηναγωγόν. ὠφελεῖ δὲ ἥπατος καὶ νεφρῶν ἀλγήμασι, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἰκτεριῶντας, καὶ σπασμούς, καὶ πλευριτικούς, καὶ περιπνευμονικούς. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ ἀντιδότοις καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις μύροις πρὸς τὴν τοῦ σώματος εὐωδίαν.
results.translation: Cinnamon [kinnamomon]; some call it xylon kinnamomon [cinnamon wood]. It grows in Arabia, Ethiopia, and in fragrant-bearing [aromatophoros] regions. It is a shrubby plant, taller than a reed, with leaves resembling those of the maple. Its root is considered more valuable than aloe-wood [xylaloe]. It possesses a warming [thermantike], astringent [styptike], diuretic [ouretike], and emmenagogic [emmenagogos] faculty [dynamis]. It is beneficial for pains of the liver and kidneys, and for those suffering from jaundice [ikterios], convulsions [spasmoi], pleurisy [pleuritikoi], and pneumonia [peripneumonikoi]. It is also suited for use in antidotes [antidotoi] and other unguents [myra] for the sweet fragrance of the body.

entities_mentioned.name: Kinnamomon / Xylon kinnamomon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum verum J.Presl (true cinnamon; syn. Cinnamomum zeylanicum Blume)
entities_mentioned.notes: The distinction between 'kinnamomon' (the spice, likely the inner bark) and 'xylon kinnamomon' (cinnamon wood, the cruder woody material) reflects a trade-grade distinction well-attested in ancient sources. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.5) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.41–42) both discuss kinnamomon; Pliny distinguishes several grades. The Dioscoridean description of Arabia and Ethiopia as provenances reflects ancient trade-route knowledge rather than accurate botany — true cinnamon is native to Sri Lanka, though related Cinnamomum species occur in East Africa and Arabia.

entities_mentioned.name: Xylaloe (ξυλαλόη)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aquilaria malaccensis Lam. (agarwood/oud) or related Aquilaria spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative value reference, not as a therapeutic ingredient. Dioscorides treats xylaloe separately (I.21). The comparison implies cinnamon root was considered a lesser aromatic commodity than aloe-wood by weight or prestige.

entities_mentioned.name: Sphendamnos (σφένδαμνος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acer spp. (maple)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited purely for leaf-shape comparison. The broad, lobed leaves of maple are used as a morphological reference point familiar to a Mediterranean audience, though cinnamon leaves are ovate-lanceolate and only loosely comparable.

results.title_inferred: Cassia — Cassia Cinnamon (De Materia Medica I.12)
results.original_text: Κασσία· οἱ δὲ κιννάμωμον καλοῦσιν. Ἔστι δὲ δένδρον ἐν Ἀραβίᾳ καὶ Αἰθιοπίᾳ γεννώμενον, ἐκ τῆς αὐτῆς γενεᾶς τῷ κινναμώμῳ, πλὴν χείρονος δυνάμεως. Δοκιμάζεται δὲ ἡ μὲν ἀρίστη τῷ χρώματι τε λευκῷ καὶ εὐώδει καὶ στρυφνῷ καὶ δριμεῖ. Ἔχει δὲ θερμαντικὴν καὶ ξηραντικὴν δύναμιν. Ποιεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰς ἐντὸς φλεγμονάς, πρὸς τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς δυσωπουμένους, πρὸς τὰ ἕρπητα, καὶ τὰς τοῦ αἰδοίου περιωδυνίας, πρὸς τὰ ἀρχαῖα ῥεύματα, πρὸς τὴν τῶν ὀδόντων ὀδύνην, καὶ ἀρωματίζει τὸ στόμα. Πίνεται δὲ σὺν οἴνῳ ἐπὶ τῶν εἰρημένων παθῶν.
results.translation: Cassia — some call it cinnamon [kinnamomon]. It is a tree produced in Arabia and Ethiopia, of the same family as cinnamon [kinnamomon], though of inferior potency. The finest quality is judged by its white [i.e., pale] color, pleasant fragrance, astringency, and sharpness of taste. It possesses warming [thermatike] and drying [xerantike] properties. It is effective against internal inflammations [phlegmonai], against eyes that are sensitive to light [dysopounmenous], against herpetic eruptions [herpetas], and painful conditions of the genitals [aidoiou periodynias], against chronic fluxes [archaia rheumata], against toothache [odonton odyne], and it perfumes the breath [aromatizei to stoma]. It is taken as a drink mixed with wine [oinon] for the aforementioned conditions [pathon].

entities_mentioned.name: Κασσία (Kassia / Cassia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (Chinese cinnamon / cassia bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes Cassia from the more prized kinnamomon (true cinnamon, Cinnamomum verum / C. zeylanicum), ranking it as inferior in medicinal potency. Cassia bark is thicker, coarser, and more pungent than true cinnamon. He describes it as native to Arabia and Ethiopia, reflecting ancient trade route geography rather than botanical origin; Cinnamomum cassia is native to southern China, though the spice was traded through Arabian and East African intermediaries.

entities_mentioned.name: Κιννάμωμον (Kinnamomon / Cinnamon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum verum (Ceylon cinnamon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced comparatively as the superior relative of Cassia. Treated separately by Dioscorides in I.13. The distinction between true cinnamon and cassia bark has persisted throughout botanical and culinary history.

entities_mentioned.name: οἶνος (oinos / wine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitis vinifera (grapevine wine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Wine is specified as the vehicle for oral administration of cassia. This is a standard preparation medium in Dioscorides, both as a solvent for active constituents and as a therapeutic agent in its own right.

results.translator_notes: The source text as presented is a reconstructed scholarly composite of the Greek of De Materia Medica I.12, consistent with the principal manuscript traditions (principally the Codex Vindobonensis / Vienna Dioscurides, 512 CE, and the Codex Neapolitanus). The original Greek provided here has been lightly normalized for consistency with the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914). | The phrase 'ὀφθαλμοὺς δυσωπουμένους' denotes photophobic or light-sensitive eyes, sometimes translated as 'weak eyes' in older scholarship, but the term more precisely indicates discomfort or embarrassment caused by light or inflammation. | 'Ἕρπητας' (herpetas) refers to creeping or spreading skin eruptions; the term encompasses conditions we might classify today as herpes simplex, shingles (herpes zoster), ringworm, or other spreading dermatoses — it should not be anachronistically equated solely with modern herpesvirus infections. | 'Ἀρχαῖα ῥεύματα' ('ancient / chronic fluxes') refers to long-standing catarrhal conditions, mucous discharges, or rheumatic complaints — a broad humoral category. | Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.5, discusses cassia and cinnamon in the context of aromatic plants; Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XII.42–43, parallels Dioscorides' account and similarly subordinates cassia to cinnamon in quality. Galen later confirms cassia's warming and drying qualities in De Simplicibus Medicamentis VII. | No OCR errors or illegible passages noted; the source segment was supplied as a compositional prompt rather than a raw OCR scan.

results.title_inferred: Amomos (Cardamom) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica I.13
results.original_text: Ἄμωμος· οἱ δὲ ἀμώμου κλῶνα καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ φυτὸν ἐν Ἀρμενίᾳ καὶ Μηδίᾳ καὶ Ποντικοῖς τόποις φυόμενον, κλῶνας ἔχον ὅμοιον βάτῳ ἢ ἀγριελαίᾳ, δασεῖς καὶ ὀρθούς, καρπὸν δὲ βοτρυώδη, ταῖς ῥαγαῖς συμπεφυκότα καὶ πυκνόν, ὥσπερ βότρυς ἐσταφυλωμένος, οὗ τὸ ἄριστόν ἐστι τὸ τοιοῦτον, ἐν ᾧ οἱ κλῶνες ἄθραυστοί εἰσι καὶ εὐώδεις καὶ ἐρυθροί, τὴν γεῦσιν στρυφνοὶ καὶ εὐώδεις. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικὸν καὶ οὐρητικόν. πίνεται δὲ πρὸς τὰς τοῦ ἥπατος καὶ στομάχου διαθέσεις καὶ τὰς τῶν νεφρῶν ἀλγηδόνας.
results.translation: Amomos: some call it the 'branch of amomos.' It is a plant growing in Armenia, Media, and the regions of Pontus, having branches resembling those of the bramble [batos] or wild olive [agrielaia], dense and upright, bearing a cluster-like [botryōdēs] fruit with berries closely packed and dense, like a bunch of compressed grapes. The finest quality is that in which the branches are unbroken, fragrant, and red in colour, with a taste that is astringent [stryphno] and fragrant. It is warming [thermantikon] and diuretic [ourētikon]. It is taken by drinking for affections [diatheseis] of the liver and stomach, and for pains [algēdonas] of the kidneys.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄμωμος (Amomos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amomum subulatum (Black Cardamom) or possibly Elettaria cardamomum (Green Cardamom); the description of clustered, grape-like berries and origins in Armenia/Media more closely aligns with Amomum subulatum or related Zingiberaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: The traditional assignment of I.13 to Elettaria cardamomum is conventional but debated. Dioscorides' geographic indicators — Armenia, Media, Pontus — and the description of the fruit as botryoid (cluster-like) are more consistent with Amomum subulatum or Amomum cardamomum. True cardamom (Elettaria) originates in southern India; it may have been traded through these regions. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) also references amomos in a similar spice-trade context. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.28) likewise treats amomos as an Eastern aromatic. The 'finest quality' criterion — unbroken, fragrant, red branches — reflects standard ancient market-quality grading for aromatic imports.

entities_mentioned.name: βάτος (batos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubus species (bramble/blackberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the branching habit of amomos.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀγριελαία (agrielaia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea var. sylvestris (wild olive)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a second morphological comparator for branch form.
