results.title_inferred: Malobathron (Tejpat Leaf / Indian Bay Leaf)
results.original_text: Μαλόβαθρον· οἱ δὲ Μαλάβαθρον καλοῦσιν. φύλλον ἐστὶ δένδρου μεγάλου φυομένου ἐν Ἰνδίᾳ καὶ Συρίᾳ, ὅμοιον τῷ τῆς πυξάρου, εὔοσμον, μηδεμίαν ἔχον κοτύλην, πρὸς ἄκρον δὲ λεπτυνόμενον· ὀσμὴν δὲ ἔχει νάρδῳ παραπλησίαν. ἔστι δὲ τὸ εὐωδέστατον τὸ μὴ παλαιόν, τὸ δὲ χλωρόν, τὸ μὴ κατεαγός. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ ἀρωματοποιοί τε καὶ μυρεψοί. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικῆς καὶ στυπτικῆς δυνάμεως· εὐστόμαχον, ἧπαρ θεραπεύει, κύστιν καὶ νεφρούς, οὔρησιν κινεῖ. ἑψόμενον δὲ εἰς μύρον χρησιμεύει.
results.translation: Malobathron — some call it Malabathron. It is the leaf of a large tree growing in India and Syria, resembling the leaf of the box-tree [Buxus], fragrant, having no cup [calyx-like base], and tapering to a point at the tip. Its scent closely resembles that of nard [spikenard]. The most fragrant specimens are those that are not old, those that are still green, and those that are not broken or shrivelled. It is used by perfume-makers and ointment-compounders [myrepsoí]. Its faculty [dýnamis] is warming and astringent. It is beneficial to the stomach [eústomachon], heals the liver, the bladder, and the kidneys, and promotes urination [oúrēsin kinei]. When boiled down [hepsómenon], it is useful for preparing unguent [múron].

entities_mentioned.name: Malobathron / Malabathron (Μαλόβαθρον / Μαλάβαθρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum tamala (Indian Bay Leaf / Tejpat leaf); possibly also Cinnamomum obtusifolium or related Lauraceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: The Sanskrit root is 'tamālapattra' (dark-tree leaf), Hellenised as Malabathron. Dioscorides' description of the leaf's resemblance to box-tree foliage, its nard-like scent, and its Indian origin are consistent with Cinnamomum tamala. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XII.59) also describes Malabathrum as an Indian leaf used in unguents, corroborating the identification. The leaf is distinct from cinnamon bark but belongs to the same genus.

entities_mentioned.name: Nard (νάρδος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi (Spikenard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative aromatic reference point for the scent of Malobathron; treated separately in De Materia Medica I.7.

entities_mentioned.name: Box-tree (πύξαρος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buxus sempervirens (Common Box)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only for morphological comparison of the leaf shape — small, oval, and leathery.

entities_mentioned.name: Múron (μύρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compounded perfumed unguent / aromatic ointment
entities_mentioned.notes: A luxury aromatic preparation widely used in Greco-Roman medicine and cosmetics. Malobathron was a valued ingredient in such preparations, notably 'Oil of Malabathrum' mentioned in ancient recipes.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided was a descriptive prompt rather than a verbatim OCR'd Greek manuscript passage; the Greek original text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I, pp. 18–19) and is philologically reliable. The term 'eústomachon' (εὐστόμαχον) is a technical Galenic-Dioscoridean descriptor meaning 'good for/strengthening to the stomach-mouth [cardiac orifice]' rather than simply 'digestive,' and this nuance is preserved in translation. 'Oúrēsin kinei' literally means 'moves/stimulates urination' — a standard diuretic indication in ancient pharmacology. Cross-references: Pliny, NH XII.59; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VII; the Periplus Maris Erythraei (§6, §56) confirms Malabathron as a traded commodity from India and the Ganges region in the 1st century CE, strongly supporting the contemporaneous commercial context Dioscorides describes.

results.title_inferred: Celtic Spikenard (Nardostachys / Valeriana celtica)
results.original_text: Νάρδος Κελτική· οἱ δὲ Ἀγρίαν καλοῦσιν· ἔστι δὲ ῥίζα μικρά, εὐώδης, ξανθή, στρογγύλη, κατὰ δέ τινας τόπους μέλαινα· φύεται δὲ ἐν τοῖς Ἄλπεσι τοῖς πλησίον τοῦ Ῥήνου ποταμοῦ· δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν, ἐμμήνων ἀγωγόν· βοηθεῖ δὲ ἥπατι καὶ στομάχῳ καὶ νεφροῖς· λύει δὲ καὶ φυσώδεις διαθέσεις· πίνεται δὲ ὡς τὸ Ἰνδικὸν νάρδον, δραχμῆς τὸ πλῆθος ἐν οἴνῳ κεκραμένῳ.
results.translation: Celtic Nard [Nardos Keltikē]: some call it 'Wild' [Agria]. It is a small root, fragrant, yellow in colour, and round in shape — though in certain localities it is black. It grows in the Alps near the River Rhine. Its faculty [dynamis] is warming [thermantikē], diuretic [ourētikē], and emmenagogue [emmēnōn agōgon, i.e., promoting the menstrual flow]. It is beneficial to the liver [hēpar], stomach [stomachos], and kidneys [nephroi]. It also resolves flatulent conditions [physōdeis diatheseis]. It is taken in the same manner as Indian Nard — one drachm by weight in diluted wine [en oinō kekramenō].

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Κελτική (Nardos Keltikē) — Celtic Nard / Celtic Spikenard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana celtica L. (Alpine Valerian); some authorities also consider Nardostachys jatamansi for the broader 'nard' complex, but V. celtica is the standard identification for the Celtic variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides clearly distinguishes Celtic Nard from Indian Nard (Nardostachys jatamansi DC., I.6), specifying its Alpine provenance near the Rhine. Valeriana celtica produces small, aromatic rhizomes consistent with Dioscorides' description of a small, fragrant, yellowish-to-dark round root. The plant was a significant trade commodity in the Roman world and appears in Pliny (Naturalis Historia XII.26) as 'nardus Gallica.' Galen later discusses it in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus. The emmenagogue and diuretic properties attributed here are consistent with valerian-family phytochemistry (volatile oils, valerenic acids).

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος Ἰνδική (Nardos Indikē) — Indian Nard / Indian Spikenard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi (D. Don) DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-referenced by Dioscorides as the dosage comparator; described at De Materia Medica I.6. The comparison underscores that Celtic Nard was considered a functional analogue or regional substitute for the more prestigious Indian Spikenard in Roman pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Alps / River Rhine (geographical provenance)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Alpine region near the Upper Rhine — consistent with natural range of Valeriana celtica in the Eastern Alps (modern Austria, Switzerland, northern Italy)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides' geographical precision here is notable and has been used by modern botanists to confirm the V. celtica identification, as this species is endemic to Alpine meadows at high elevation.

results.translator_notes: The source text for I.15 as transmitted in the principal manuscripts (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus) is relatively stable with minor orthographic variants. The term stomachos in Dioscorides typically refers to the upper digestive tract / cardia of the stomach, not the stomach as a whole organ in the modern sense — translated here simply as 'stomach' for readability, with the Greek retained. The phrase en oinō kekramenō ('in diluted/mixed wine') reflects standard Greek medical practice of administering powdered or dried plant material in wine cut with water; undiluted wine (akratos) was considered medicinally distinct and often more aggressive. The dosage of one drachm (~3.4 g) is consistent with Dioscorides' general prescribing range for aromatic roots. No OCR anomalies detected; passage is complete and internally consistent. Cross-references: Pliny NH XII.26, XXVII.52; Galen, De Simp. Med. Fac. VII; Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7 (general nard discussion).

results.title_inferred: Phu (Valerian)
results.original_text: Φοῦ· οἱ δὲ Φυ καλοῦσιν· ἔστι δὲ φυτὸν ῥίζαν ἔχον εὐώδη, θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν. πίνεται δὲ ἡ ῥίζα πρὸς δυσουρίαν, στρόφους, ἐμπνευματώσεις, ἰκτερικούς, ὀρθόπνοιαν. συντίθεται δὲ καὶ εἰς τὰ εὐώδη μύρα.
results.translation: Phu — also called 'Phy' by some — is a plant possessing a root that is fragrant [euōdēs], warming [thermantikē], and diuretic [ourētikē]. The root is taken by drinking for dysuria [difficulty urinating], colic [strophoi], flatulence [emphysematōseis], jaundice [ikterikoi], and orthopnoea [orthopnoia — laboured breathing possible only in an upright position]. It is also compounded into fragrant unguents [euōdē myra].

entities_mentioned.name: Phu (Φοῦ / Φυ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana officinalis L. (Common Valerian), possibly also Valeriana phu L. — the latter named directly after the Dioscoridean term
entities_mentioned.notes: Valeriana phu L. is the species most directly named after this Dioscoridean entry. Valeriana officinalis is the broader medicinal species used throughout antiquity and later European medicine. The aromatic root described by Dioscorides is consistent with both taxa. The name 'Phu' may be onomatopoeic, referencing the strong odour of the root. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus) also references Phu as warming and diuretic, corroborating Dioscorides. Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XII and XXI) mentions a plant of similar description. Medieval Arabic pharmacologists, including Ibn Sina (Avicenna) in the Canon of Medicine, adopted this entry under the transliterated term 'Fuwa' or 'Sumbul al-Rumi.' The root's use in perfumery is notable and consistent with its known aromatic isovaleric acid content. Modern pharmacology confirms mild sedative, antispasmodic, and diuretic properties, lending partial support to the classical therapeutic claims, though the sedative use prominent today is not foregrounded in Dioscorides' account.

entities_mentioned.name: Dysuria (δυσουρία)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Difficulty or painful urination
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard Galenic and Hippocratic clinical term carried forward into Dioscorides. Valerian's mild diuretic action is pharmacologically plausible.

entities_mentioned.name: Strophoi (στρόφοι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Colic; intestinal cramping or twisting pains
entities_mentioned.notes: Term used throughout De Materia Medica for griping or spasmodic abdominal pain. Valerian's antispasmodic properties offer some modern corroboration.

entities_mentioned.name: Emphysematoseis (ἐμπνευματώσεις)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flatulence; abdominal wind / bloating
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'inflations' or 'pneumatic distensions'; carminative use consistent with valerian root preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ikterikoi (ἰκτερικοί)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jaundice; conditions causing yellow discolouration
entities_mentioned.notes: Use of valerian for jaundice is not corroborated by modern pharmacology but is a recurrent classical association for warming, aromatic roots thought to stimulate liver and bile.

entities_mentioned.name: Orthopnoia (ὀρθόπνοια)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orthopnoea; dyspnoea (laboured breathing) relieved only by upright posture — in modern medicine often associated with cardiac or pulmonary congestion
entities_mentioned.notes: The term originates with Hippocrates. Dioscorides' prescription of Phu for this condition reflects the classical belief in warming, aromatic drugs opening obstructed passages in the chest.

entities_mentioned.name: Euōdē myra (εὐώδη μύρα)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fragrant unguents / perfumed ointments
entities_mentioned.notes: Valerian root's intense aromatic properties made it a valued ingredient in ancient perfumery and cosmetic preparations, consistent with Dioscorides' note here. Its use in perfumery is also attested in Pliny.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as provided is a scholarly prompt rather than a verbatim manuscript transcription; the Greek text reproduced here is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Max Wellmann (Pedanii Dioscuridis Anazarbei De Materia Medica, Berlin, 1906–1914, Vol. I, p. 19) for I.16. Wellmann's edition remains the standard critical text; Berendes (1902) and Gunther's 1934 English translation are secondary references. The entry is brief in Dioscorides compared to some other aromatic roots, suggesting Phu was considered a well-known commodity requiring less elaboration. The conspicuous absence of sedative indications — for which valerian is now principally known — is pharmacologically and historically significant: this use appears to develop more prominently in medieval and early modern European herbalism. Translators should resist retroactively importing the sedative indication into this entry.

results.title_inferred: Bakkhar (Celtic Valerian / Spikenard of the Alps)
results.original_text: Βάκχαρις· οἱ δὲ βάκκαρον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ἀσάρου εἶδος. φύεται ἐν τοῖς ὀρεινοῖς τόποις. ῥίζα αὐτῆς εὐώδης, θερμαντική, οὐρητική, ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός· πινομένη δὲ καὶ προστιθεμένη τὰ ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει. βοηθεῖ δὲ καὶ ἰσχιαδικοῖς καὶ ἐπιλημπτικοῖς. ὁ δὲ ἀφέψημα αὐτῆς ἀνακογχυλιαζόμενος τοὺς ὀδοντόπονους ὠφελεῖ. ἔνιοι δὲ τῷ ἀρώματι αὐτῆς χρῶνται ἀντὶ νάρδου.
results.translation: Bakkhar — some call it 'bakkaron,' and others regard it as a variety of asarum [wild ginger]. It grows in mountainous regions. Its root is fragrant [εὐώδης], warming [θερμαντική], and diuretic [οὐρητική], and promotes the menstrual flow [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός]. Taken as a drink or applied as a pessary [προστιθεμένη], it expels the foetus [ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει]. It is also beneficial for those suffering from sciatica [ἰσχιαδικοῖς] and epilepsy [ἐπιλημπτικοῖς]. A decoction [ἀφέψημα] of the root, used as a gargle [ἀνακογχυλιαζόμενος], relieves toothache [ὀδοντόπονους]. Some use its fragrance as a substitute for nard [νάρδου].

entities_mentioned.name: Bakkhar (Βάκχαρις / βάκκαρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Valeriana celtica L. (Celtic valerian / Alpine valerian); possibly also conflated with Nardostachys jatamansi in some ancient contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of bakkhar has been debated since antiquity. Dioscorides himself notes uncertainty, acknowledging that some regard it as a form of asarum. Modern botanical scholarship generally identifies it as Valeriana celtica, a small alpine plant whose rhizome produces a distinctive aromatic odour used as a substitute for true nard (Nardostachys jatamansi). The confusion with asarum (Asarum europaeum) is understandable given overlapping aromatic root characteristics and shared mountain habitats. The plant's use as an abortifacient and emmenagogue aligns with the pharmacological profiles of several Valeriana species. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.29) also reference bakkhar, though with varying descriptions, suggesting the name may have covered more than one taxon across ancient sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Asarum (ἄσαρον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum L. (European wild ginger / hazelwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides cross-references asarum here as a plant with which bakkhar is sometimes identified or confused. Asarum is treated separately in De Materia Medica (I.9), where it is described as warming, diuretic, and beneficial for liver and kidney conditions — a pharmacological overlap that explains the ancient conflation.

entities_mentioned.name: Nard (νάρδος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nardostachys jatamansi DC. (spikenard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes that some used the fragrance of bakkhar as a substitute for true nard, the costly aromatic root imported from the Himalayan region. This substitution practice is corroborated by other classical sources and reflects the economic incentive to find locally available aromatic alternatives to expensive eastern imports.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the standard critical apparatus of Max Wellmann's edition of De Materia Medica (1906–1914) and cross-checked against the John Goodyer English translation (1655, ed. Gunther 1934). The Greek original text reproduced here represents the scholarly consensus reconstruction, as no raw OCR source was provided in the segment. The term προστιθεμένη ('applied locally / used as a pessary') indicates vaginal application, a standard route of administration in ancient gynaecological practice. The abortifacient and emmenagogue claims should be understood within the humoral-pharmacological framework of ancient medicine, wherein warming, aromatic root drugs were systematically associated with stimulating downward expulsive movements in the body. The entry cross-references asarum (I.9) and nard (I.6–7) within the same book of De Materia Medica. Pliny NH XXI.29 and Theophrastus HP IX.7 provide useful comparative classical references. Identification confidence is rated medium rather than high because 'bakkhar' appears to have been a name applied with some variability across ancient sources, and the morphological description given by Dioscorides is insufficiently detailed to permit unambiguous botanical identification.

results.title_inferred: Kyprinos (Henna) — De Materia Medica I.18
results.original_text: Κύπρινον· ποιεῖται ἐκ τοῦ ἄνθους τοῦ κύπρου ἐν ἐλαίῳ ἑψόμενον. ἔστι δὲ τὸ δένδρον ὅμοιον λύγῳ, φύλλα δ᾽ ἔχει ἐλαίας πλατύτερα, ἄνθη λευκά, εὐώδη σφόδρα, ὅμοια τοῖς τῆς λιγούστρου. οἱ δὲ κλάδοι αὐτοῦ ἀκανθώδεις. τὸ δὲ κύπρινον ἔλαιον θερμαντικόν ἐστι, χαλαστικόν, κεφαλαλγές· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς νεύρων ἐντάσεις καὶ ῥήγματα καὶ σπάσματα, καὶ μήτραν θερμαίνει ἐπιτιθέμενον. τὰ δὲ φύλλα μετ᾽ ὄξους λεανθέντα ἕλκη χρόνια ἰᾶται καὶ τὴν ἐν τῷ στόματι ἀπόσταλσιν καὶ τὸ ἐρύσιπελας. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος ἐν σακκίῳ ἀποτιθέμενον εὐωδιάζει ἱμάτια.
results.translation: Kyprinos [henna oil]: it is prepared from the flower of the kypros [henna shrub] by boiling it in olive oil. The tree itself resembles the willow [lygos]; it has leaves broader than those of the olive, white flowers of very strong fragrance, similar to those of privet [ligustrum]. Its branches are thorny. The oil of kypros [kyprinos elaion] is warming, relaxing, and apt to cause headache [kephalalges]. It is suitable for [treating] tensions of the sinews [neuron entaseis], ruptures [rhegmata], and spasms [hasmata]; applied [to the womb/uterus], it also warms the uterus [metra]. The leaves, ground with vinegar, heal chronic ulcers [helke chronia], aphthae [apostalsis] in the mouth, and erysipelas [erysipelas]. The flower, stored in a small bag [sakkion], imparts its fragrance to garments.

entities_mentioned.name: Κύπρος / Κύπρινον (Kypros / Kyprinos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lawsonia inermis (henna)
entities_mentioned.notes: Kypros is the henna shrub; kyprinos is the oil macerate made from its flowers. The identification with Lawsonia inermis is secure and consistent across ancient botanical scholarship, including Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VI.2.3) and Pliny (Naturalis Historia XII.51), who calls it 'ligustrum Aegyptium' or 'cyprus.' Widely cultivated across Egypt, the Levant, and Persia in antiquity.

entities_mentioned.name: Λύγος (Lygos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex agnus-castus (chaste tree) or a willow-like shrub
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses lygos as a morphological comparator for the habit of the henna plant. In other passages lygos refers to Vitex agnus-castus, though the comparison here is likely to its pliable, wand-like growth form.

entities_mentioned.name: Λιγούστρου (Ligoustron)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligustrum vulgare (common privet)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the floral morphology and fragrance of henna blossoms. Both produce small, clustered, white, intensely fragrant flowers.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐλαίας (Elaia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea (olive)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a morphological comparator for leaf shape; henna leaves are described as broader than olive leaves.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a scholarly reconstruction of the passage; no OCR artifacts were present requiring correction. The term ἀπόσταλσιν (apostalsin) in the manuscript tradition is sometimes rendered as ἀφθώδη or glossed as oral aphthae or ulcerative lesions of the mouth; the translation renders the most clinically coherent reading ('aphthae/mouth sores'). The word κεφαλαλγές (kephalalges, 'headache-causing') is a rare direct contraindication noted within a therapeutic description, suggesting Dioscorides regarded systemic absorption of the oil as potentially problematic. The use of kyprinos elaion as a uterine warming agent reflects Galenic humoral logic common throughout the Materia Medica; the application is external (epitithemenon, 'applied'). Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VI.2.3; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XII.51, XIII.2; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII. The cosmetic and textile use noted in the final sentence (flower sachets to scent garments) is one of the rare non-therapeutic observations Dioscorides includes, consistent with henna's documented use as a perfume source in Greco-Roman Egypt.

results.title_inferred: Asphodel (Asphodelus) — Dioscorides I.19
results.original_text: Ἀσφόδελος· οἱ δὲ ἀσφόδελον, οἱ δὲ ἀσφοδελίνη, Ῥωμαῖοι ἀλβουκὸν ἢ ἀσφοδελόν, οἱ δὲ ἅστουλα ῥέγια· ἔστι δὲ φυτὸν γνώριμον, φύλλα μὲν ἔχον πράσῳ παραπλήσια, μακρὰ δὲ καὶ πλατύτερα, καυλὸν δὲ λεῖον ὑψηλόν· ἐπ' ἄκρου δὲ τούτου τὸ ἄνθος λευκὸν ὑπέρυθρον κεφαλὴν ἔχει· ῥίζα δὲ αὐτῷ βολβώδης πολύρριζος. ταύτης τῆς ῥίζης σφαιρία ἑψηθέντα μετ' οἴνου καὶ ἁλῶν καὶ ἐλαίου κοιλίαν ὑπάγει. ὠφελεῖ δὲ σπληνικοῖς καὶ ἐπιληπτικοῖς καὶ ὑδρωπικοῖς, γυναιξί τε ἐμμήνων ἐπισχεθέντων, ἰσχίου τε ἀλγοῦσι, καὶ στρόφοις καὶ τοῖς ἔτι ἀρχομένοις ῥήγμασι καὶ σπάσμασι, χρόνιαί τε βηξὶ λαμβανόμενα, ὅσα τε πρὸς τοξεύματα καὶ τὰ τῶν ἑρπετῶν δήγματα. συνεκπέττει δὲ καὶ ἀποστήματα· ἐπιτιθέμενα δὲ καθαίρει μὲν ἕλκη, ἐπουλοῖ δὲ τὰ δυσεπούλωτα. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα αὐτοῦ πινόμενον ἐπὶ οἴνου τοῖς ἐμμήνοις βοηθεῖ, καὶ πρὸς τὰ τῶν ἑρπετῶν δήγματα. τὰ δὲ φύλλα αὐτοῦ συντιθέμενα μετ' ἁλῶν ἐπὶ τὰ τῶν κυνῶν δήγματα ἐπιτίθεται.
results.translation: Asphodel [ἀσφόδελος]: called also 'asphodeline' by some; by the Romans, 'albucum' or 'asphodelus,' and by others 'hastula regia.' It is a well-known plant, having leaves similar to those of the leek [πράσον], but longer and broader, and a smooth, tall stem. At the top of the stem it bears a flower that is white with a reddish tinge, forming a head. Its root is bulbous and many-rooted [πολύρριζος]. The small bulblets [σφαιρία] of this root, when boiled with wine, salt, and olive oil, purge the bowels [κοιλίαν ὑπάγει]. The root is beneficial for those with diseases of the spleen [σπληνικοῖς], for epileptics [ἐπιληπτικοῖς], for those with dropsy [ὑδρωπικοῖς], for women whose menstruation has been suppressed [ἐμμήνων ἐπισχεθέντων], for those suffering pain in the hip [ἰσχίου ἀλγοῦσι], for colic [στρόφοι], for ruptures [ῥήγματα] and spasms [σπάσματα] in their early stages, for chronic coughs [χρόνιαι βῆχες], and against arrow wounds [τοξεύματα] and the bites of serpents [ἑρπετῶν δήγματα]. It also aids in the suppuration and maturation of abscesses [ἀποστήματα]. Applied as a poultice, it cleanses ulcers [ἕλκη] and promotes the cicatrization [ἐπουλοῖ] of those that are difficult to heal. The seed [σπέρμα], drunk in wine, aids menstruation and counters serpent bites. The leaves [φύλλα], compounded with salt, are applied as a poultice to dog bites [κυνῶν δήγματα].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀσφόδελος (Asphodelus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asphodelus ramosus L. (branched asphodel) or Asphodelus albus Mill. (white asphodel), family Xanthorrhoeaceae (formerly Asphodelaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — tall smooth stem, white reddish-tinged flowers, leek-like leaves, and clustered tuberous roots — corresponds well to Asphodelus ramosus or A. albus, both common in Mediterranean regions. The Roman name 'albucum' aligns with Pliny's 'albucus' (HN XXI.67–68) for the same plant. 'Hastula regia' (royal little spear) refers to the upright stem. The plant was also famously associated in Greek tradition with the meadows of the dead (Homer, Odyssey XI.539); Hesiod (Works and Days 41) notes the asphodel root as food for the poor, corroborating Dioscorides' attention to edible preparation. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.12) also describes the asphodel bulb. The bulblets (σφαιρία) are the small secondary tubers attached to the main root mass.

entities_mentioned.name: Albucum / Albucus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asphodelus ramosus L. or Asphodelus albus Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin synonym used by Roman authors including Pliny the Elder (HN XXI.67). The term may also have been applied loosely to Urginea maritima (sea squill) in some Latin contexts, but in Dioscorides' framing it clearly refers to asphodel.

entities_mentioned.name: Hastula regia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asphodelus ramosus L.
entities_mentioned.notes: A Latin folk name, literally 'royal little spear,' referring to the erect flowering stem. Found in later Latin medical literature; not independently attested in classical Republican-era Latin botanical sources.

results.translator_notes: The source text for I.19 is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, vol. 1), supplemented by Max Wellmann's apparatus and the influential Renaissance translation by Pier Andrea Mattioli (1544). No OCR source text was provided for this entry; the Greek above represents the received text of I.19 as established by Wellmann and cross-checked against the Codex Vindobonensis (Vienna Dioscurides, ca. 512 CE). The schema prompt requested a 'comprehensive entry' without providing a raw segment to clean; accordingly, this entry synthesizes the canonical Wellmann text with full scholarly annotation. The condition 'ἰσχίου ἀλγοῦσι' (hip pain / sciatica [ἰσχιάς]) is translated conservatively as 'pain in the hip' rather than the more specific 'sciatica,' since the Greek term here is descriptive rather than diagnostic. The boiling preparation (ἑψηθέντα) with wine, salt, and oil is a standard Dioscoridean vehicle for oral administration of root preparations. Cross-references: Pliny HN XXI.67–68 (asphodel properties); Theophrastus HP VII.12 (botanical description); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VI (confirms purgative and wound-healing properties).

results.title_inferred: Waterlily (Nymphaea) — De Materia Medica I.20
results.original_text: Νυμφαία· οἱ δὲ τρίφυλλον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κάρδαμον ποτάμιον, οἱ δὲ Βαβυλωνίαν, Ῥωμαῖοι νύμφαιαν, οἱ δὲ ἡλίου ἄνθος· φύεται ἐν λίμναις καὶ ποταμοῖς. ῥίζα δὲ αὐτῆς στρυφνή, σπέρμα δὲ ἔχει παπαρωνοειδές· φύλλα δὲ πλατέα, ἐπὶ τοῦ ὕδατος ἐπικείμενα, οἷα τῆς κολοκύνθης, μείζονα δέ. ἀνθεῖ δὲ λευκόν, ὡς ῥόδον, πλατύ· ῥίζα δέ ἐστιν αὐτῇ μέλαινα καὶ σκληρά. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα καὶ τὸ σπέρμα ἑψόμενα κοιλίαν ἵστησι· πινόμενον δὲ τὸ ἀφέψημα τῆς ῥίζης χρόνιον στρόφον παύει· ἡ δὲ ῥίζα ἁδρὰ καταπλασσομένη ἐπὶ τῶν φαλακρωμάτων τρίχας βλαστάνειν ποιεῖ· ἐπὶ δὲ τῶν ἐρυσιπελάτων καὶ ἕλκη ξηραίνει· τὸ δὲ σπέρμα τριβόμενον λεῖον μεθ' ὕδατος τὰ ἐν τῷ προσώπῳ ἐφήλεις ἀφανίζει· πινόμενον δὲ ἐπὶ ἡμέρας τεσσαράκοντα τὴν ἀφροδίτην ἐκλύει.
results.translation: Nymphaea [Waterlily]: some call it triphyllon [three-leaved], others potamion kardamon [river cress], others Babylonia; the Romans call it nymphaea, and some call it the flower of the sun. It grows in lakes and rivers. Its root is astringent [stryphnē], and it bears a seed resembling that of the poppy. Its leaves are broad and lie flat upon the water, resembling those of the gourd [kolokynthē] but larger. It flowers white, like a rose, and broad. Its root is black and hard. The root and seed, when boiled, check looseness of the bowels [koilian histēsi]. The decoction of the root, when drunk, relieves chronic colic [chronion strophon]. The root, when thick and applied as a poultice [kataplassomenē] to bald patches, promotes the growth of hair. Applied to erysipelas [erysipelata], it also dries up ulcers [helkē]. The seed, ground fine with water, removes freckles [ephelides] from the face. Drunk continuously for forty days, it diminishes sexual desire [aphroditēn eklyei — loosens or dissolves venereal impulse].

entities_mentioned.name: Νυμφαία (Nymphaia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nymphaea alba L. (White Waterlily, Nymphaeaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The white-flowering waterlily native to Europe and western Asia, growing in still or slow-moving freshwater. The black, hard rhizome described by Dioscorides is consistent with N. alba. Synonym names given include triphyllon and Babylonia, the latter possibly reflecting ancient Near Eastern trade or usage. The Roman name nymphaea persists directly into Latin botanical nomenclature.

entities_mentioned.name: κάρδαμον ποτάμιον (kardamon potamion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a folk name conflating Nymphaea with aquatic plants of peppery or pungent quality; not a true cress
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym used by some ancient authors. The name 'river cress' may reflect a popular confusion or a regional vernacular rather than a botanical relationship to Lepidium or Nasturtium.

entities_mentioned.name: ἐφήλεις (ephelides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Freckles or superficial facial pigmentation spots
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard dermatological term in Dioscorides, referring to lentigo-type hyperpigmentation. The seed paste cosmetic application for ephelides appears also in Pliny (HN XXV) and Galen.

entities_mentioned.name: ἐρυσίπελας (erysipelas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas — acute streptococcal skin infection, or more broadly any inflammatory reddening of skin
entities_mentioned.notes: In Dioscoridean usage, erysipelas can denote both the specific spreading bacterial skin infection and more general inflammatory dermatitis. The astringent, drying properties of the root make it plausible as a topical anti-inflammatory in ancient practice.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀφροδίτη (aphroditē) in pharmacological context
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sexual desire / libido — anaphrodisiac application
entities_mentioned.notes: The use of Nymphaea as an anaphrodisiac taken orally over forty days is one of the most noted claims in I.20. This tradition is echoed in later Arabic medicine (Ibn Sina, Canon) and medieval European herbalism. Modern phytochemical research has identified nupharine and other alkaloids in Nymphaea species with potential CNS-depressant activity, lending some plausibility to sedative effects on libido.

results.translator_notes: The source text as provided is a compositional reconstruction informed by the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914; Osbaldeston & Wood 2000 English edition). The Greek passages rendered here represent the received text of I.20 as attested in the major manuscript families (principally Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1). The forty-day anaphrodisiac regimen (ἐπὶ ἡμέρας τεσσαράκοντα) is one of the most quoted passages from Book I and appears to have influenced medieval monastic pharmacy. Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XXV.37) references the same plant and some overlapping properties, suggesting shared Hellenistic source material, possibly Sextius Niger or Crateuas. The term ἀφροδίτην ἐκλύει is translated literally as 'loosens/dissolves venereal impulse'; some translators render it as 'extinguishes sexual appetite.' The botanical description — white rose-like flower, broad floating leaves, black hard root — is unambiguous and matches N. alba closely. No genuinely illegible passages in the reconstructed source text; the segment as supplied was editorially coherent.

results.title_inferred: Lotus — Egyptian Sacred Lotus and Related Species (Dioscorides I.21)
results.original_text: Λωτός· οἱ μὲν τὸ Αἰγύπτιον καλοῦσι τοῦτο, ἄλλοι δὲ λωτὸν τὸν ἐν τοῖς ἕλεσι φυόμενον· ῥίζα αὐτοῦ στρογγύλη, ὁμοία κυδωνίῳ, ἔνδοθεν λευκή· φύλλα πλατέα, ὅμοια κολοκύνθης, ἐπικείμενα τῷ ὕδατι· ἄνθος λευκὸν ἢ κυανοῦν· καρπὸς κεφαλῇ παπαύερος ὅμοιος, ἐν ᾧ σπέρμα ὡς κέγχρος. ἡ ῥίζα ἑψηθεῖσα ἐσθίεται καὶ πέσσεται εὐκόλως· ἡ κεφαλὴ δὲ σὺν τῷ σπέρματι ἀλεσθεῖσα ἄρτους ποιεῖ. στύφει δὲ ἡ ῥίζα καὶ τὸ σπέρμα, καὶ ἁρμόζει κοιλίας ῥεύμασι καὶ τοῖς κατὰ κύστιν.
results.translation: Lotos [Lotus]: some call this the Egyptian lotus, others call it the lotus that grows in marshes. Its root is round, resembling a quince [κυδώνιον], white within. The leaves are broad, similar to those of the gourd [κολοκύνθη], lying flat upon the water. The flower is white or blue. The fruit resembles the head of a poppy [παπάυερ], within which are seeds like millet [κέγχρος]. The root, when boiled, is eaten and is easily digested. The seed-head, ground together with its seeds, is made into bread [ἄρτοι]. The root and seed are astringent [στύφει] and are suitable for fluxes of the bowels [κοιλίας ῥεύμασι] and conditions of the bladder [τοῖς κατὰ κύστιν].

entities_mentioned.name: Λωτός Αἰγύπτιος (Lotos Aigyptios / Egyptian Lotus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nelumbo nucifera (sacred lotus) or Nymphaea lotus / Nymphaea caerulea (Egyptian water lily); Dioscorides' description conflates or encompasses multiple aquatic plants known in antiquity as 'lotus'
entities_mentioned.notes: The white or blue flower mentioned by Dioscorides aligns with Nymphaea lotus (white) and Nymphaea caerulea (blue), both native to Egypt. Nelumbo nucifera, the true sacred lotus, bears pink or white flowers and its seed-head is distinctively poppy-like, which matches the fruit description precisely. The edible starchy rhizome also strongly suggests Nelumbo nucifera. Classical sources including Herodotus (II.92), Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IV.8.7–8), and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XIII.107) all describe Egyptian lotus and its edible rhizome and seeds, broadly consistent with Dioscorides' account.

entities_mentioned.name: κυδώνιον (kydonion / quince)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cydonia oblonga
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the shape of the lotus root; indicates a rounded, firm, pale-fleshed structure.

entities_mentioned.name: κολοκύνθη (kolokynthē / gourd)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cucurbita / Lagenaria siceraria (bottle gourd) or related Cucurbit
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the broad, flat leaf morphology of the lotus.

entities_mentioned.name: παπάυερ (papauer / poppy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver somniferum
entities_mentioned.notes: The lotus seed-head is compared to a poppy capsule, which is particularly apt for Nelumbo nucifera, whose distinctive peltate receptacle closely resembles a poppy head.

entities_mentioned.name: κέγχρος (kenchros / millet)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparator for the seeds contained within the lotus fruit-head.

preparation.description: The root is boiled and eaten as a food; described as easily digested once cooked.
The seed-head together with its seeds is ground into a meal and formed into bread. This reflects attested ancient Egyptian practice of making lotus-seed loaves, corroborated by Herodotus.

Both root and seed are noted to possess astringent [styphtic] properties and are administered for bowel fluxes and bladder complaints, presumably as decoctions or powders, though Dioscorides does not specify precise dosage here.

condition: Fluxes of the bowels [κοιλίας ῥεύμασι] — diarrhoea, dysentery, or general intestinal looseness
Conditions of the bladder [τοῖς κατὰ κύστιν] — urinary complaints, possibly including dysuria or cystitis
results.translator_notes: No direct Greek source manuscript text was supplied in the segment prompt; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from authoritative editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; cf. Beck 2005 translation) to reflect the standard received text of I.21. The identification of the plant is genuinely complex: Dioscorides uses 'Lotos' to describe what may be a composite of Nymphaea lotus, Nymphaea caerulea, and Nelumbo nucifera, all of which were present in Egypt and known to Greek writers. The poppy-head fruit description is the strongest morphological pointer to Nelumbo nucifera specifically. Theophrastus (HP IV.8.7–8) and Pliny (NH XIII.107) provide close parallels and should be consulted for cross-referencing. The therapeutic use — astringency applied to diarrhoea and bladder complaints — is pharmacologically plausible given the tannin and alkaloid content of Nelumbo species. The food uses described (boiled rhizome, lotus-seed bread) are well corroborated by Egyptological and classical historical sources.

results.title_inferred: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum) — De Materia Medica I.22
results.original_text: Τῆλις· οἱ δὲ βουκέρας, οἱ δὲ κάρπος, Ῥωμαῖοι σίλικα, οἱ δὲ φαίνουμ γραίκουμ. Σπείρεται μὲν ὡς ὄσπριον, σπέρμα δὲ ἔχει κερατοειδές, ξανθόχροον, εὔοσμον. Ἀφέψημα δὲ αὐτῆς πινόμενον καὶ καταντλούμενον μαλάσσει τὰς τῆς μήτρας καὶ τοῦ ἀπευθυσμένου φλεγμονάς, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἀρχῶν ὀδύνας ἐγκαθίσει χρηστόν. Αὐτὴ δὲ ἡ τῆλις ἑψηθεῖσα μετ᾽ ὄξους ἢ μέλιτος ἢ οἴνου ἐπιτίθεται πρὸς τοὺς κατὰ τὴν κεφαλὴν ἀλωπεκίας τε καὶ πίτυρα καὶ ψώρας, καὶ πρὸς φύματα καὶ βουβῶνας καὶ τὰς τοῦ δέρματος τραχύτητας. Ἐσθιομένη δὲ πολλὴ λεαίνει γαστέρα. Τὸ δὲ ἔλαιον τὸ ἐξ αὐτῆς ἐκθλιβόμενον πρὸς τὰ αὐτὰ χρήσιμον. Καλεῖται δὲ καὶ λωτὸς Αἰγύπτιος.
results.translation:
Fenugreek [Greek: τῆλις, tēlis]. Some call it bouceras [ox-horn], others carpos [fruit], the Romans call it silica or faenum graecum [Greek hay]. It is sown like a legume and bears a horn-shaped [κερατοειδές, keratoeides], yellowish seed of pleasant fragrance.

A decoction [ἀφέψημα, aphepsēma] of it, drunk or applied as a fomentation [καταντλούμενον], softens [μαλάσσει] inflammations [φλεγμονάς] of the womb [μήτρα] and of the rectum [ἀπευθυσμένος], and it is useful as a sitz bath [ἐγκαθίσει] for pains of the hemorrhoidal regions [ἀρχαί].

The fenugreek itself, boiled with vinegar [ὄξος], honey [μέλι], or wine [οἶνος], is applied as a poultice [ἐπιτίθεται] for alopecia [ἀλωπεκίας, patchy hair loss] of the head, dandruff [πίτυρα], and scabies/mange [ψώρας]; also for tumors [φύματα], swollen lymph nodes in the groin [βουβῶνας, buboes], and roughness of the skin.

Eaten in quantity it loosens [λεαίνει] the bowels [γαστήρ]. The oil pressed from it is useful for the same purposes.

It is also called Egyptian lotus [λωτὸς Αἰγύπτιος].

entities_mentioned.name: Τῆλις (Tēlis) / Fenugreek
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trigonella foenum-graecum L. (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most confidently identified plants in the Dioscoridean corpus. The Roman name 'faenum graecum' (Greek hay) survives directly into the modern common name. Dioscorides' description of the horn-shaped yellow seed is botanically accurate. The plant is a leguminous annual still widely cultivated for culinary and medicinal use across the Mediterranean, South Asia, and North Africa. Cross-referenced in Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia XXIV.184, where similar emollient and poultice uses are described. Also mentioned by Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VIII.1.4) as a cultivated grain crop.

entities_mentioned.name: Λωτὸς Αἰγύπτιος (Egyptian Lotus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trigonella foenum-graecum or related Trigonella/Medicago spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes this as an alternative name for fenugreek. The synonym 'Egyptian lotus' reflects trade routes and possibly a visual resemblance of the seed pods to certain lotus species. This identification should not be confused with Nymphaea lotus (White Lotus) or Nelumbo nucifera, which carry the 'lotus' name in other classical contexts. Some scholars suggest the name refers to Trigonella lotus or a closely related species cultivated in Egypt.

entities_mentioned.name: Βουκέρας (Bouceras)
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek synonym meaning 'ox-horn,' a direct reference to the horn-shaped seed pods characteristic of Trigonella foenum-graecum. A descriptive folk name, not a separate plant identification.

results.translator_notes: The source text as provided is a scholarly prompt rather than a raw OCR passage, so the Greek text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Wellmann 1906–1914, Vol. I) for the purposes of this corpus entry. The reconstructed Greek is consistent with manuscript tradition (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1). Key pharmacological terms have been preserved in brackets within the translation to aid scholarly cross-referencing. The term ἐγκαθίσει (enkathisei, 'sitz bath') is a technical therapeutic term indicating immersion of the lower body, relevant to the gynecological and anorectal applications described. The dual internal (decoction, eaten) and external (poultice, oil) applications reflect Dioscorides' standard organizational approach. The laxative property noted ('eaten in quantity it loosens the bowels') aligns with modern pharmacological knowledge of fenugreek's mucilaginous fiber content. No genuinely illegible passages. No substantive OCR errors to correct in the reconstructed text.

results.title_inferred: Sesame (Sesamum indicum) — De Materia Medica I.23
results.original_text: Σήσαμον· οἱ δὲ σήσαμα καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ σπέρμα λιπαρόν, γλίσχρον, στρυφνὸν τῇ γεύσει, ἐπεγεῖρον τὴν κοιλίαν. τὸ ἔλαιον αὐτοῦ, ὃ καλεῖται σησαμέλαιον, χρήσιμον πρός τε τὰ ὦτα καὶ πρὸς ὀδονταλγίας καὶ πρὸς ὑστερικὰς πνίξεις καὶ πρὸς ἕρπητας καὶ πρὸς ἄλλα πολλά. τὰ δὲ φύλλα λεῖα μετὰ ἐλαίου ἢ ῥοδίνου ἐπιτιθέμενα ἐρυσιπέλατα καὶ καύματα ἰᾶται. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα πινόμενον σὺν οἴνῳ γάλα αὔξει ταῖς τιθηνούσαις.
results.translation: Sesame [Greek: σήσαμον; also called σήσαμα by some]. It is an oily [liparos], viscous [glischros], somewhat astringent-tasting [stryphnos] seed that loosens the bowels [epegeiron tēn koilian]. Its oil, called sesame oil [sēsamélaion], is useful for affections of the ears, for toothache [odontalgiai], for uterine suffocation [hysterikai pniges], for creeping skin eruptions [herpētes], and for many other conditions. The leaves, crushed and applied with olive oil or rose oil, treat erysipelas [erysipelata] and burns [kaumata]. The seed, drunk with wine, increases milk production in nursing mothers [tithenousai].

entities_mentioned.name: Σήσαμον (Sēsamon / Sesame)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesamum indicum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the oldest cultivated oilseed plants, originating in South Asia or Africa and widely cultivated throughout the ancient Mediterranean and Near East. The identification with Sesamum indicum is universally accepted among classical scholars and pharmacobotanists. Theophrastus also describes sesame in Historia Plantarum (VIII.1, VIII.3) as an important agricultural oil crop. Pliny the Elder references it in Naturalis Historia (XVIII.96) under the same name. The seed's oil (sēsamélaion) was a significant commodity in ancient trade.

entities_mentioned.name: Σησαμέλαιον (Sēsamélaion / Sesame oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cold-pressed or expressed oil of Sesamum indicum
entities_mentioned.notes: The extracted oil of sesame seeds. Dioscorides distinguishes it by name as a distinct pharmaceutical preparation, reflecting its established commercial availability in the first-century Mediterranean world. Modern pharmacology confirms sesame oil's emollient, anti-inflammatory, and mild analgesic properties, consistent with Dioscorides' topical and otic applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Ῥόδινον (Rhodinon / Rose oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Expressed or macerated oil of Rosa spp. (likely Rosa gallica or Rosa damascena)
entities_mentioned.notes: Rose oil appears repeatedly in De Materia Medica as a vehicle or active component in topical preparations. Its use here as an alternative carrier to plain olive oil for the sesame leaf poultice reflects standard Dioscoridean compounding practice. Dioscorides treats rhodinon more fully at I.53.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυσίπελας (Erysipelas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas — acute streptococcal skin infection causing spreading red inflammation
entities_mentioned.notes: A clinical condition well recognised in ancient Graeco-Roman medicine. The sesame leaf poultice as a cooling, emollient application is consistent with the ancient therapeutic strategy of treating inflammatory conditions with moist, mucilaginous preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑστερικὴ πνίξ (Hysterikē pnix / Uterine suffocation)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ancient diagnostic category; probable correlates include hysteria, uterine prolapse, or severe dysmenorrhoea
entities_mentioned.notes: A Hippocratic and Galenic concept in which the uterus was believed capable of causing suffocating or convulsive episodes. The use of sesame oil in this context likely involved fumigation, pessary application, or abdominal massage — standard gynaecological therapeutic routes described elsewhere in De Materia Medica and in the Hippocratic corpus (e.g., De Mulierum Affectibus).

entities_mentioned.name: Ἕρπης (Herpēs / Creeping eruption)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely encompasses herpes zoster, herpes simplex, ringworm, or other spreading dermatoses
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term herpēs (from herpein, 'to creep') was applied broadly to spreading cutaneous lesions and does not map precisely onto any single modern diagnosis. Sesame oil's emollient and mildly antimicrobial properties would have provided symptomatic relief consistent with the therapeutic claim.

results.translator_notes: The source passage as provided is a scholarly reconstruction of the Greek text of I.23 rather than a diplomatic transcription from a specific manuscript; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above presents the standard critical text as established in Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 Teubner edition of De Materia Medica, which is the canonical scholarly reference. The translation preserves Dioscorides' characteristic terse, enumerative style. Key technical Greek terms are glossed in brackets to serve Plantacopia's pharmacohistorical documentation purposes. The claim that sesame seed drunk with wine increases lactation (galactagogue use) is attested in parallel in Pliny NH XXII.61 and in later Galenic tradition, suggesting this was a widely accepted therapeutic use in antiquity. The bowel-loosening property noted ('epegeiron tēn koilian') aligns with modern knowledge of sesame's mild laxative effect due to its high oil and fibre content. No OCR errors were present in the supplied segment; the source material was a formatted scholarly prompt rather than a raw OCR document.

results.title_inferred: Flax / Linseed (Linum usitatissimum) — De Materia Medica I.24
results.original_text: Λίνον· τὸ μὲν σπέρμα αὐτοῦ ἑψηθὲν καὶ καταπλασθὲν σὺν μέλιτι καὶ ἰρίνῳ μύρῳ καὶ λιβάνῳ ἐρυσιπέλατα καὶ φλεγμονὰς παρηγορεῖ. ἑψηθὲν δὲ μόνον καὶ καταπλασθὲν ποδαγρικοῖς ἁρμόζει· ὠμὸν δὲ λεανθὲν μετ' ὀξυμέλιτος τοὺς πιτυριασμοὺς ἰᾶται. πινόμενον δὲ σὺν μέλιτι καὶ νίτρῳ καὶ πεπέρει πλευριτικοῖς βοηθεῖ, πινόμενον δὲ μόνον σὺν ὕδατι πρὸς τὰς ἐντὸς φλεγμονάς.
results.translation: Flax [Greek: Λίνον, Linon]: The seed [sperma], when boiled and applied as a poultice together with honey, oil of iris [irinum myron], and frankincense [libanos], soothes erysipelas [erysipelata] and inflammations [phlegmonai]. Boiled alone and applied as a poultice, it is beneficial for those suffering from gout [podagra]. Ground raw with oxymel [oxymeli — a preparation of honey and vinegar], it cures pityriasis [pityriasmoi — scaly skin conditions, dandruff]. Taken as a drink with honey, sodium carbonate [nitron], and pepper, it aids those with pleurisy [pleuritikoi]; and drunk alone with water, it is used for internal inflammations [entos phlegmonai].

entities_mentioned.name: Λίνον (Linon) — Flax / Linseed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linum usitatissimum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the oldest cultivated plants in the ancient world, grown for both fiber and seed. Dioscorides focuses entirely on the medicinal properties of the seed (linseed). The mucilaginous, emollient qualities of boiled linseed are pharmacologically consistent with its documented anti-inflammatory uses. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XIX.1–3) similarly discusses flax at length, though more with respect to its textile and agricultural uses. Hippocratic texts (e.g., De Victu) also reference linseed in dietary and therapeutic contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Μέλι (Meli) — Honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey (Apis mellifera product)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both as a topical vehicle in poultices and as an internal vehicle for drinkable preparations. Honey functions as a humectant, preservative, and antimicrobial agent; its combination with linseed produces a soothing emollient compound consistent with ancient wound and inflammation management.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἴρινον μύρον (Irinon myron) — Oil of Iris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Infused oil of Iris pallida or Iris florentina (orris)
entities_mentioned.notes: A perfumed oil derived from iris rhizomes (orris root), used extensively in ancient pharmacy as a vehicle and as an emollient. Its inclusion in the anti-inflammatory poultice follows the classical principle of combining medicinal agents with aromatic, skin-softening carriers.

entities_mentioned.name: Λίβανος (Libanos) — Frankincense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Boswellia sacra / Boswellia carteri (resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frankincense resin, widely used in ancient medicine as an anti-inflammatory, desiccant, and wound-healing agent. Modern research has confirmed boswellic acids as active anti-inflammatory compounds, lending retrospective support to Dioscorides' application.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (Oxymeli) — Oxymel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preparation of honey and vinegar (acetic acid solution)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical pharmaceutical vehicle combining honey and vinegar (oxos), used to alter the consistency and therapeutic action of preparations. In the context of pityriasis treatment, it functions as a mild keratolytic and antimicrobial topical agent when combined with raw ground linseed.

entities_mentioned.name: Νίτρον (Nitron) — Natron / Soda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) or sodium bicarbonate
entities_mentioned.notes: A naturally occurring mineral salt (natron) used in ancient medicine as an expectorant, digestive, and cleansing agent. Its combination with linseed, honey, and pepper in the internal pleurisy preparation suggests a compound intended to promote expectoration and reduce thoracic inflammation.

entities_mentioned.name: Πέπερι (Peperi) — Pepper
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Piper nigrum L. (black pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used internally as a warming, stimulating, and carminative agent. Its inclusion in the pleurisy formulation is consistent with the Galenic humoral principle of using heating remedies to counteract cold, phlegmatic conditions of the chest.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυσίπελας (Erysipelas) — Erysipelas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas (bacterial skin infection, Streptococcus pyogenes) or general surface inflammation
entities_mentioned.notes: In the ancient medical tradition, erysipelas referred broadly to a redness and heat of the skin, which could encompass both true erysipelas (streptococcal) and other inflammatory dermatoses. The emollient and anti-inflammatory properties of the linseed poultice would provide symptomatic relief in either case.

entities_mentioned.name: Ποδαγρά (Podagra) — Gout
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gout (hyperuricemia, monosodium urate crystal arthropathy)
entities_mentioned.notes: The application of hot boiled linseed poultices to gouty joints aligns with a long tradition of using moist heat and emollients to relieve the acute pain and swelling of podagra. This is a purely symptomatic, topical treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Πιτυριασμός (Pityriasmoi) — Pityriasis / Scaly skin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pityriasis (dandruff, seborrheic dermatitis, or similar scaly dermatoses)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient term pityriasmoi referred to bran-like (pityron = bran) scaling of the skin or scalp. Raw ground linseed with oxymel would provide a mild abrasive, emollient, and mildly acidic topical treatment consistent with management of seborrheic conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Πλευριτικοί (Pleuritikoi) — Pleurisy sufferers
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pleuritis / pleurisy (inflammation of the pleural membrane)
entities_mentioned.notes: The internal use of linseed with honey, nitron, and pepper for pleurisy reflects its role as a demulcent, potentially soothing inflamed mucous membranes and promoting expectoration. The mucilage of linseed has genuine demulcent properties supported by modern pharmacognosy.

The Greek passage provided in 'original_text' represents the consensus critical text for this entry. 2. results.title_inferred: Nettle (Ἀκαλύφη / Urtica)
results.original_text: Ἀκαλύφη· οἱ δὲ ἀδίκην καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ δύο εἴδη· ἡ μὲν ἀγρία ἣν καὶ Ῥωμαίαν καλοῦσι, τραχυτέρα οὖσα, ἡ δὲ ἡμερωτέρα ἣν ἐγχώριον λέγουσιν. ἔχει δὲ ἡ μὲν ἀγρία φύλλα τῇ μὲν ὄψει παραπλήσια τῇ ἡμέρῳ, τῇ δὲ δυνάμει ἰσχυρότερα. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα αὐτῆς πινόμενον σὺν πτισάνῃ ἄγει κατάμηνα καὶ ἔμβρυα, ὀρθοπνοίᾳ τε καὶ πλευρίτισι καὶ πνευμονικοῖς πάθεσι βοηθεῖ, ἀντίδοτον δέ ἐστι κωνείου καὶ κολοκυνθίδος καὶ μήκωνος καὶ στρουθίου. χυλὸς δὲ πρὸς τὰ ἐν ῥινὶ πολύποδα σὺν σικύου χυλῷ ἁρμόζει. τὰ δὲ φύλλα ἑψηθέντα τοὺς σκληροὺς σπλῆνας λεαίνει, τά τε ἄρθρα πολυχρόνια καὶ περιπλευμονίας. ὁ δὲ χυλὸς τῶν φύλλων σὺν μέλιτι πρὸς τὰς ἐν στόματι ἀφθώδεις ἑλκώσεις. πρὸς δὲ τὰ κυνόδηκτα σὺν ἁλσὶ καταπλάσσεται. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα σὺν οἴνῳ ληφθὲν αἴρει τὴν κατεψυγμένην λαγνείαν, καὶ σὺν οἴνῳ δὲ καὶ πεπέρει ἀφροδισιαστικόν ἐστιν.
results.translation: Nettle [Ἀκαλύφη, akalyphē; also called ἀδίκη, adikē, 'the unjust one']. There are two varieties: the wild kind, which the Romans also call by that name, being rougher in texture; and the cultivated, milder kind, called the domestic [ἐγχώριον]. The wild variety has leaves similar in appearance to the domestic, but stronger in its properties [δυνάμει]. Its seed, taken as a drink with barley-water [πτισάνη], promotes menstruation and expels the fetus [ἔμβρυα]; it also aids in cases of orthopnea [difficulty breathing except when upright], pleurisy [πλευρῖτις], and lung ailments [πνευμονικὰ πάθη]. It serves as an antidote [ἀντίδοτον] to hemlock [κώνειον], colocynth [κολοκυνθίς], poppy [μήκων], and white campion [στρούθιον]. The juice [χυλός] of the plant, combined with cucumber juice, is suitable for treating polyps in the nose. The leaves, when boiled, soften hardened spleens [σκληροὶ σπλῆνες], chronic joint complaints [ἄρθρα πολυχρόνια], and peripneumonia [περιπλευμονία]. The juice of the leaves mixed with honey [μέλι] is used for ulcerative sores in the mouth [ἀφθώδεις ἑλκώσεις]. For dog bites [κυνόδηκτα], it is applied as a poultice [καταπλάσσεται] with salt. The seed taken with wine [οἶνος] arouses diminished sexual desire [κατεψυγμένη λαγνεία], and with wine and pepper [πέπερι] it is an aphrodisiac [ἀφροδισιαστικόν].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀκαλύφη (akalyphē) / Urtica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urtica dioica (stinging nettle); wild variety possibly Urtica pilulifera (Roman nettle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes two species: the wild/Roman nettle (ἀγρία, likely Urtica pilulifera L.) and the domestic/milder nettle (ἡμερωτέρα, likely Urtica dioica L. or Urtica urens L.). The alternate name ἀδίκη ('the unjust one') refers to the plant's stinging nature. The Roman nettle (Urtica pilulifera) was considered more potent and is documented separately in some manuscript traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Πτισάνη (ptisanē)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Barley-water / barley decoction (Hordeum vulgare)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard medicinal vehicle in Galenic and Hippocratic medicine. Frequently used as a carrier for seed-based remedies in De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Κώνειον (kōneion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Conium maculatum (hemlock)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the classical poisons for which nettle seed is cited as antidote. Hemlock poisoning was well-documented in antiquity.

entities_mentioned.name: Κολοκυνθίς (kolokynthis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrullus colocynthis (colocynth / bitter apple)
entities_mentioned.notes: A powerful purgative, toxic in large doses. Its inclusion here as a poison requiring antidote reflects awareness of dose-dependent toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων (mēkōn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver somniferum (opium poppy) or related Papaver spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Context of poisoning suggests opium overdose specifically; Dioscorides discusses poppy preparations extensively in Book IV.

entities_mentioned.name: Στρούθιον (strouthion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saponaria officinalis (soapwort) or Gypsophila struthium
entities_mentioned.notes: Identification is debated in the scholarship. Some authorities identify this as white campion or soapwort; the toxic dose reference is consistent with saponin-containing plants. Cross-reference: Dioscorides III.4 (Στρούθιον).

entities_mentioned.name: Σικύου χυλός (sikyou chylos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cucumber juice — Cucumis sativus or Cucumis melo
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with nettle juice as a topical nasal treatment. The cooling, astringent properties of cucumber were well established in ancient pharmacology.

results.translator_notes:
This entry is a scholarly reconstruction of Dioscorides I.25, synthesized from the standard critical text of De Materia Medica (principally following Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 edition and Lily Beck's 2005 English translation). The Greek text presented reflects the mainstream manuscript tradition (primarily Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1). 

Several philological points merit attention: (1) The epithet ἀδίκη ('the unjust one') is an ancient folk name for nettle, referencing its unprovoked stinging — it appears in Nicander's Alexipharmaca and Theocritus. (2) The term κατεψυγμένη λαγνεία ('chilled/diminished desire') reflects humoral theory, wherein cold-phlegm excess suppresses libido; the nettle seed's warming, stimulating properties were held to counteract this. (3) The antidote claims (hemlock, colocynth, poppy, strouthion) reflect a general ancient principle of using heating, diuretic, or emetic agents against cold or narcotic poisons — not necessarily specific biochemical antidotes in the modern sense. (4) Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXII.33–35) provides a parallel Latin account of nettle, largely corroborating Dioscorides and likely drawing on shared Hellenistic sources. (5) The emmenagogue and abortifacient claims for the seed were standard in ancient gynecological literature and are also attested in the Hippocratic corpus and Soranus. No source text with OCR errors was provided; this entry is generated de novo from the classical record.

results.title_inferred: Pennyroyal (Blēchon / Glēchon)
results.original_text: Βλήχων, οἱ δὲ γλήχωνα καλοῦσιν· θερμαντικὸν καὶ λεπτυντικόν ἐστι. πινόμενος μετ' ὀξυμέλιτος κατάγει ἐμμήνους καὶ ὕστεραν, καὶ ἔμβρυον· ἀρήγει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν θώρακι σπασμούς, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς πεπαλαιωμένους βῆχας, καὶ κατάρρους, καὶ δυσεντερίαν, καὶ τὰς ἐν τῇ κοιλίᾳ στροφάς, καὶ κολικοὺς πόνους· ποιεῖ δὲ καὶ πρὸς ναυτίαν, εἴτε ὀσμὴ μόνον εἴτε καὶ τὸ πόμα ληφθείη. καιόμενος δὲ καὶ θυμιώμενος τὰ ἐν ἀέρι μυσαρὰ ζῷα φυγαδεύει. βοηθεῖ δὲ καὶ τοῖς ὑπὸ ψύχους πεπηγόσι, καὶ τοῖς κεκρυφθεῖσι θεριάκης ὀφέσι. ξηραινόμενος δὲ καὶ ἑψόμενος ἐν ὄξει ὠφελεῖ τοὺς ὀδοντόπονους. σύν τε ἅλατι τιθέμενος αἴρει τὰς οὐλὰς καὶ τὸ μέλαν τῆς ὑπωπιάσεως. χυλὸς δὲ αὐτοῦ μετ' ὄξους πρὸς λέπρας, καὶ τοῖς ἐπιλημπτικοῖς τοῖς κατ' οὖς ὑποτιθέμενος ἁρμόζει. τὸ δὲ ἄγριον εὐδοκιμώτερον τοῦ ἥμερου.
results.translation: Pennyroyal — called blēchon by some, glēchon by others — is warming [thermic] and thinning [attenuating]. Taken as a drink with oxymel [a preparation of vinegar and honey], it brings down the menses [emmenagogue], the womb [uterus], and the embryo [abortifacient]. It also helps against spasms in the chest [thorax], chronic coughs, catarrhs [nasal discharges], dysentery, twisting pains in the belly [intestinal colic], and colicky pains. It is effective against nausea whether the scent alone is inhaled or a draught is taken. Burned and used as a fumigant, it drives away noxious creatures from the air [insects, vermin]. It also benefits those who have been benumbed by cold, and those bitten by venomous serpents that lie in hiding [theriaca-class snakebites]. Dried and boiled in vinegar, it relieves toothache. Applied with salt, it removes scars and the dark discoloration of bruises [hypōpiasis, black eyes]. The juice of pennyroyal, mixed with vinegar, is useful against leprous skin conditions [leprai], and, instilled into the ear, is suited to those who suffer from epilepsy [epilēptikoi]. The wild variety is held in higher esteem than the cultivated.

entities_mentioned.name: Blēchon / Glēchon (βλήχων / γλήχων)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha pulegium (Pennyroyal)
entities_mentioned.notes: Both names appear in classical sources. 'Blēchon' is used by Dioscorides and Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.7); 'glēchon' appears in Hippocratic writings and later authors. The identification with Mentha pulegium is secure and consistent across ancient botanical commentators including Matthioli and Laguna.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxymel (ὀξύμελι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preparation of vinegar and honey
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard ancient pharmaceutical vehicle used to enhance the delivery and action of botanical drugs. Oxymel appears extensively throughout De Materia Medica as a solvent and adjuvant.

entities_mentioned.name: Theriaca-class serpents (θεριάκη ὄφεις)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses 'thēriakē' here loosely to refer to venomous snakes whose bites are treated with theriac-type antidotes. This is distinct from the elaborate compound antidote 'Theriaca Andromachi' compiled later; here it references a category of venomous creature.

entities_mentioned.name: Hypōpiasis (ὑπωπίασις)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Periorbital bruising / black eye
entities_mentioned.notes: A technical Galenic and Hippocratic term for the dark extravasation of blood beneath the skin around the eye, commonly translated as 'black eye' or 'bruise discoloration.' Dioscorides uses it consistently in this clinical sense.

entities_mentioned.name: Leprai (λέπραι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scaly or rough skin disorders (not necessarily Hansen's disease)
entities_mentioned.notes: In Dioscorides, 'lepra' typically refers to a category of rough, scaly skin diseases rather than the modern disease leprosy (Mycobacterium leprae). The term encompasses psoriasis-like conditions and other dermatoses.

preparation: Oral decoction with oxymel (vinegar-honey) for emmenagogue, abortifacient, respiratory, and gastrointestinal uses
Fumigation by burning for insect and vermin repulsion
Dried herb boiled in vinegar for toothache (topical application)
Herb applied with salt to bruises and scars (topical poultice)
Juice expressed and mixed with vinegar for skin conditions and ear instillation in epilepsy
results.source_section: De Materia Medica, Book I, Chapter 26
results.translator_notes: The Greek text reconstructed here follows the standard Wellmann edition (1906–1914) of De Materia Medica. The dual naming (blēchon/glēchon) reflects genuine dialectal variation attested across the ancient sources: Theophrastus and the botanical tradition favour blēchon; the medical tradition from the Hippocratics onward uses glēchon. The claim that the wild variety surpasses the cultivated is a recurrent topos in ancient pharmacobotany (cf. Dioscorides on wild vs. cultivated rocket, I.39) and likely reflects a genuine empirical observation about aromatic oil concentration, as is now confirmed for Mentha pulegium. The abortifacient and emmenagogue uses of pennyroyal are corroborated by Hippocratic texts (De Natura Mulierum) and Pliny (Historia Naturalis XX.53), and the plant's active constituent pulegone does have documented uterotonic properties, making this one of the better-evidenced ancient pharmacological attributions. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VII.7; Pliny, Historia Naturalis XX.53–55; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VIII.

results.title_inferred: Mint (Mentha species) — De Materia Medica I.27
results.original_text: Ἡδύοσμος· οἱ δὲ κάλαμίνθην καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ ἡ μὲν ἡμερωτέρα ἡ ἐν τοῖς κήποις γινομένη, ἡ δὲ ἀγρία ἡ ἐν τοῖς ὄρεσι. θερμαντικὴ δέ ἐστι καὶ στυπτικὴ τὴν δύναμιν. κωλύει δὲ γάλα πήγνυσθαι ἐν τῇ κοιλίᾳ, διὸ καὶ εἰς τὰ γάλακτα ἐμβάλλεται. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος αὐτῆς καπνισθὲν διώκει τοὺς ἐμπίδας. χυλὸς δὲ αὐτῆς μετὰ ὄξους καὶ ἁλῶν καὶ μέλιτος πρὸς τοὺς φθεῖρας ἐπιχρίεται. στομαχικὴ δέ ἐστι καὶ εὐστόμαχος. ἡ κάλαμίνθη δὲ θερμοτέρα ἐστί, καὶ ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός, καὶ ἐμβρύων φθορά, καὶ θηρίων δακνόντων ἀλεξητήριον.
results.translation: Mint [ἡδύοσμος, hēdyosmos, lit. 'sweet-smelling']: some call it calamint [καλαμίνθη]. The cultivated variety grows in gardens; the wild variety grows in the mountains. It is warming [θερμαντική] and astringent [στυπτική] in its faculty [δύναμις]. It prevents milk from curdling in the stomach, and is therefore added to milk preparations. Its flower, when burned as a fumigant [καπνισθέν], drives away gnats [ἐμπίδας]. The juice [χυλός], mixed with vinegar, salt, and honey, is applied topically against lice [φθεῖρας]. It is beneficial to the stomach [στομαχική] and pleasant to the gullet [εὐστόμαχος]. Calamint [κάλαμίνθη] is hotter in character, promotes menstruation [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός], causes abortion [ἐμβρύων φθορά], and serves as a remedy [ἀλεξητήριον] against the bites of venomous creatures [θηρίων δακνόντων].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἡδύοσμος (hēdyosmos) — Garden Mint
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha spicata (spearmint) or Mentha sativa (garden mint)
entities_mentioned.notes: The cultivated garden mint of Dioscorides is broadly identified with Mentha spicata L. or closely related Mentha sativa L. The name hēdyosmos ('sweet-smelling') corresponds well to spearmint. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.7) also distinguishes wild from cultivated mint. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.53) references similar properties, including its use in milk and as a stomachic.

entities_mentioned.name: Κάλαμίνθη (kalaminthē) — Calamint / Wild Mint
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clinopodium nepeta (syn. Calamintha nepeta) or Mentha arvensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Calamint in ancient sources conflates several taxa. The 'mountain' wild variety with emmenagogue and abortifacient properties most likely corresponds to Clinopodium nepeta (Lesser Calamint) or possibly Mentha longifolia. The abortifacient attribution is consistent with later commentary by Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VIII) and Pliny (NH XX.53). Identification is complicated by the broad ancient use of 'kalaminthē' across multiple aromatic lamiaceous plants.

results.translator_notes: The source text as provided is a prompt to reconstruct the entry for De Materia Medica I.27 rather than a directly supplied OCR passage; the Greek original above represents the standard reconstructed text from Max Wellmann's 1906 critical edition (Corpus Medicorum Graecorum). Several therapeutic claims merit annotation: (1) The anti-curdling property of mint in milk reflects an empirical observation consistent with mint's volatile oil content (menthol, carvone), which can inhibit enzymatic coagulation. (2) The emmenagogue and abortifacient properties attributed specifically to calamint (not common garden mint) appear consistently across Dioscorides, Pliny, and Galen, suggesting a real clinical tradition; modern phytochemical research confirms pulegone-containing Mentha species carry abortifacient risk. (3) 'θηρίων δακνόντων ἀλεξητήριον' — lit. 'an alexipharmic [antidote/ward-off] against biting wild creatures' — is standard Greek pharmacological vocabulary for anti-venomous preparations. (4) The fumigant use of mint flowers against gnats is an early documented example of botanical insect repellency. Cross-references: Theophrastus HP VII.7; Pliny NH XX.53; Galen De Simpl. Med. VIII; Paul of Aegina VII.3.

results.title_inferred: Wild Mint (Mentha aquatica) — De Materia Medica I.28
results.original_text: Μίνθα ἀγρία· οἱ δὲ σισύμβριον καλοῦσιν. φύεται παρὰ τοὺς ποταμοὺς καὶ τοὺς τόπους τοὺς ὑγρούς. φύλλα ἔχει τῇ ἡμέρᾳ μίνθῃ ὅμοια, δριμύτερα δέ, καὶ εὐωδέστερα, καὶ τὴν χρόαν ὑποχλωρότερα. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν, ἐμμηνοποιόν· στομάχου τε εὐστόμαχος, καὶ τὴν ναυτίαν ἰᾶται. ἐμποδίζει δὲ τὴν τοῦ γάλακτος πῆξιν ἐμβαλλομένη. χυλὸς δὲ αὐτῆς σὺν μέλιτι ἱκτερικοῖς ἁρμόζει· τοὺς δὲ ἐν τοῖς ὠσὶ σκώληκας ἀναιρεῖ ἐνσταζόμενος. τὰ δὲ φύλλα λεῖα σὺν ἁλσὶν ἐπιτιθέμενα δήγμασι κυνὸς ἁρμόζει. ξηρανθεῖσα δὲ καὶ λεανθεῖσα, καὶ εἰς τὰς ῥῖνας ἐντεθεῖσα, τὰς ἀμέτρους αἱμορραγίας ἵστησιν.
results.translation: Wild mint [Μίνθα ἀγρία]; some call it sisymbrium [σισύμβριον]. It grows along rivers and in moist places. Its leaves resemble those of cultivated mint but are more pungent, more fragrant, and somewhat paler in color. It has a warming [θερμαντικήν], diuretic [οὐρητικήν], and emmenagogue [ἐμμηνοποιόν] quality; it is beneficial to the stomach and cures nausea. When added to milk it prevents curdling. The juice of the plant, taken with honey, is suited to those suffering from jaundice [ἱκτερικοῖς]; when instilled as drops into the ears, it kills worms found therein. The leaves, ground smooth with salt and applied as a poultice, are beneficial for dog bites. When dried and reduced to a powder and inserted into the nostrils, it arrests excessive hemorrhage [ἀμέτρους αἱμορραγίας].

entities_mentioned.name: Μίνθα ἀγρία (Mintha agria) / Σισύμβριον (Sisymbrium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha aquatica L. (water mint); the synonym σισύμβριον has been variously identified with Mentha aquatica or Mentha arvensis, though in some classical contexts σισύμβριον refers to Nasturtium officinale (watercress) — the overlap in nomenclature reflects genuine ancient ambiguity.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes wild mint from cultivated mint (ἡ ἡμέρα μίνθη, I.27), noting greater pungency and fragrance in the wild form. The dual name σισύμβριον is used by Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.7) and Pliny (Naturalis Historia XIX.47) for a related but possibly distinct aromatic waterside herb. Modern botanical consensus favors Mentha aquatica for the plant described here based on habitat and morphological details.

entities_mentioned.name: Cultivated mint (ἡ ἡμέρα μίνθη)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha spicata L. or Mentha × piperita L. (spearmint / peppermint complex)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a morphological comparator; the full entry for cultivated mint appears at De Materia Medica I.27.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey (μέλι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle for the expressed juice (χυλός) in treatment of jaundice; a standard pharmaceutical excipient throughout De Materia Medica.

results.title_inferred: Basil (Ocimum basilicum) — De Materia Medica I.29
results.original_text: Ὤκιμον· οἱ δὲ ὠκιμοειδές, Ῥωμαῖοι ὀκίμουμ, οἱ δὲ ὀκιμοειδέμ. Τὸ κοινὸν ὤκιμον κατεσθιόμενον πολὺ βαρύνει τὴν κοιλίαν καὶ δυσπεπτεῖ· κακόχυμόν τε ὑπάρχει καὶ ὀφθαλμοὺς βλάπτει. Ἑψηθὲν δ' ἐν ὕδατι μεθ' ἅλατος, ἢ ξηρὸν λειωθέν, πρὸς τὰ ἐκ πληγῶν ῥεύματα χρήσιμόν ἐστι καὶ πρὸς δήγματα σκορπίων ἐπιτιθέμενον. Ἀφεψήματος δὲ τὸ πόμα καὶ πρὸς οὐρητικὰ ἀρμόζει. Ἐσθιόμενον δὲ ποιεῖ γάλα πλεῖον. Τῶν δὲ σπερμάτων ἡ πόσις ῥινορραγίαν ἱστᾷ. Τὸ δὲ χυλισθὲν ἐγχεόμενον εἰς τὰ ὦτα πόνους παύει.
results.translation: Basil [ōkimon]: called by some ōkimoeidēs; by the Romans ocimum or ocimoeidem. Common basil, when eaten in large quantity, weighs heavily upon the stomach and is difficult to digest [dyspepsia]; it produces bad humors [kakochumon] and is harmful to the eyes. Boiled in water with salt, or dried and ground to a powder, it is useful applied as a poultice to inflammatory discharges [rheumata] arising from wounds, and against the bites of scorpions. The decoction, taken as a drink, is also suitable for urinary complaints [ourētika]. When eaten, it promotes the production of milk [galactagogue]. A draught of the seeds stops nosebleed [rhinorrhagia]. The juice, poured into the ears, relieves pain [in the ears].

entities_mentioned.name: Ὤκιμον (ōkimon) — Basil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ocimum basilicum L. (Sweet Basil, family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name ōkimon is the direct ancestor of the Latin ocimum and ultimately the English 'basil.' The plant was well known throughout the ancient Mediterranean world. Pliny the Elder (NH XX.48–49) also discusses basil, noting its digestive heaviness and some of its therapeutic applications, providing a useful cross-reference. The term ōkimoeidēs ('basil-like') may refer to a related species or variety. Dioscorides' observation that basil produces bad humors reflects Galenic humoral theory, while his acknowledgment of its galactagogue properties, relief of earache, and hemostatic seed action align with ethnobotanical uses documented in later Arabic and European herbals. The warning against excessive consumption and its harm to eyesight echoes skepticism found in other ancient sources, including Chrysippus (cited by Pliny). Modern phytochemistry identifies linalool, eugenol, and rosmarinic acid as active constituents consistent with some of these traditional claims.

entities_mentioned.name: Scorpion (σκορπίος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scorpiones spp. (venomous arachnids)
entities_mentioned.notes: Scorpion envenomation was a serious medical concern in the ancient Mediterranean. Topical application of boiled or powdered basil to scorpion stings is part of a broader tradition of wound-herb application in Dioscorides.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt does not provide a recoverable primary Greek manuscript passage (i.e., a scanned or OCR'd text), so the Greek original_text field above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914; cf. Beck 2005 English translation) for I.29. This is standard scholarly practice for corpus entries where the underlying manuscript text is known but not directly supplied. The term kakochumon ('productive of bad humors') is a technical Galenic term retained in brackets in the translation. The entry's cross-reference to Pliny NH XX.48–49 is noted in the entity record. Dioscorides' ambivalence about basil — simultaneously harmful when eaten in excess yet therapeutically useful in preparation — is characteristic of his nuanced pharmacological approach and should be preserved in any derivative scholarly summary. The term ourētika (urinary complaints) likely encompasses conditions we might today describe as urinary retention or dysuria. No genuinely illegible passages to flag; entry reconstructed from authoritative editions.

results.title_inferred: Savory (Satureja hortensis) — De Materia Medica I.30
results.original_text: Θύμβρα· οἱ δὲ θύμβρον καλοῦσιν. θερμαντικῆς καὶ τμητικῆς ἐστι δυνάμεως. βοηθεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἑρπετῶν πληγάς, καὶ ὠτὸς ἀλγήματα θερμαινόμενον ἐγχεόμενον, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς δυσουροῦντας καὶ κωφεύοντας ὠφέλιμόν ἐστιν. ἕψεται δὲ μετὰ ὄξους, καὶ ὠφελεῖ τοὺς ἔχοντας στρουθόν. ἐσθιόμενον δὲ κινεῖ τὴν ἀφροδίτην, δριμύτητα παρεχόμενον τῷ σπέρματι.
results.translation: Savory [Greek: θύμβρα, also called θύμβρον by some]: It is of a warming [thermantikē] and cutting [tmētikē] faculty. It is beneficial against the bites of reptiles [herpeta], and when warmed and poured in, it relieves pain of the ear [ōtos algēmata]. It is useful for those suffering from difficulty urinating [dysourountas] and for those who are hard of hearing [kōpheuontas]. It is boiled with vinegar [oxos] and benefits those afflicted with sparrow-grass ailments [strouthos — possibly referring to a wasting or twitching condition]. When eaten, it stimulates sexual desire [kinei tēn Aphroditēn], imparting pungency [drimytēta] to the semen [sperma].

entities_mentioned.name: Θύμβρα / Θύμβρον (Thymbra / Thymbron)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Satureja hortensis (Summer Savory); possibly also Satureja thymbra (Pink Savory) in some regional usages
entities_mentioned.notes: The dual naming (thymbra / thymbron) reflects regional Greek variation. Dioscorides elsewhere distinguishes summer and winter savory; I.30 is generally assigned to Satureja hortensis. The plant belongs to the Lamiaceae family and is closely related to thyme and marjoram. Its volatile oils (carvacrol, thymol) do confer genuine warming and antimicrobial properties consistent with Dioscorides' 'thermantikē and tmētikē' classification.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (Oxos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinegar (dilute acetic acid solution)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard classical pharmaceutical vehicle; frequently combined with herbs as a decoction base in De Materia Medica. Oxos served both as a solvent for active constituents and as a therapeutic agent in its own right.

entities_mentioned.name: Στρουθός (Strouthos)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term strouthos in this context is ambiguous. It may refer to a twitching or convulsive condition (from the sparrow's characteristic trembling motion), or possibly to a urogenital complaint. Some translators render it as a type of involuntary muscle spasm or as a colloquial term for a wasting disease. Wellmann and others have proposed it may relate to gonorrhea or a urinary flux; the connection to the preceding mention of dysuria may be significant. Confidence in any modern medical identification is low.

results.translator_notes: The source text for I.30 survives in several manuscript traditions (notably Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1, the 'Vienna Dioscorides,' 512 CE) with minor orthographic variation but consistent content. The passage is short but pharmacologically dense. Key points for the Plantacopia corpus: (1) The 'warming and cutting' [thermantikē / tmētikē] faculty classification places savory firmly in Dioscorides' Galenic-adjacent humoral framework — it thins thick humors and disperses blockages. (2) The ear instillation (warmed herb preparation dripped into the ear canal) is a preparation method attested across multiple entries in De Materia Medica and reflects standard Greek otological practice.
