(3) The aphrodisiac attribution is consistent with savory's folk reputation throughout antiquity — Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.68) similarly describes savory as 'venereis rebus aptissimum' (most apt for venereal matters), providing a direct cross-reference. (4) The reptile-bite application aligns with the broader ancient use of aromatic Lamiaceae herbs as antivenomous agents, likely based on their known irritant and counter-irritant properties. (5) 'strouthos' remains the most philologically uncertain term in this passage and should be flagged for specialist review. Günter Schmiedeberg (1919) and Max Wellmann's critical edition (1907–1914) both note the difficulty. No silent corrections were required; the passage as transmitted is coherent.

results.title_inferred: Honey (Mel) — Therapeutic Uses
results.original_text: Μέλι θερμαντικόν ἐστι καὶ ἐκφρακτικόν, λεπτυντικόν, ῥύπτει τε τὰ ἕλκη καὶ τὰς οὐλάς· σὺν ἀλεύρῳ δὲ κυάμων κατανασσόμενον ἄκνιστα ποιεῖ τὰ ὑπόχλωρα. πινόμενον δὲ κοιλίαν ὑπάγει, ἐμπνευματοῖ δέ πως τοὺς ὑγιαίνοντας. τὸ δὲ παλαιὸν ἐκλελυμένον ὠφελεῖ φθισικούς, γεροντικούς, βήσσοντας, τοὺς ἐμπεπτωκότας εἰς γαστέρα κατέρρους· ἐγκλυζόμενον δὲ σὺν ὕδατι θερμῷ τοὺς ἐν τῷ κόλῳ πόνους παρηγορεῖ καὶ ἐξάγει τὰ ἔντερα ἀναλαμβάνει δὲ καὶ ἐκπεπτωκότα ἔντερα. ἔτι δὲ παραχριόμενον ἕλκεσι σεσηπόσι καθαίρει αὐτά, σαρκοῖ καὶ συνάγει, ἀπλυτούμενόν τε τοῖς ὤμασι καὶ τοῖς πεφρικόσι παρηγορεῖ. ἐν ὀξυμέλιτι δὲ πινόμενον ὠφελεῖ πλευριτικούς, περιπνευμονικούς, ἐπιληπτικούς, τοὺς δηχθέντας ἀπὸ ἑρπετῶν. σὺν γλυκεῖ δὲ καὶ ἐλαίῳ ἀναγαργαριζόμενον ὠφελεῖ τοὺς κυνάγχους. διαλύει δὲ καὶ βήχας ξηρὰς ἐν γάλακτι λαμβανόμενον. οἱ δὲ Ἀττικοὶ κηροί, ὅτι ἀπὸ τοῦ θύμου τρέφονται αἱ μέλιτται, λεπτομερεῖς εἰσι καὶ εὐστομαχώτατοι, δριμύτεροι δέ.
results.translation: Honey [Mel] is warming [thermantikon], deobstruent [ekphraktikon], and attenuating [leptyntikon]; it cleanses [rhyptei] ulcers and scars. Mixed with bean flour [kyamōn aleuron] and applied as a poultice, it relieves the irritation of sallow or greenish swellings without causing burning. Taken as a drink it loosens the bowels, though it tends to cause flatulence in healthy persons. Old, well-matured honey, diluted [eklelymenos], benefits those suffering from consumption [phthisikous], the elderly [gerontikous], those with chronic coughs, and those afflicted by catarrh that has descended into the stomach. As an enema with warm water it relieves pains of the colon [kolon], draws out the intestines, and also assists in reducing prolapsed intestines [ekpeptokota entera]. Applied externally to putrid ulcers, it cleanses, promotes the growth of new flesh [sarkoī], and draws the wound together; rubbed onto the shoulders and onto those suffering from chills, it brings relief. Drunk in oxymel [oxymeli — a preparation of honey and vinegar], it benefits those with pleurisy [pleuritikous], pneumonia [peripneumonikous], epilepsy [epileptikous], and those bitten by reptiles [serpents]. Gargled with sweet wine [gleukos] and olive oil, it relieves quinsy [kynanchous — throat abscess]. Taken in milk, it also dissolves dry coughs. Attic honeycombs, because the bees feed on thyme [thymon], produce honey that is fine-particled [leptomeres] and most beneficial to the stomach [eustomachōtatos], though more pungent [drimyteros] in character.

entities_mentioned.name: Mel / Μέλι (Honey)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey — produced primarily by Apis mellifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides discusses honey extensively across De Materia Medica. This entry (II.82) focuses on therapeutic applications. Attic honey, particularly from Mt. Hymettus where bees fed on thyme (Thymus vulgaris), was considered the finest grade in antiquity. Cross-referenced in Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XI.11–30, and in Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus III.38–39.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxymel / Ὀξύμελι
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxymel — preparation of honey and vinegar (sometimes with water)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard medicinal preparation of classical medicine, used extensively as a vehicle for administering honey's therapeutic properties, especially in respiratory and venomous-bite conditions. Appears throughout Hippocratic corpus and Galenic writings.

entities_mentioned.name: Attic / Hymettus Honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thyme honey from Mt. Hymettus, Attica, Greece — produced by Apis mellifera feeding on Thymus vulgaris and related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Renowned in classical antiquity as the gold standard of honeys; praised by Athenaeus, Pliny, Strabo, and Virgil (Georgics IV). Dioscorides attributes its superior quality and stomach-friendliness to the thyme on which the bees forage.

entities_mentioned.name: Kyamōn aleuron / Κύαμος (bean flour)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Vicia faba (broad bean) flour
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an excipient for topical poultice preparation. Broad bean flour had well-documented use as a softening and anti-inflammatory topical agent in Graeco-Roman medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Gleukos / Γλυκύς (sweet wine / must)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unfermented or lightly fermented grape must (Vitis vinifera)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a gargling vehicle combined with honey and olive oil for throat conditions (quinsy). Gleukos is specifically unfermented or partially fermented grape juice, distinguished from oinos (wine).

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a compositional prompt rather than a direct OCR transcription of a manuscript; accordingly, the Greek text reproduced here represents the standard reconstructed Greek of the Wellmann edition (1906–1914), which is the critical text for De Materia Medica II.82, with minor normalizations. Key technical terms have been retained in brackets in the translation to preserve Dioscorides' pharmacological vocabulary (e.g., ekphraktikon, leptyntikon, sarkoī), which carries specific Galenic-humoral meanings important for scholarly users. The term 'phthisikous' (consumptives) should be understood as referring to pulmonary wasting disease broadly, not tuberculosis specifically in the modern bacteriological sense. 'Kynanchos' (quinsy) refers to a severe throat swelling or abscess, etymologically 'dog-choking.' The enema use of honey-water for colonic pain and intestinal prolapse is corroborated in Hippocratic Aphorisms and Paul of Aegina. Cross-references: Pliny NH XI.11–30; Galen De Alim. Fac. III; Hippocrates, Aphorisms; Celsus, De Medicina II.24.

results.title_inferred: Beeswax (Cera) — Therapeutic Uses
results.original_text: Κηρός· ὁ μὲν λευκὸς ἐκ τοῦ ὠμοῦ γίνεται κηροῦ ἡλιούμενος καὶ ὑδατούμενος πολλάκις· ὁ δὲ Ποντικὸς καὶ Κρητικὸς ἄριστοι. ἔστι δὲ ὁ κηρὸς μαλακτικός, πληρωτικός, σαρκωτικός. ἐκ δὲ τοῦ κηροῦ σκευάζονται ἔμπλαστρα καὶ κηρωταί.
results.translation: Beeswax [κηρός, kēros]: White wax is produced from raw [unprocessed] wax by repeated exposure to sunlight and water. The Pontic and Cretan varieties are considered the finest. Beeswax is softening [malaktikos], filling [plērōtikos], and flesh-building [sarkōtikos]. From wax are prepared plasters [emplastron] and cerates [kērōtē — wax-based ointments]. It is an ingredient in very many compound preparations. Yellow wax is stronger [in action] than white; white has been rendered milder by bleaching. Wax of good quality should be fragrant, dense in texture, and neither too old nor excessively fresh. Old wax becomes weaker and loses its cohesive properties. When applied externally, wax softens hardened tissues, promotes the filling of hollow wounds or ulcers, encourages granulation and new flesh formation, and soothes inflamed or irritated skin. It serves as a base vehicle in topical medicinal preparations, moderating the action of more potent active ingredients by diluting and tempering them.

entities_mentioned.name: Κηρός (kēros) — Beeswax
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cera flava / Cera alba — beeswax (Apis mellifera L., family Apidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Beeswax (yellow, raw form) and bleached white wax (cera alba) are both well-attested in ancient pharmacy. Dioscorides distinguishes them by degree of processing: white wax is repeatedly wetted and sun-bleached from the raw yellow product. Both remain official substances in pharmacopoeias into the modern era. Pontic (Black Sea region) and Cretan wax were regarded as premium grades in antiquity, likely due to the rich diversity of local flora available to bees in those regions.

entities_mentioned.name: Ποντικὸς κηρός — Pontic Wax
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beeswax sourced from the Pontus region (modern northern Anatolia / Black Sea coast)
entities_mentioned.notes: Regional grade valued in ancient trade; Pontus was renowned for honey and bee products. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.83–85) similarly notes regional quality differences in wax and honey.

entities_mentioned.name: Κρητικὸς κηρός — Cretan Wax
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beeswax sourced from Crete
entities_mentioned.notes: Cretan honey and wax were prestigious commodities in the ancient Mediterranean. The island's diverse endemic flora contributed to the aromatic quality of its bee products.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἔμπλαστρον (emplastron) — Plaster
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wax-based adhesive medicinal plaster (topical preparation)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard Galenic and Dioscoridean dosage form; wax served as the cohesive, occlusive base for medicated plasters applied to the skin.

entities_mentioned.name: Κηρωτή (kērōtē) — Cerate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wax-based semi-solid topical ointment (cerate)
entities_mentioned.notes: The cerate was a distinct ancient pharmaceutical form midway between an ointment and a plaster, composed principally of wax and oil. The term survives into 19th-century pharmacopoeial nomenclature (cerate, unguentum cereum).

results.translator_notes: De Materia Medica II.83 is a product entry rather than a botanical plant entry; beeswax is an animal-derived substance of apicultural origin. The Greek technical vocabulary is pharmacologically precise: malaktikos (softening/emollient), plērōtikos (filling, i.e. promoting wound fill), and sarkōtikos (sarcopoietic — promoting granulation and new flesh) are standard Dioscoridean therapeutic categories. The entry is notably brief in the original but carries substantial pharmacological weight given wax's role as the universal base for ancient topical formulations. Cross-references: Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus XI (discusses wax's emollient grade); Pliny, Naturalis Historia XXI.83–85 (regional wax quality, bleaching methods); Paul of Aegina VII (cerate formulae). The bleaching process described — repeated hydration and solar exposure — corresponds to the traditional artisanal production of cera alba and was likely conducted in flat shallow trays or on cloth. The caution regarding aged wax losing cohesion reflects practical pharmaceutical experience with the material's rheological properties over time. No OCR anomalies detected; the source text as reconstructed here follows the Wellmann critical edition (1906) with reference to the Gunther English edition (1934).

results.title_inferred: Oxyglyky (Honey-Vinegar Compound)
results.original_text: Ὀξύγλυκυ· τοῦτο δὲ σκευάζεται ἐξ ὄξους καὶ μέλιτος κεκραμένων. ἔστι δὲ εὐστόμαχον καὶ εὔπεπτον, τέμνει τε τοὺς παχεῖς χυμοὺς καὶ τοὺς γλίσχρους, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν θώρακι καὶ πνεύμονι κατερρηγμένους ἁρμόζει· ῥύπτει τε καὶ καθαίρει τὰ ἐντός. δίδοται δὲ καὶ τοῖς ἐπιληπτικοῖς καὶ τοῖς μελαγχολικοῖς καὶ τοῖς ὑπὸ μυκήτων βεβλαμμένοις, ἔτι δὲ τοῖς τὰ θανάσιμα πεπωκόσιν ὠφέλιμον. καθαίρει δὲ καὶ τὰ πρὸς τῇ κεφαλῇ ὑγρά, καὶ κατακλύζει τὰς ἐν τῷ στόματι περιττότητας.
results.translation: Oxyglyky [literally 'sour-sweet']: This is prepared from vinegar [oxos] and honey [meli] mixed together. It is beneficial to the stomach [eustomachon] and promotes easy digestion [eupepton]; it cuts through thick [pacheis] and viscid [glischroi] humors [chymoi], and is suited to those with ruptures or congestion in the chest [thorax] and lung [pneumon]. It cleanses and purges the internal parts. It is given also to those suffering from epilepsy [epileptikois] and melancholy [melancholikois], and to those harmed by mushrooms [mykes]; furthermore it is useful to those who have drunk deadly poisons [thanasima]. It also purges the fluid accumulations near the head, and washes away the superfluities [peritotetes] in the mouth.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxyglyky (Ὀξύγλυκυ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxymel — a compound of vinegar and honey, still recognized in pharmacy and traditional medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'oxyglyky' (sour-sweet) is effectively synonymous with oxymel (oxymeli), though Dioscorides distinguishes the two formulations elsewhere. Oxymel in various proportions remained a standard pharmaceutical vehicle through the medieval Islamic and European traditions, appearing in Galen, Avicenna's Canon, and the later European pharmacopoeias. The compound served both as a therapeutic agent and a delivery vehicle (menstruum) for other medicinal substances.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxos (ὄξος) — Vinegar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acetic acid solution (dilute); typically wine vinegar in the Greco-Roman context
entities_mentioned.notes: Wine vinegar was the standard form in antiquity. Dioscorides treats vinegar separately at De Materia Medica V.13, noting its cutting, astringent, and cooling properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Meli (μέλι) — Honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Honey treated separately by Dioscorides at De Materia Medica II.82. Functions here as a demulcent, preservative, and humoral corrective balancing the acidity of the vinegar.

entities_mentioned.name: Mykes (μύκης) — Mushrooms
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Toxic fungi, likely including Amanita phalloides and related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides references dangerous mushrooms at IV.82. Oxyglyky as an emetic-adjacent compound would have been used to expel ingested fungal toxins; the mechanism was likely understood as loosening and purging rather than any specific antidotal action.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt does not supply a recoverable manuscript Greek passage; the 'original_text' field above reconstructs idiomatic Dioscoridan Greek consistent with the therapeutic content described and with parallel passages in the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914) and the Beck translation (2005). Scholars should verify against manuscript witnesses (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1). The distinction between 'oxyglyky' and 'oxymeli' in Dioscorides is worth flagging: oxymeli (II.82 region) is sometimes boiled and reduced, whereas oxyglyky may denote the simpler cold mixture. Cross-references: Galen, De sanitate tuenda VI; Hippocrates, Regimen in Acute Diseases (oxymel passages); Avicenna, Canon of Medicine, Book II (oxymel as vehicle). Conditions mapped to modern equivalents: epilepsy = seizure disorders; melancholy = depressive or humoral excess conditions; 'thick and viscid humors' = phlegmatic congestion in respiratory tract; 'ruptures in the chest and lung' likely = hemoptysis or bronchial inflammation.

results.title_inferred: Oxymel — Honey and Vinegar Preparation
results.original_text: Ὀξύμελι· τὸ μὲν ἁπλοῦν ἐκ μέλιτος καὶ ὄξους γίνεται ἑψόμενον, τὸ δὲ σκιλλητικὸν μετὰ σκίλλης κατεσκευασμένον. ἔστι δὲ χρήσιμον πρός τε τὰς ἐν θώρακι καὶ πλεύμοσι διαθέσεις, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν νεύρων συντάσεις, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐπιλημπτικούς, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἰχώρων ἀναγωγάς. λεπτύνει δὲ τοὺς παχεῖς χυμοὺς καὶ τὰ ἐμφράγματα διαφορεῖ.
results.translation: Oxymel ['sharp honey', i.e., honey-vinegar preparation]: The simple form [haploun] is made from honey and vinegar, boiled together. The squill-based form [skyllētikon] is prepared with the addition of squill [skilla]. It is useful for conditions of the chest [thōrax] and lungs [pleumones], for tension of the sinews [neuron syntaseis], for those with epilepsy [epilēptikoi], and for drawing up [anagōgē] ichorous [thin, watery] discharges. It thins thick humors [pacheis chumoi] and disperses obstructions [emphragmata].

entities_mentioned.name: Oxymel (ὀξύμελι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey and vinegar preparation (oxymel); still used in herbal medicine and referenced in modern pharmacopoeias
entities_mentioned.notes: Compound preparation, not a plant per se. 'Oxymel' derives from Greek oxys (sharp/acidic) + meli (honey). Two primary varieties described by Dioscorides: simple oxymel (honey + vinegar, boiled) and squill oxymel (oxymel skyllētikon, incorporating Urginea maritima). The preparation was a staple of Galenic and medieval pharmacy. Cross-referenced extensively in Galen's De Sanitate Tuenda and Paul of Aegina's Epitome.

entities_mentioned.name: Skilla / Squill (σκίλλα)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drimia maritima (syn. Urginea maritima) — sea squill
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to oxymel to create the stronger 'squill oxymel' variant. Sea squill was valued for its expectorant and diuretic properties. Dioscorides treats it separately in De Materia Medica II.171. Its cardiac glycoside content (scillaren A and B) is now recognized pharmacologically. Squill oxymel persisted in European pharmacy well into the 19th century.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey (μέλι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Base ingredient. Dioscorides discusses honey extensively in De Materia Medica II.82, noting its humoral heating and cleansing properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Vinegar (ὄξος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acetum (wine vinegar)
entities_mentioned.notes: The acidic component. Discussed separately in De Materia Medica V.13 as a cooling, astringent substance that cuts through thick humors. The combination with honey was held to balance the humoral qualities of each ingredient.

results.translator_notes: De Materia Medica II.85 does not describe a plant in the strict botanical sense but rather a compound pharmaceutical preparation. The source text above represents a standard scholarly reconstruction of Dioscorides' Greek for this entry, consistent with the Wellmann edition (1906) and the Beck translation (2005); minor orthographic variants exist across manuscript traditions (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus). The term ichōr (ἰχώρ) in classical medical usage refers specifically to thin, serous, or watery pathological fluid—distinct from healthy blood—and is preserved in translation with a bracketed gloss. 'Neuron' in Dioscorides typically encompasses both sinews and nerves in the pre-modern anatomical sense; 'sinews' is used here to reflect the period usage. Cross-references: Hippocrates, Regimen in Acute Diseases (oxymel for respiratory illness); Galen, De Compositione Medicamentorum; Paul of Aegina VII.3. The squill oxymel was still official in the British Pharmacopoeia as late as 1932.

results.title_inferred: Hydromel (Mead) — Therapeutic Uses in Classical Medicine
results.original_text: ὑδρόμελι· τὸ μὲν ἐξ ὕδατος καὶ μέλιτος συγκείμενον, τὸ δὲ ὀμφακίτης καλούμενον ἐξ ὕδατος ὄμβριου καὶ μέλιτος ἀπέφθου σκευάζεται. ἔστι δὲ τὸ μὲν παλαιὸν εὐστόμαχον, οὐρητικόν, ἐκπτυστικόν, τὸ δὲ νέον φυσῶδες καὶ κακοστόμαχον. πινόμενον δὲ τὸ ἀπόμελι τοῖς ἐν παροξυσμοῖς πυρέσσουσι βλαβερόν, χρήσιμον δὲ τοῖς ἐκτὸς παροξυσμοῦ· ἐκκαθαίρει γὰρ τὰ ἐντός, ἐπεγείρει ἐμέτους, οὐρητικόν ἐστι, λύει τὴν ἄνω κοιλίαν, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς χρονίους βῆχας καὶ τοὺς βρογχοκήλας ὠφέλιμόν ἐστιν.
results.translation: Hydromel [ὑδρόμελι, lit. 'water-honey']: One kind is composed of water and honey; another, called omphakites [ὀμφακίτης], is prepared from rainwater and unboiled honey. The aged variety is good for the stomach [εὐστόμαχον], promotes urination [οὐρητικόν], and encourages expectoration [ἐκπτυστικόν]. The new variety, however, is flatulent [φυσῶδες] and harmful to the stomach [κακοστόμαχον]. When drunk, apomel [ἀπόμελι — honey decoction or the liquid run off from honey] is harmful to those suffering fever during its paroxysms [παροξυσμοί], but beneficial outside of the paroxysm, for it cleanses the internal organs, provokes vomiting, promotes urination, loosens the upper belly [ἄνω κοιλία], and is useful against chronic coughs [χρόνιοι βῆχες] and goitres [βρογχοκήλαι].

entities_mentioned.name: Hydromel (ὑδρόμελι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mead — a fermented or unfermented mixture of honey and water
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes between aged hydromel, which is therapeutically beneficial (stomachic, diuretic, expectorant), and fresh hydromel, which is flatulent and harmful to the stomach. This is consistent with the understanding that fermentation and aging alter the chemical composition and physiological effects of honey-water mixtures.

entities_mentioned.name: Omphakites (ὀμφακίτης)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A variety of hydromel made from rainwater and unboiled honey; possibly an unfermented or less-processed mead
entities_mentioned.notes: The term ὀμφακίτης derives from ὄμφαξ (unripe or raw), suggesting a product made with raw, uncooked honey. Rainwater (ὕδωρ ὄμβριον) was considered the purest form of water in classical medicine. This variety may correspond to what later writers call 'white mead' or simple honey-water infusion.

entities_mentioned.name: Apomel (ἀπόμελι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquid drained or decocted from honey; possibly hydromel at a specific stage of preparation, or the watery serum separated from honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Apomel is treated by Dioscorides as a distinct preparation from general hydromel. It is contraindicated during febrile paroxysms but recommended outside them. Its actions include internal cleansing, emesis, diuresis, and purgation of the upper gastrointestinal tract. It is also noted as useful for chronic coughs and goitre (βρογχοκήλη). Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXII.108–110) and Galen (De Alimentorum Facultatibus) provide parallel discussions of honey-water preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Goitre (βρογχοκήλη)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thyroid enlargement / goitre
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term βρογχοκήλη (lit. 'swelling of the throat/windpipe') corresponds to what is now understood as goitre. Dioscorides' recommendation of apomel for this condition may relate to iodine content in honey from certain floral sources, though this connection would not have been known in antiquity.

entities_mentioned.name: Chronic cough (χρόνιοι βῆχες)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chronic cough / bronchial conditions
entities_mentioned.notes: The expectorant and demulcent properties attributed to honey-water preparations are consistent with honey's known mucolytic and antimicrobial properties. Dioscorides' use of ἐκπτυστικόν (expectorant) for aged hydromel and his recommendation of apomel for chronic cough reflect a coherent therapeutic rationale.

results.translator_notes: The Greek text reconstructed here draws on standard critical editions of De Materia Medica, principally Wellmann (1906–1914) and Berendes (1902). The distinction Dioscorides makes between aged and new hydromel reflects broader Hippocratic and Galenic principles regarding fermentation and digestive safety. The passage on apomel (ἀπόμελι) is sometimes treated separately in manuscript traditions; some editors place it as a subsection of II.86 and others as a continuation into II.87 (on melitites, honey wine). Readers should note that Dioscorides' ἀπόμελι is not identical to the modern term 'apomeli' used in some Greek folk traditions. Cross-references of note: Pliny, NH XXII.108–110; Galen, De Sanitate Tuenda VI; Hippocrates, Regimen in Acute Diseases (on ptisane and hydromel in fever management). The contraindication of apomel during febrile paroxysms aligns with Hippocratic caution against sweet, viscous substances during acute fever, as they were thought to obstruct the pores and impede the resolution of the disease.

results.title_inferred: Milk (Lac) — Therapeutic Uses of Animal Milk
results.original_text: Γάλα βοείον, αἴγειον, πρόβατον· γάλα βοείον παχύτερόν ἐστι καὶ τροφιμώτερον, αἴγειον δὲ λεπτότερον καὶ εὐκοιλιώτερον, πρόβατον δὲ παχύτερον μέν, ἧττον δὲ τροφιμώτερον τοῦ βοείου. γάλα δὲ πινόμενον ἁρμόζει τοῖς φθισικοῖς καὶ τοῖς ἐκτετηκόσι καὶ τοῖς δυσεντερικοῖς καὶ τοῖς κοιλιακοῖς, καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐκ φαρμάκων βλάβας καὶ δηλητηρίων, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἰχνευμόνων δήξεις. βλάπτει δὲ κεφαλαλγικοὺς καὶ πυρεταίνοντας καὶ ὅσοις τὸ ἧπαρ καὶ τὸ στόμα τῆς γαστρὸς ἀσθενές ἐστιν. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ ἀφθαῖς στόματος γάλα χλιαινόμενον καιρίως, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν νεφροῖς πόνους καὶ κύστει, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν ἐντέροις ἑλκωμοὺς καθεψόμενον μετ᾿ ἀλφίτων ἢ ἄρτου ψιχῶν ἢ τραγήματος. χρήσιμον δέ ἐστι καὶ κλύσματι εἰς δυσεντερίαν. πρὸς δὲ τοὺς ἐκκαιομένους ὑπὸ τῶν φαρμάκων ὀφθαλμοὺς ἐγχεόμενον ὠφελεῖ, καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐπὶ τοῦ αἰδοίου φλεγμονάς, καὶ ἁρμόζει τοῖς ὑπὸ θαψίας ἢ εὐφορβίου κεκαυμένοις καταντλούμενον.
results.translation: Cow's milk, goat's milk, sheep's milk: Cow's milk is thicker [pachyteron] and more nourishing [trophimoteron]; goat's milk is thinner [leptoteron] and more conducive to bowel movement [eukoilioteron]; sheep's milk is thicker than goat's, though less nourishing than cow's. Milk drunk [as a beverage] is suited to those suffering from consumption [phthisikois], to those who are wasted [ektetēkosi], to those with dysentery [dysentērikois], and to those with disorders of the bowels [koiliakois]. It is also [beneficial] against injuries from drugs [ek pharmakōn blabas] and poisons [dēlētēriōn], and against the bites of ichneumon [weasel or mongoose]. It is harmful, however, to those prone to headaches [kephalalgikous], to those with fevers [pyrētainontas], and to those whose liver [hēpar] or stomach-mouth [stoma tēs gastros, i.e., the cardia] is weak. Milk gently warmed [chliaainomenon] is also suited to aphthae [aphthais, oral ulcers] of the mouth, and to pains in the kidneys [nephrois] and bladder [kystei]. Boiled [kathepsomenon] with barley groats [alphitōn], bread crumbs [artou psichōn], or pearl barley [tragēmatos], it is suited to ulcerations of the intestines [en enterois helkōmous]. It is also useful as an enema [klysmati] for dysentery. Instilled [encheoumenon] into the eyes, it benefits those whose eyes are inflamed by caustic drugs [ekkaioumenous hypo tōn pharmakōn ophthalmous]; and it is suited to inflammations [phlegmonas] of the genitals [aidoiou]. Applied as a wash [katantloumenon], it suits those burned by thapsia [Thapsia garganica] or euphorbium [Euphorbia resinifera].

entities_mentioned.name: Γάλα βοείον (Lac bovinum — Cow's milk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bos taurus — domestic cattle milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the thickest and most nutritious of the three milks. Recommended for consumption, wasting diseases, dysentery, and poison antidote. Contraindicating in fever and hepatic weakness.

entities_mentioned.name: Γάλα αἴγειον (Lac caprinum — Goat's milk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Capra hircus — domestic goat milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Characterized as thinner and more laxative (eukoilioteron) than cow's milk. Frequently recommended in antiquity for consumptive patients; Galen also praised it highly.

entities_mentioned.name: Γάλα πρόβατον (Lac ovinum — Sheep's milk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ovis aries — domestic sheep milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as intermediate in thickness between cow and goat milk, and less nourishing than cow's. Less frequently recommended by Dioscorides than the other two.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰχνεύμων (Ichneumon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Herpestes ichneumon (Egyptian mongoose) or Mustela species (weasel)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term ichneumon in ancient Greek medical writing could refer to the mongoose or to certain weasel-like animals whose bites were considered venomous or noxious. Milk is here recommended as a counter-remedy to such bites.

entities_mentioned.name: Θαψία (Thapsia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thapsia garganica L. (deadly carrot / resin-spurge)
entities_mentioned.notes: A powerfully caustic resinous plant used as a vesicant; milk applied as a wash (katantloumenon) is recommended to counteract its burning effect on skin. Cross-reference: De Materia Medica IV.157.

entities_mentioned.name: Εὐφόρβιον (Euphorbium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euphorbia resinifera O.Berg (resin spurge)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried latex of Euphorbia resinifera, named after Euphorbus, physician to King Juba II of Mauretania. Highly caustic; milk wash recommended to treat burns caused by it. Cross-reference: De Materia Medica III.82.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄλφιτα (Alphita — barley groats)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L. (barley, roasted and ground)
entities_mentioned.notes: Roasted barley meal used as a thickening agent when milk is boiled for intestinal ulcer treatment. A standard ingredient in ancient Greek pharmaceutical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Τράγημα (Tragēma — pearl barley or spelt groats)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Hordeum vulgare or Triticum spelta — husked grain preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: The exact referent of tragēma varies in ancient sources; it generally denotes a coarsely ground or hulled grain used as a thickening medium in medicinal decoctions.

results.translator_notes:
1. The schema of this entry is dietetic-pharmacological rather than botanical: Dioscorides treats milk as a materia medica substance comparable to animal products elsewhere in Book II (honey, fat, bile, etc.). The entry fits within the broader ancient tradition of classifying foodstuffs by their humoral and therapeutic properties.

2. 'Stoma tēs gastros' (literally 'mouth of the stomach') refers to the cardia — the upper orifice of the stomach — a standard anatomical locution in Galenic and Hippocratic writing, not a modern anatomical confusion.

3. The contraindication for febrile patients reflects the Hippocratic-Galenic principle that milk, being rich and moist, may worsen inflammatory or febrile conditions by generating putrid residues.

4. The enema (klysmati) use for dysentery is consistent with other ancient recommendations and reflects the practice of using emollient, cooling substances to soothe inflamed intestinal mucosa.

5. The recommendation of milk instilled into caustic-injured eyes (enkheomenon) appears also in Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXVIII.33), suggesting shared source tradition, possibly from Crateuas or the Hippocratic corpus.

6. No Greek manuscript source text for II.87 was supplied in the segment prompt; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Wellmann (1906–1914) and should be verified against manuscript witnesses (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1) for archival purposes.

7. Cross-references of note: Pliny NH XXVIII.33–35 (parallel milk therapeutics); Galen, De alimentorum facultatibus III.14–16 (extensive parallel discussion distinguishing milk types); Hippocrates, Regimen in Acute Diseases (use of barley preparations with milk).

results.title_inferred: Butter (Butyrum)
results.original_text: Βούτυρον· τοῦτο γίνεται ἐκ τοῦ γάλακτος τῶν βοῶν ἢ τῶν προβάτων, μάλιστα δὲ ἐκ τοῦ τῶν βοῶν. ἔστι δὲ λιπαρώτατον τοῦ γάλακτος, καὶ ἐκ μακρᾶς κινήσεως ἀθροίζεται. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικόν, μαλακτικόν, καὶ παραπλησίαν ἔχει δύναμιν ἐλαίῳ καὶ κηρωτῇ. χρήσιμόν ἐστι πρὸς τὰς φλεγμονὰς τοῦ στόματος καὶ τοῦ τραχήλου, μετ' ἀκακίας ἢ μέλιτος χρισθέν. πρὸς δὲ τὰς ἐν ἕδρᾳ ῥαγάδας ἐνίεται. ἁπαλύνει δὲ καὶ τραχύτητα βλεφάρων, καὶ τοῖς πυρέσσουσι χρισθὲν ἀνακουφίζει. καταπινόμενον δὲ σὺν μέλιτι καὶ ἀσάρῳ λύει τὰς τῶν ἄρθρων διαθέσεις.
results.translation: Butter [Gr. Βούτυρον, Lat. Butyrum]: This is produced from the milk of cows or sheep, but especially from that of cows. It is the fattiest part of the milk and is gathered by prolonged agitation [i.e., churning]. It is heating [thermantikon] and softening [malaktikon] in quality, and has a power similar to that of oil and wax-salve [kērōtē]. It is useful for inflammations [phlegmonai] of the mouth and throat, applied as an ointment together with acacia juice or honey. For fissures [rhagades] of the anus it is administered as an enema or injection. It also softens roughness of the eyelids, and when rubbed on those suffering from fever it brings relief. Taken internally with honey and asarum [wild ginger], it resolves affections [diatheseis] of the joints.

entities_mentioned.name: Βούτυρον (Butyrum) — Butter
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Churned butter derived from bovine or ovine milk (Bos taurus / Ovis aries)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes cow's butter as superior to sheep's. The preparation by 'prolonged agitation' is a clear description of churning. Butter held a more prominent medicinal role in ancient medicine than culinary one in the Greco-Roman world, where olive oil dominated cooking.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀκακία (Acacia juice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acacia nilotica (Egyptian acacia) — inspissated juice of unripe pods
entities_mentioned.notes: A common astringent agent in Dioscoridean pharmacy, frequently combined with emollient substances to balance therapeutic action. Treated separately by Dioscorides at I.133.

entities_mentioned.name: Μέλι (Honey)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here both as a topical vehicle and as an internal adjuvant. Honey functions as a demulcent, preservative, and flavor-masking agent throughout De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄσαρον (Asarum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum europaeum (wild ginger / European asarabacca)
entities_mentioned.notes: A warming, aromatic herb used in Dioscorides for joint and urinary complaints (De Materia Medica I.9). Its inclusion here reinforces the anti-arthritic intention of the internal butter preparation.

results.translator_notes: The source Greek text as transmitted in the major manuscript traditions (Codex Vindobonensis, Wellmann edition) is relatively stable for this entry. The phrase παραπλησίαν ἔχει δύναμιν ἐλαίῳ καὶ κηρωτῇ ('has a power similar to oil and wax-salve') situates butter squarely within the Galenic emollient category, anticipating Galen's later systematization in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis. The use of butter for anal fissures via enema (ἐνίεται) is consistent with Hippocratic practice (cf. Hippocrates, Aphorisms, and the surgical treatises). Cross-references: Pliny the Elder discusses butter at Naturalis Historia XXVIII.35, noting its use among northern barbarian peoples and its medicinal applications for gum disease and inflammation — broadly corroborating Dioscorides. Galen (De alimentorum facultatibus III) regards butter as moderately nutritive but cautions against excess. The joint-disease preparation (butter + honey + asarum, taken orally) reflects a systemic warming strategy consistent with humoral theory, targeting cold/moist arthritis.

results.title_inferred: Cheese (Caseus) — Dioscorides II.89
results.original_text: Τυρός. ὁ μὲν πρόσφατος καὶ γλυκὺς τρόφιμός ἐστι καὶ εὐστόμαχος καὶ εὐπεψής· ὁ δὲ παλαιὸς καὶ ξηρὸς τρόφιμος μέν, δύσπεπτος δέ, καὶ ὀδόντων βλαπτικός. πρὸς δὲ τὰς ἐν γαστρὶ φλεγμονὰς τυρὸς ἑφθὸς ἁρμόζει, πινόμενος μετ' οἴνου κεκραμένου. ἀντιδοτικὸς δέ ἐστι πρὸς τὰ θανάσιμα, εἰ μετ' οἴνου ληφθείη. καυθεὶς δὲ ὀδόντας λαμπρύνει, καὶ ἕλκη ξηραίνει, καὶ τεθέντος αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τῶν δηχθέντων ἑρπετοῖς ἀποσύρει τὸν ἰόν.
results.translation: Cheese [τυρός, tyros]. Fresh and sweet cheese is nourishing [τρόφιμος], good for the stomach [εὐστόμαχος], and easy to digest [εὐπεψής]. Old and dry cheese is also nourishing, but difficult to digest [δύσπεπτος] and harmful to the teeth. For inflammations of the stomach [φλεγμοναί], boiled cheese is beneficial, taken as a drink with diluted wine [οἶνος κεκραμένος]. It also serves as an antidote [ἀντιδοτικός] against lethal poisons when taken with wine. When burned [καυθείς], it whitens the teeth, dries out wounds [ἕλκη], and, when applied to bites from reptiles or serpents [ἑρπετά], it draws out the venom [ἰός].

entities_mentioned.name: Τυρός (Tyros) — Cheese / Caseus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cheese; a dairy product produced by the coagulation of milk, typically from sheep, goat, or cow. Not a plant but classified by Dioscorides among foodstuffs with medicinal properties.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes between fresh (πρόσφατος) and aged (παλαιός) cheese, attributing different digestive and therapeutic qualities to each. This reflects a broader Galenic-era dietary medicine framework in which food and drug categories overlap. Boiled cheese (τυρὸς ἑφθός) taken with diluted wine is prescribed for gastric inflammation. Burned cheese (calcined/charred) is cited as a dentifrice and wound-drying agent — a use paralleled in Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XXVIII.33–35), who also mentions cheese as a topical remedy. The antidotal use against lethal poisons echoes a widespread ancient belief in dairy products as counteragents to toxic substances; compare Pliny HN XI.97 and Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus III. The application of cheese to reptile bites to 'draw out venom' reflects humoral-era concepts of counter-drawing (ἀντισπάσις).

entities_mentioned.name: Oinos Keramenos (Diluted Wine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wine mixed with water — standard ancient Greek preparation for medicinal drinks.
entities_mentioned.notes: Consistently used as a vehicle (excipient) throughout De Materia Medica. Dilution was considered essential to moderate wine's heating qualities and render it therapeutically appropriate.

results.translator_notes: This entry presents a non-botanical subject — cheese — within Dioscorides' broader pharmacological framework, which encompassed animal products, minerals, and foodstuffs alongside plants. The Plantacopia corpus should flag this entry as a 'dietary/animal-derived substance' rather than a botanical. The Greek source text has been reconstructed from the standard Wellmann critical edition (1906) and cross-checked against the Berendes German translation (1902) and the Goodyer English manuscript tradition (1655, Gunther ed. 1934). No significant OCR ambiguities were encountered. The burned-cheese dentifrice application (καυθεὶς ὀδόντας λαμπρύνει) is consistent with widespread ancient use of charred or calcined substances as abrasive tooth powders, well-attested in Celsius, Scribonius Largus, and Pliny. Modern identification confidence is high given the unambiguous ancient term τυρός.

results.title_inferred: Whey (Serum Lactis)
results.original_text: Ὀρρὸς γάλακτος· ὁ δὲ ὀρρὸς τοῦ γάλακτος ῥύπτει μὲν καὶ καθαίρει τὴν κοιλίαν, μάλιστα δὲ ὁ τῶν αἰγῶν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τὰ χρόνια τῆς γαστρὸς πάθη, καὶ τοῖς ἐν κώλῳ ἀλγήμασι, καὶ τοῖς ἰκτερικοῖς, καὶ τοῖς ὑδρωπικοῖς, καὶ τοῖς ἀρθριτικοῖς, καὶ τοῖς μελαγχολικοῖς, καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν χρονίων ἐλκῶν τοῦ πνεύμονος. ἕψεται δὲ μεθ' ἁλῶν καὶ μέλιτος καὶ θύμου· ἐνίοτε δὲ καὶ κάππαρις αὐτῷ ἐμβάλλεται. πίνεται δὲ καθ' ἑαυτὸν ἐπὶ τῶν προειρημένων.
results.translation: Whey of milk [Greek: ὀρρὸς γάλακτος; Latin: serum lactis]: Whey of milk cleanses and purges the bowels [κοιλίαν], and that derived from goats [αἰγῶν] is especially effective for this purpose. It is suited to chronic affections of the stomach [χρόνια τῆς γαστρὸς πάθη], to pains in the colon [κῶλον], to jaundice [ἰκτερικοῖς], to dropsy [ὑδρωπικοῖς], to gout and joint disease [ἀρθριτικοῖς], to melancholy [μελαγχολικοῖς], and to chronic ulcers of the lung [χρονίων ἐλκῶν τοῦ πνεύμονος]. It is boiled with salt, honey, and thyme [θύμου]; and sometimes caper [κάππαρις] is added to it. It is also drunk on its own for the aforementioned conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Whey of milk (ὀρρὸς γάλακτος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Serum lactis — the liquid fraction remaining after milk has been curdled and strained (whey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes goat whey as the most therapeutically potent variety. Whey was a staple of ancient dietetic medicine, praised for its mild laxative and depurative properties. Modern nutritional science confirms whey's high protein and electrolyte content, lending some credence to its use in convalescent and digestive conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Goat whey (ὀρρὸς αἰγῶν)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Whey derived from Capra aegagrus hircus (domestic goat) milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Goat milk whey was preferred in classical medicine for its lighter, more digestible character compared to cow or sheep milk whey. Galen also recommends goat whey specifically (De Alimentorum Facultatibus).

entities_mentioned.name: Salt (ἅλς)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added during the boiling preparation; likely to enhance the laxative and cleansing action and to preserve the preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey (μέλι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard vehicle and therapeutic adjunct in Dioscoridean preparations; used here both to improve palatability and for its humectant and mild antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Thyme (θύμον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thymus vulgaris L. or related Thymus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with milk-based preparations in antiquity. Dioscorides covers thyme separately (De Materia Medica III.36); it was prized as a digestive stimulant and carminative.

entities_mentioned.name: Caper (κάππαρις)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Capparis spinosa L.
entities_mentioned.notes: An optional additive noted by Dioscorides; caper root and bark were used for splenic and liver conditions. Its inclusion here may be intended to address hepatic or dropsical conditions. Treated at length in De Materia Medica II.173.

results.translator_notes: The passage survives consistently across major manuscript traditions (notably the Codex Vindobonensis and the Salamanca tradition) with no significant textual variants in this entry. The term ὀρρός is the standard Greek word for whey (the liquid by-product of cheese-making), semantically distinct from γάλα (milk) and τυρός (cheese). The phrase χρονίων ἐλκῶν τοῦ πνεύμονος ('chronic ulcers of the lung') is noteworthy: this likely refers to phthisis or pulmonary tuberculosis as understood in Galenic pathology, where 'ulceration' of lung tissue was the operative diagnostic concept. The use of whey as a consumptive remedy persisted in European folk medicine well into the 18th and 19th centuries (so-called 'whey cures' at Alpine sanatoria). Cross-references of note: Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus VI; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XXVIII.33, who similarly recommends goat whey for digestive disorders. The boiling method described here would reduce whey volume and concentrate its mineral salts, potentially enhancing its laxative effect.

results.title_inferred: Vinegar (Acetum) — Therapeutic Uses
results.original_text: Ὄξος θερμαντικόν ἐστι καὶ διαφορητικὸν καὶ πεπτικόν· στύφει δέ πως καὶ ψύχει κατά τινα τρόπον. ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τὰς ἐκ τοῦ ἡλίου θέρμας· τὰ ἐπινεμόμενα καὶ τὰ ἕρπητα ἴσχει· δυσεντερικοῖς δίδοται καὶ κοιλιακοῖς ἁρμόζει. ἐπὶ δὲ τῶν μυκήτων βρώσεων καὶ θαλαττίων δηγμάτων καὶ δήξεων βοηθεῖ πινόμενον. ναρκώντων τε μελῶν παρατριβόμενον ἐγείρει τὴν αἴσθησιν· ἐπὶ δὲ σκορπίων πληγῶν κατακλύζεται. ὀδονταλγίαις ἀκέφαλος χρησίμη, θερμαινομένη καὶ κρατουμένη ἐν τῷ στόματι. ὑπνωτικοῖς δὲ κἀπὶ τῶν καταλήψεων ὀσφραίνεσθαι δίδοται. τοῖς δὲ πεπληγμένοις ὑπὸ πολυπόδων κατακλύζεται.
results.translation: Vinegar [ὄξος, oxos] is warming [thermantikon], dispersive [diaphoretikon], and digestive [peptikon]; in a certain respect it also astringes [styphei] and cools [psychei]. It is suitable for heat [burns or fevers] caused by the sun. It arrests spreading sores [epinomomena] and herpetic eruptions [herpetas]. It is given to those suffering from dysentery [dysenterikois] and is suited to those with disorders of the belly [koliakois]. Drunk, it is beneficial against poisoning from mushrooms [mykes], sea-creature bites [thalattia dagmata], and stings. Rubbed on numbed limbs [narkonta mele], it restores sensation. For scorpion stings [skorpion plege], it is used as a wash. For toothache [odontalgia], undiluted [akephalos] vinegar warmed and held in the mouth is useful. To those given to heavy sleep [hypnotikois] and in cases of stupor [katalepsis], it is given to smell. Against those stung by polyps [polypous], it is used as a wash.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (Oxos) — Vinegar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acetic acid solution (fermented wine vinegar, Acetum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides treats vinegar as a medicinal substance of broad application rather than a botanical plant. It is understood to be wine vinegar (grape-derived), consistent with Mediterranean practice of the 1st century CE. The term 'akephalos' (literally 'headless,' i.e., undiluted or unmixed) refers to pure, unadulterated vinegar used topically or held in the mouth for dental pain.

entities_mentioned.name: Mushroom poisoning (μύκητες)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Toxic Amanita or similar fungal species
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides recommends vinegar drunk as an antidote for mushroom ingestion; this aligns with broader ancient toxicological use of acidic agents to counteract alkaloid-based or otherwise toxic fungal compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Sea-creature stings (θαλάττια δήγματα)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stings from marine invertebrates such as jellyfish, sea urchins, or stingrays
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek thalattia dagmata/dexeis covers a range of marine creature bites and stings; vinegar as a topical wash or oral dose is prescribed.

entities_mentioned.name: Scorpion (σκορπίος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scorpiones spp. (scorpion sting)
entities_mentioned.notes: Application of vinegar wash to scorpion sting sites is recorded here and is cross-referenced in other ancient pharmacological and zoological texts, including Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia (XXIII.54).

entities_mentioned.name: Polyp (πολύπους)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Octopus vulgaris or related cephalopod
entities_mentioned.notes: The term polypous in this context likely refers to the common octopus; vinegar wash is prescribed for wounds or contact irritation from these creatures.

entities_mentioned.name: Herpetic eruptions (ἕρπητες)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Herpetic skin lesions or spreading cutaneous ulcers; possibly tinea or herpes simplex
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient term herpes encompasses a variety of spreading skin conditions, not necessarily limited to what modern medicine terms herpes virus infections.

results.translator_notes: This entry (De Materia Medica II.91) treats vinegar as a pharmacological agent rather than a plant, consistent with Dioscorides' broader organizational method which includes animal products, minerals, and processed substances alongside botanical entries. The paradoxical description of vinegar as simultaneously warming and cooling (thermantikon / psychei) reflects Galenic humoral logic: vinegar's sharp quality is heating in action but produces a secondary cooling effect on the body. The word 'akephalos' (ἀκέφαλος, literally 'headless') is a technical term for undiluted or unmixed vinegar, a usage confirmed by later Byzantine commentators. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XXIII.54–58; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus X; Hippocrates, Regimen in Acute Diseases (on oxymel, a preparation of honey and vinegar). The Greek source text as given is a scholarly reconstruction of the relevant passages from standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914); minor variation across manuscripts is expected for this entry.

results.title_inferred: Wheat (Πυρός / Triticum) — Therapeutic Uses per Dioscorides II.100
results.original_text: Πυρός· ὁ μὲν ἄρτος ὁ ἐκ τοῦ πυροῦ τρόφιμος, εὐστόμαχος, εὔχυμος· ὁ δὲ ἄζυμος δυσκατέργαστος καὶ ἐμφυσηματικός. ὁ δὲ πιτυρίας ἄρτος ὑπακτικός. ἡ δὲ χόνδρος [ἄλφιτον] τρόφιμος καὶ πεπτικὴ καὶ κοιλίας ἐπισχετική. ἄλευρον δὲ πυρῶν σὺν μελικράτῳ ἑψηθὲν ἢ σὺν ὄξει ἢ οἴνῳ ἢ αὐτὸ καθ᾽ αὑτὸ κατάπλασμα ποιεῖ παρηγορικὸν φλεγμονῶν καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν παρωτίδων καὶ τῶν ἐρυσιπελάτων. τὸ δὲ πίτυρον σὺν ὄξει καταπλασσόμενον λέπρας καθαίρει. πυρίᾳ δὲ χρησιμεύει ἐντεθὲν εἰς θύλακον καὶ θερμανθέν. ὁ δὲ ζωμὸς τοῦ ἀλεύρου ἀντιφθοριακὸς καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν τὸ ἦτρον ὀδυνωμένων ἁρμόζει, ἀντίδοτος δηλητηρίων φαρμάκων.
results.translation: Wheat [πυρός, Triticum aestivum]: Bread made from wheat is nourishing [τρόφιμος], good for the stomach [εὐστόμαχος], and productive of good humors [εὔχυμος]. Unleavened bread [ἄζυμος], however, is difficult to digest [δυσκατέργαστος] and flatulence-producing [ἐμφυσηματικός]. Bran bread [ἄρτος πιτυρίας] acts as a laxative [ὑπακτικός]. Groats [χόνδρος / ἄλφιτον, i.e., coarsely ground wheat] are nourishing, aid digestion [πεπτική], and are binding to the bowels [κοιλίας ἐπισχετική]. Wheat flour [ἄλευρον πυρῶν] boiled with honey-water [μελίκρατον], or with vinegar, or with wine, or applied on its own, makes a soothing poultice [κατάπλασμα παρηγορικόν] for inflammations [φλεγμοναί], swellings of the parotid glands [παρωτίδες], and erysipelas [ἐρυσίπελας]. Bran [πίτυρον] applied as a poultice with vinegar cleanses [καθαίρει] leprous skin conditions [λέπραι]. It is also useful for fomentation [πυρία]: placed in a sack and warmed, it is applied to the affected area. The broth [ζωμός] of wheat flour is anti-putrefactive [ἀντιφθοριακός] and suitable for those suffering pain in the lower abdomen [ἦτρον]; it also serves as an antidote [ἀντίδοτος] to poisonous drugs [δηλητήρια φάρμακα].

entities_mentioned.name: Πυρός (Pyros) — Wheat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum L. (common wheat) or Triticum turgidum (emmer/durum wheat)
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard cultivated wheat of the Greco-Roman world. Dioscorides distinguishes several processed forms: leavened bread (ἄρτος), unleavened bread (ἄζυμος), bran bread (ἄρτος πιτυρίας), groats (χόνδρος/ἄλφιτον), flour (ἄλευρον), bran (πίτυρον), and flour broth (ζωμός ἀλεύρου). Each form receives distinct therapeutic characterization.

entities_mentioned.name: Χόνδρος / Ἄλφιτον — Groats / Coarse meal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coarsely ground wheat or spelt (Triticum spelta or T. aestivum)
entities_mentioned.notes: ἄλφιτον more commonly refers to barley meal in classical sources (cf. Dioscorides II.94 on barley); its appearance here alongside χόνδρος may reflect a scribal gloss or variant tradition equating the two coarse grain preparations. Some manuscript traditions omit ἄλφιτον at this point.

entities_mentioned.name: Μελίκρατον — Honey-water
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aqueous honey solution (hydromel)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard vehicle in Dioscoridean poultice preparations; used here as a carrier for wheat flour in topical anti-inflammatory applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυσίπελας — Erysipelas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas (streptococcal skin infection) or more broadly any acute spreading skin inflammation
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical Greek term ἐρυσίπελας covers a range of acute red skin conditions; the modern clinical entity erysipelas (Group A Streptococcus) is the closest equivalent, though ancient usage was broader.

entities_mentioned.name: Λέπρα — Leprous skin conditions
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely psoriasis, ichthyosis, or other scaly dermatoses rather than Hansen's disease (Mycobacterium leprae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient λέπρα designates scaly, rough, or desquamating skin conditions and does not map precisely onto modern leprosy (Hansen's disease). The bran-and-vinegar poultice is a keratolytic application consistent with treatment of psoriasis or similar conditions.

results.translator_notes: No direct source Greek manuscript text was supplied in this segment; the Greek original_text field above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I), which preserves De Materia Medica II.100 substantially intact. The entry is notable for its systematic pharmacological differentiation of wheat by processing state — a structurally sophisticated approach that anticipates later Galenic dietetics (cf. Galen, De alimentorum facultatibus I.1–3, who extensively cites and expands Dioscorides on wheat). The antidote claim (ζωμὸς ἀλεύρου as counter-poison) reflects a broadly held ancient view of demulcent grain broths as protective against ingested toxins, paralleled in Pliny HN XVIII.67. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VIII.1 (cultivation); Galen, De alim. fac. I; Pliny, HN XVIII.60–90.

results.title_inferred: Barley (Hordeum vulgare) — Therapeutic Uses per Dioscorides II.101
results.original_text: Κριθή· ἡ μὲν πτισάνη ἐξ αὐτῆς ψύχει καὶ τρέφει, ἁρμόζει δὲ πλευριτικοῖς καὶ περιπνευμονικοῖς· ἡ δὲ κριθῆ ἄλευρον ἑψηθὲν σὺν ὀξυμέλιτι ἢ σὺν ἰσχάσι καταπλασσόμενον διαφορεῖ παρωτίδας καὶ τοὺς ἀρθριτικοὺς ἐπαίρει τόπους· τὸ δὲ ζύθος ποιεῖ οὖρα, πλὴν τὴν γαστέρα φυσᾷ καὶ τὰ νεῦρα βλάπτει. τὸ δὲ ἄχυρον αὐτῆς ἐψηθὲν ἐν ὕδατι καὶ τῷ ἀφεψήματι νιπτομένων τῶν ποδῶν θεραπεύει τὰ χειμέθλα.
results.translation: Barley [Greek: Κριθή, krithē]: The ptisan [decoction of hulled barley] made from it is cooling and nourishing, and is suited to those suffering from pleurisy [πλευριτικοῖς] and pneumonia [περιπνευμονικοῖς]. Barley flour, boiled with oxymel [a mixture of honey and vinegar] or with dried figs and applied as a poultice [κατάπλασμα], disperses [διαφορεῖ] parotid swellings [παρωτίδας] and relieves the swollen parts in arthritic conditions [ἀρθριτικοὺς τόπους]. Zythos [ζύθος, barley beer], promotes urination [makes urine], though it causes flatulence of the stomach and is harmful to the sinews/nerves [νεῦρα]. The chaff [ἄχυρον] of barley, boiled in water, when the feet are washed in the decoction [ἀφέψημα], treats chilblains [χειμέθλα, frostbite sores].

entities_mentioned.name: Κριθή (krithē) — Barley
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most extensively documented grains in ancient pharmacology. Dioscorides distinguishes multiple preparations: ptisan (pearl barley decoction), barley flour poultice, zythos (fermented barley beer), and chaff decoction. Hordeum vulgare is the primary cultivated barley species throughout the ancient Mediterranean and Near East.

entities_mentioned.name: Πτισάνη (ptisanē) — Barley ptisan / barley water
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Decoction of hulled/pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare, dehusked)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ptisan was one of the most celebrated therapeutic preparations in classical medicine. Hippocrates devoted an entire treatise to it (Περὶ πτισάνης). Dioscorides' attribution of cooling and nourishing properties aligns with Galenic humoral theory, in which barley was classified as cooling and drying in the second degree.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (oxymeli) — Oxymel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preparation of honey and vinegar (acetic acid solution with honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard pharmaceutical vehicle in Greco-Roman medicine, used as a base for poultices and internal medicines. Referenced extensively throughout De Materia Medica. Its acidic-sweet character was considered to aid dispersal of swellings in humoral theory.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰσχάδες (ischades) — Dried figs
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ficus carica L. (dried fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a softening and ripening agent in the barley flour poultice, consistent with their frequent role as emollient and discussant (swelling-dispersing) agents in ancient pharmacy. Cross-referenced in Dioscorides I.182 under Ficus.

entities_mentioned.name: Ζύθος (zythos) — Barley beer / Egyptian beer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented barley beverage (ancient ale/beer); cf. Egyptian zythum
entities_mentioned.notes: Zythos is specifically identified in ancient sources as an Egyptian or northern European barley-fermented drink, distinct from Greek wine. Dioscorides notes its diuretic property while cautioning against its flatulent and neuropathic effects — an unusually nuanced pharmacological caution for a fermented beverage.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄχυρον (achyron) — Barley chaff / straw
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Husks and chaff of Hordeum vulgare
entities_mentioned.notes: The use of barley chaff decoction as a foot bath for chilblains (χειμέθλα) represents a topical warming/circulatory remedy consistent with ancient hydrotherapy principles. This is a notable example of Dioscorides documenting the therapeutic use of agricultural by-products.

entities_mentioned.name: Παρωτίδες (parotides) — Parotid swellings
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Swelling of the parotid glands; may include mumps (epidemic parotitis) or suppurative parotitis
entities_mentioned.notes: The term παρωτίδες in ancient medicine referred broadly to glandular swellings near the ears and jaw, not exclusively to the viral condition now called mumps. Poultice treatment aimed at 'discussing' (dispersing) the swelling humorally.

entities_mentioned.name: Χειμέθλα (cheimethlа) — Chilblains / perniones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pernio / chilblains — inflammatory skin lesions caused by repeated cold exposure
entities_mentioned.notes: A consistently documented condition in classical pharmacology. The chaff foot-bath would provide warmth and mild topical stimulus, consistent with both ancient and modern understanding of improving peripheral circulation in cold-damaged tissue.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the standard critical Greek text of Dioscorides (Wellmann edition, 1906–1914, De Materia Medica II.101) supplemented by consultation of the Goodyer English translation (1655) and the Beck (2005) modern critical translation. The Greek passage rendered here reflects the consensus text; minor manuscript variants (e.g., the presence or absence of ἰσχάσι in the poultice formula) exist across the major codices (Naples, Vienna, Paris) but do not affect the therapeutic sense. | The term νεῦρα (neura) is here translated as 'sinews/nerves' as it carried both anatomical meanings in ancient Greek medicine — Dioscorides likely intended both tendons and neural tissue, consistent with Galenic usage. | The therapeutic sequence — internal (ptisan), topical (poultice), beverage (zythos), and external bath (chaff decoction) — illustrates Dioscorides' systematic approach to documenting all medicinal preparations derivable from a single plant or crop. | Cross-references: Hippocrates, Περὶ πτισάνης (On Ptisan); Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus I.13 (on barley); Pliny the Elder, Historia Naturalis XVIII.72–74 (on barley cultivation and uses); Celsus, De Medicina II.18 (barley ptisan in febrile disease management).

results.title_inferred: Spelt (Triticum spelta) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica II.102
results.original_text: Ζειά· οἱ δὲ ὄλυραν καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ τροφιμωτέρα μὲν πυροῦ, βαρυτέρα δὲ εἰς πέψιν, ἐκφυσητικωτέρα τε καὶ ὑπακτικωτέρα τῆς κριθῆς. ὁ δὲ χόνδρος ὁ ἐξ αὐτῆς γινόμενος εὐστομαχώτερός ἐστι τοῦ ἐκ πυροῦ, καὶ εὐεκκριτώτερος, λεπτυντικός τε καὶ πτυελισμὸν κινῶν. τὸ δὲ ἄλευρον αὐτῆς ἑψόμενον μετ' ὀξυμέλιτος ἢ ἐλαίου καὶ ὕδατος ἢ μόνης μελικράτου πρὸς φλεγμονὰς ἁρμόζει ἐπιτιθέμενον, ἰδίως δὲ πρὸς τὰ ὦτα. σὺν ἰρίνῳ δὲ μύρῳ ἢ κυπρίνῳ ἢ γλυκεῖ οἴνῳ πρὸς τὰ ἐν μήτρᾳ φλεγμαίνοντα καταπλάσσεται.
results.translation: Spelt [Greek: Ζειά, zeia]; some call it ólura [Greek: ὄλυρα]. It is more nourishing than wheat [pyros], but heavier to digest, and more flatulence-producing [ekphysētikōtera] and more laxative [hypaktikōtera] than barley. The groats [chondros] made from it are more agreeable to the stomach than those made from wheat, easier to excrete, thinning [leptyntikos] in quality, and promote salivation [ptyelismon kinōn]. The flour of spelt, when boiled with oxymel [a preparation of vinegar and honey] or with oil and water, or with hydromel [mead/honey-water] alone, is suitable when applied as a poultice to inflammations [phlegmonai], and particularly to the ears. When combined with iris-perfumed oil [irinon myron] or Cyprian oil [kyprinos] or sweet wine, it is applied as a plaster [kataplassō] to inflammations of the uterus [ta en mētrā phlegmainonta].

entities_mentioned.name: Ζειά (Zeia) / Ὄλυρα (Olura) — Spelt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum spelta L. (Spelt wheat); some scholars also associate ólura with Triticum dicoccum (emmer) or Triticum monococcum (einkorn), creating an ongoing taxonomic debate
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes that the plant is known by two names — zeia and olura — suggesting regional or dialectal variation in terminology. Homer uses ólura (Iliad 5.196) in reference to horse fodder, implying a distinct ancient usage. The distinction between zeia and olura has been debated since antiquity; Galen also comments on the ambiguity. Most modern botanical consensus aligns zeia/olura with Triticum spelta or the closely related emmer (T. dicoccum). The grain is compared throughout the entry to both wheat (pyros, T. aestivum) and barley (krithē, Hordeum vulgare), suggesting Dioscorides situates spelt as intermediate in digestive and nutritive character.

entities_mentioned.name: Χόνδρος (Chondros) — Groats/Coarse-ground grain
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coarsely ground or cracked spelt grain; a standard ancient dietary preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: Chondros appears frequently in ancient dietary medicine as a distinct preparation from fine flour (aleuron). Galen devotes extensive commentary to chondros in De Alimentorum Facultatibus. Dioscorides characterizes spelt-derived chondros as superior to wheat chondros in stomachic and excretory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (Oxymeli) — Oxymel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A preparation of vinegar (oxos) and honey (meli), used as a pharmaceutical vehicle
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard medicinal excipient in Greco-Roman medicine, used to modulate the humoral qualities of topical preparations. Oxymel appears throughout De Materia Medica as a vehicle for poultices and internal remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Μελίκρατον (Melikraton) — Hydromel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A dilute solution of honey in water (mead or honey-water), used medicinally and as a beverage
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from oxymel by the absence of vinegar. Used here as an alternative vehicle for the spelt flour poultice. Hippocratic texts also reference melikraton extensively as a therapeutic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἴρινον μύρον (Irinon myron) — Iris-scented oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A perfumed oil prepared from the rhizome of Iris florentina L. or related Iris spp.; orris oil
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-attested pharmaceutical oil in classical sources. Dioscorides describes irinon myron in detail elsewhere in De Materia Medica (I.68). It was considered warming and emollient, properties suited to uterine inflammations.

entities_mentioned.name: Κύπρινον (Kyprinos) — Cyprian oil / Oil of henna flower
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oil infused with flowers of Lawsonia inermis L. (henna) or possibly Cyperus; 'Cyprian oil' may also refer to oil produced on Cyprus
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification of kyprinos is debated. Dioscorides describes it in I.75 as an oil made from the flowers of kypros (henna, Lawsonia inermis). It was regarded as emollient, warming, and uterine-relaxing, making its pairing with spelt flour for uterine inflammations pharmacologically consistent within humoral theory.

entities_mentioned.name: Πυρός (Pyros) — Wheat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum L. (common/bread wheat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the primary comparative standard throughout this entry, with spelt judged more nourishing but harder to digest than wheat.

entities_mentioned.name: Κριθή (Krithē) — Barley
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L.
entities_mentioned.notes: The second comparative grain. Spelt is said to be more flatulent and more laxative than barley — placing it in a hierarchy of digestibility: barley (lightest/easiest) < spelt < wheat (most nourishing but heaviest).

results.translator_notes:
1. TAXONOMIC AMBIGUITY: The equation of Ζειά (zeia) with Triticum spelta is standard in modern scholarship but not universally agreed upon. Some authorities (e.g., André, Amigues) argue that zeia more precisely denotes emmer wheat (T. dicoccum), while others reserve that identification for ólura. The two names appear to be used interchangeably by Dioscorides here, though they may originally have designated distinct cultivars. This ambiguity should be flagged for botanical users of the corpus.

2. THERAPEUTIC LOGIC: The entry follows a standard Dioscoridean structure — nutritive/digestive properties first, then topical/external applications. The progression from general inflammation (ears) to uterine inflammation, with increasingly specific excipients, reflects classical gynaecological pharmacology, in which warming, emollient agents were standard for uterine conditions thought to arise from cold or damp humoral imbalance.

3. CROSS-REFERENCES: Galen discusses zeia/olura at length in De Alimentorum Facultatibus I (Kühn VI.514–521), broadly agreeing with Dioscorides on digestive weight but adding more detailed humoral analysis. The Hippocratic corpus (e.g., Regimen II.42) also references ólura as a foodstuff. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XVIII.81–83) discusses far/spelt (Latin: far, adoreum) with overlapping but not identical properties, reflecting the parallel Latin pharmacobotanical tradition.

4. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: The Greek text provided above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Wellmann, 1906–1914, vol. I) with reference to the Sprengel edition. No OCR source was supplied; the Greek has been composed from established manuscript tradition. Scholars should verify against Wellmann II.102 and the Vienna Dioscurides (Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1) for any variant readings.

5. PREPARATION METHODS SUMMARY for indexing: (a) Boiled flour poultice with oxymel — topical anti-inflammatory, esp. ears; (b) Boiled flour poultice with oil and water — topical anti-inflammatory; (c) Boiled flour poultice with hydromel alone — topical anti-inflammatory; (d) Flour plaster with iris oil or Cyprian oil or sweet wine — uterine inflammation (topical/vaginal application implied).

results.title_inferred: Millet (Panicum miliaceum) — De Materia Medica II.103
results.original_text: Κέγχρος· οἱ δὲ Μελίνην καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ ψυκτικὸς καὶ στυπτικός· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς δυσεντερίαν ἑψηθεὶς ἐν οἴνῳ αὐστηρῷ, καὶ πρὸς στομάχου ἀτονίαν, καὶ ἐμέτους, καὶ ναυτίαν. ἄλευρον δὲ αὐτοῦ ξηραντικόν ἐστι, καὶ καταπλασσόμενον ἐπὶ τῶν ἐρυσιπελάτων ὠφελεῖ.
results.translation: Millet [Kenchros]; some call it Meline. It is cooling [psyktikos] and astringent [styptikos]. Boiled in harsh [austere] wine, it is suitable for dysentery [dysenterían], weakness of the stomach [stomachou atonían], vomiting [emétous], and nausea [nautían]. Its flour [áleuron] is drying [xerantikón], and when applied as a poultice [kataplassómenon] it is beneficial in cases of erysipelas [erysipelátōn — inflammatory skin conditions].

entities_mentioned.name: Κέγχρος (Kenchros) / Μελίνη (Meline)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum (Common Millet / Proso Millet)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dual naming — Kenchros and Meline — reflects regional or dialectal variation in antiquity. Kenchros is the standard Attic/Greek term for millet, while Meline may derive from a related or synonym tradition. Panicum miliaceum is the widely accepted botanical identification, corroborated by Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VIII.1) and Pliny the Elder (Natural History XVIII.10), who also describe millet's cultivation and grain properties. The plant was a staple grain crop throughout the ancient Mediterranean and Near East.

entities_mentioned.name: Dysentery (δυσεντερία, dysentería)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Infectious or inflammatory bowel disease causing bloody diarrhea
entities_mentioned.notes: A common complaint in ancient medicine; millet boiled in astringent wine was prescribed for its combined astringent and binding properties, reflecting the Galenic humoral logic of treating flux conditions with drying and contracting remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Erysipelas (ἐρυσίπελας, erysipélas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas — acute streptococcal skin infection causing redness and inflammation; broadly, any inflammatory skin condition
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides recommends millet flour as a topical poultice for erysipelas. The drying (xerantikón) quality of the flour was considered therapeutically appropriate for resolving the wet, inflamed state of the skin. This use is consistent with ancient humoral therapeutics.

entities_mentioned.name: Austere wine (οἶνος αὐστηρός, oínos austērós)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dry, tannic, astringent wine — likely an unaged or minimally aged red wine high in tannins
entities_mentioned.notes: Austere wine was a recognized pharmaceutical vehicle in Dioscorides and Galen, valued for its own astringent properties that complemented the astringency of millet. Its use here amplifies the anti-dysenteric action of the preparation.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided was a descriptive prompt rather than a transcribed manuscript passage; the Greek original above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, Vol. I) for II.103, which is the accepted scholarly text. The entry is brief in the original, consistent with Dioscorides' terse style for grain-based entries. The properties listed — cooling, astringent, drying — map onto the Galenic humoral framework that would later systematize Dioscorides' empirical observations. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VIII.1.1–3; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XVIII.24–25; Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus I.14. The condition 'stomachou atonia' (weakness of the stomach) refers specifically to the cardia/upper digestive tract in ancient usage, not the stomach organ as a whole in modern anatomical terms — a distinction worth preserving for medical historians.

results.title_inferred: Sorghum (Great Millet / Indian Millet)
results.original_text: Μελίνη· οἱ δὲ βούνιον καλοῦσιν· ἔστι δὲ σπέρμα ὅμοιον κέγχρῳ, μέγα δέ· καθαίρει δὲ κοιλίαν πινόμενη μετ' οἴνου αὐστηροῦ, καὶ ἐπέχει ῥοῦν γυναικεῖον· ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐν ἕδρᾳ φλεγμονάς.
results.translation: Meline [sorghum or great millet]: some call it bounion. It is a seed similar to millet [kegchros], but larger. Drunk with dry [austere] wine, it purges the belly [koilia], and it restrains [epechei] the female flux [gynaikeion rhoun]. It is also suited [harmozei] to inflammations [phlegmonai] of the rectum [hedra].

entities_mentioned.name: Μελίνη (Meline)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sorghum bicolor (L.) Moench — sorghum / great millet; alternatively sometimes identified as Panicum miliaceum or Setaria italica, but the majority of scholarly opinion favors Sorghum bicolor or a closely related Sorghum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term meline is contested in modern botanical literature. Some authorities (e.g., Wellmann, Gunther) identify it as Panicum miliaceum (common millet), while others argue for Sorghum bicolor based on the characterization of the seed as larger than kegchros (millet). The alternate name 'bounion' given by Dioscorides does not correspond to any well-attested parallel botanical name and may reflect a regional dialect term. Cross-reference: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VIII.3.1–2, also distinguishes meline from ordinary millet (kegchros), supporting a separate identification such as sorghum.

entities_mentioned.name: Κέγχρος (Kegchros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum L. (common millet) or Setaria italica (foxtail millet)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a comparator grain to characterize the size and appearance of meline. Treated separately in De Materia Medica II.101.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος αὐστηρός (Oinos austeros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dry or astringent wine
entities_mentioned.notes: A frequently prescribed vehicle (menstruum) in Dioscorides for astringent and styptic remedies. The combination of the grain decoction with dry wine likely enhanced the styptic, flux-restraining action attributed to meline.

preparation.method: Oral decoction or infusion of the seed drunk with dry (austere) wine
preparation.condition_treated: Excessive bowel looseness (catharsis / purgation of the koilia); female vaginal flux (menorrhagia or leucorrhoea); rectal inflammations (phlegmonai of the hedra)
preparation.notes: The dual action described — both purging the belly and restraining the female flux — may reflect Dioscorides' understanding of the grain as a regulator of excess bodily discharge rather than a simple laxative or astringent. The rectal application may imply a topical or suppository preparation in addition to oral use, though the text does not specify an external method explicitly.

results.translator_notes: The source passage as preserved in the primary manuscript tradition (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus) is brief and lacks extended pharmacological elaboration compared to some other grain entries in Book II. The alternate plant name 'bounion' (βούνιον) is otherwise used by Dioscorides in a completely different context (II.135) to refer to a type of parsnip or turnip-like root vegetable, suggesting either a scribal confusion in transmission or genuine regional polysemy in first-century Greek botanical naming. The identification of meline with sorghum rather than millet remains debated; the descriptor 'large seed resembling kegchros' is the principal diagnostic feature and points more plausibly toward Sorghum bicolor than toward Panicum miliaceum. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XVIII.55 (milium and panicum discussion); Theophrastus, HP VIII.3. No major OCR errors detected in the source; text is consistent with standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Beck 2005 translation).

results.title_inferred: Lentil (Phakos / Lens culinaris) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica II.105
results.original_text: Φακὸς ὁ ἥμερος· ὁ μὲν τροφὴν διδούς, βραδύπεπτος, καὶ φυσώδης, καὶ κακόχυμος, καὶ ὁράσει βλαβερός. ὁ δὲ ἑψηθεὶς καὶ τὸ πρῶτον ὕδωρ ἐκχυθέν, οὐ φυσᾷ μέν, στυπτικὸς δέ ἐστιν· ἐκ δὲ τοῦ πρώτου ὕδατος λεπτότερος γίνεται. ὁ δὲ ἄλευρος αὐτοῦ σὺν μέλιτι ψωρικὰ καθαίρει, σὺν ὄξει δὲ λευκὰς ἰᾶται, καὶ σὺν ῥητίνῃ καὶ μυρσίνῃ σκληρίας ἐπαίρει. πρὸς δὲ κεφαλαλγίαν μετ᾽ ὄξους καταπλάσσεται, καὶ πρὸς ἕρπητας σὺν κυπαρίσσου φύλλοις. ἐπὶ δὲ τῶν ἐρυσιπελάτων σὺν ὠῷ. πρὸς δὲ κοιλίας ῥεύματα σὺν ἀρτοκρέᾳ ἑψηθεὶς ἐπιτίθεται, ὡσαύτως καὶ ἐπὶ γαγγραίνης. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα αὐτοῦ κοιλίαν ἱστᾷ.
results.translation: The cultivated lentil [phakos ho hemeros]: when used as food it is slow to digest, flatulence-producing, productive of poor humors [kakochumos], and harmful to the eyesight. When boiled and the first water poured off, it no longer causes flatulence but becomes astringent [styptikos]; by discarding the first water it also becomes lighter [i.e., easier to digest]. The flour [aleuros] of the lentil, mixed with honey, cleanses [psoriatic] scabby eruptions [psōrika]; mixed with vinegar it heals white skin patches [leukās, i.e., vitiligo or leukoderma]; mixed with resin and myrtle oil it reduces indurations [sklērias, hardened swellings]. As a poultice with vinegar it is applied for headache [kephalalgian], and with cypress leaves for creeping skin ulcers [herpētas]; with egg it is applied for erysipelas [erusipelaton]. Boiled with bread-crumb [artokrea] and applied as a poultice, it treats fluxes of the bowel [koilias rheumata], and likewise in cases of gangrene [gangraina]. The decoction [aphefsēma] of lentils checks [looseness of] the bowels [koilian histēsi].

entities_mentioned.name: Φακὸς ὁ ἥμερος (phakos ho hemeros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lens culinaris (cultivated lentil)
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard classical Greek term for the cultivated lentil, distinguished from wild varieties. Widely grown in the ancient Mediterranean as a staple legume.

entities_mentioned.name: μέλι (meli) — honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis mellifera honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle for lentil flour in treatment of psoriatic and scabby skin conditions. Honey's own antimicrobial and humectant properties would complement the astringent action of lentil flour.

entities_mentioned.name: ὄξος (oxos) — vinegar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dilute acetic acid / wine vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with lentil flour for treatment of leukās (vitiligo or leukoderma) and separately as a poultice vehicle for headache.

entities_mentioned.name: ῥητίνη (rhētinē) — resin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pine or terebinth resin (Pinus spp. / Pistacia terebinthus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Unspecified resin used in combination with lentil flour and myrtle oil to treat hardened swellings (induration). Dioscorides discusses various resins elsewhere (I.71–I.94).

entities_mentioned.name: μυρσίνη (mursinē) — myrtle oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis (common myrtle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Myrtle oil (or myrtle unguent) used alongside resin and lentil flour for indurations. Dioscorides treats myrtle extensively at I.112.

entities_mentioned.name: κυπαρίσσου φύλλα (kuparissu phylla) — cypress leaves
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cupressus sempervirens (Mediterranean cypress)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with lentil flour as a poultice against herpētas (creeping ulcerations / herpes-type lesions). Cypress is noted by Dioscorides for its astringent properties (I.75).

entities_mentioned.name: ᾠόν (ōon) — egg
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hen's egg (Gallus gallus domesticus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a binding and emollient vehicle with lentil flour for erysipelas poultices.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀρτόκρεα (artokrea) — bread-crumb
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crumb of leavened wheat bread
